https://people.mpim-bonn.mpg.de/scholze/Gestalten.pdf  Scholze's paper on Gestalten or the opposite category of Stefanich rings. Key definitions: (1) from pages 11 to 12 the buildup to the definition of a Stefanich ring is given; (2) on page 7 he gives a definition of Tannaka formalism by stating that the functor from A to symmetric monoidal presentable abelian categories Mod(A) is fully faithful, as a generalisation of representation theory; (3) the geometry of Mod(A) can be captured by quasicompact quasiseparated schemes; (4) truncations to the heart is the motivation for stable infinity-categories on page 9; (5) Aoki's fix is to have 1Pr type judged as commutative algebras of symmetric monoidal presentable categories with kappa-compact objects, then it can be defined equivalently to itself as 1Pr, then 2Pr means one can apply Tannaka duality again to have it type judged as Mod(1Pr)_--1Pr on page 11; (6) on page 27, Theorem 3.11 is the main theorem; (7) page 28 gives a definition of Stefanich rings emphasising kappa-compactly generated objects; (8) page 29 gives objects of Stefanich rings to be sequences of Tannakian derivations of categories, and also describes that the functor from the opposite category of a infinity derived ring to Stefanich ring takes an E_infty ring spectrum to Stefanich sequences; (9) page 33 has the requirement that descent satisfies aleph1-cardinality; (10) n-affineness need care see page 51; (11) definition of Gestalten is given on page 60 as a careful Ind limit, to read carefully; (11) comments on shtukas on page 84. Key examples: (1) examples of Tannakian duality are given on page 6 to page 8; (2) product is kappa-inductive product, coproduct is naive product like in finite dim vector spaces, Gabriel-Ulmer duality is the key step, page 25; (3) page 36 gives surveys on dualisable objects; (4) unit objects and maps of Stefanich rings are described; (5) colimits require spectrification as per page 50; (5) Kunneth is explained on page 63; (6) examples of internal objects and symmetric monoidal categories for the 6 functor formalism on page 71; (7) examples motivating the need for n-affine by six functors on page 84; (8) worked example on Cartier duality on page 94; (9) I noticed that on page 6, Spec(A) is no longer thought as a set of maps from A to a fixed field (like a base), it is now an equivalence classes of maps from A to all possible fields, there is an final object Spec(Z) sure, but this is no longer that critical?; (10) I also noticed that sheaf properties are killed off by passing to isomorphism classes on page 7; (11) notice that  Tannakian perspective means that the functor taking any commutative ring A to its symmetric monoidal presentable abelian category of A-modules (using the Tannakian perspective) Mod(A) usually satisfies dscent for the topology, extending a functor X to QCoh(X) in all schemes or higher stacks, the classical example being a quasicoherent sheaf being the structure sheaf O_X in QCoh(X) as the unit object (like a 1), endormorphisms are ring of global functions (like a Z, endomorphisms give a semifield I think), sections Gamma(X, O_X).; (13) on page 9 Deligne's ideal is fleshed out in detail, take CAlg(C) of commutative algebras in C otimes, for symmetric monoidal presentable abelian otimes-category C, generalising the category of commutative rings, formally declare the opposite category as C-affine schemes. Define some Grothendieck topology, you always have fpqc topology since those are the strongest, pass over to the category of sheaves, you get Faltings' almost mathematics. To get a good theory of analytic spaces, non flat gluings are needed, analogue of localisation turns out to not be flat. You need nonflat gluing, so ClAlg(C) is not sufficient, work in CAlg(Pr_st^L), Pr^L is stable presentable infinity categories of left colimit preserving functors.

https://www.fields.utoronto.ca/programs/scientific/08-09/o-minimal/miniworkshops/Prelli.pdf  o minimal program, 99 slides on Sobolev sheaves, good intro to sheaf theory using category of opens PROBABLY THE BEST INTRO ON HOW TO BUILD SHEAF THEORY FROM SCRATCH, really good example of how one develops a sheaf theory from scratch based on considerations and need to develop a six functor formalism. Important example on opens with and morphism as inclusion in categories. Now use this category as the one where a sheaf of k-vector space, open sets of X are Mod(K), subneighourhoods are global sections (now I understand what sections are intuitively, as constant functions on opens in R), and inclusions are dual to restriction maps via contravariance. Examples of sheaves are holomorphic functions (literally numbers), C^\infty functions (smoothness), distributions (for obvious reasons), L^2 functions and tempered distributions are not sheaves. Need to weakn to less opens or less covers i.e. glue over a lot, need sites, then it may become sheaves. fibre is directed limit of modules of sections for Mod(k), see slide defining fibre, then these direct limits are equivalence classes under neighbourhood U, recall that these are the subsections, the equivalence class relation here is very vague to be honest, but I have a mental image of these now knowing that U are global sections somehow. Define topological site for subfamily of opens, recall that in definition of sheaf in category of opens you just need open subsets and coverings since we change subn'hoods U into F(U) or Section(U;F) for F sheaf. Finite coverings of U, the U correspond to global sections under coefficients of continuous bounded sections. This is an example of finite coverings, they worked concretely,then define site by opens as subsets of the family, and finite coverings of the family. Now the family is chosen to be open, relatively compact, subanalytic subsets of a real analytic manifold, this is a subanalytic site. Alternatively, one can use oppen definable subsets of N^n given as a O-minimal structure (N, <, ...) or DTOP site. Sheaves are constructed using split exact sequences. Subanalytic sheaves allow us to use tempered holomorphic functions, but direct limits of F(U) or fibres do not exist. However to fix this, need to consider spectral topology since if we only conside fibres associated at points. Consider countable, locally finite covering U_n, with U_n being relatively compact and subanalytic, in U_n consider the ULTRAFILTERS of globally subanalytic subsets (ultrafilters give you more points via surjections), a neighbourhood of an ultrafilter is a globally subanalytic open subset U contained in ultrafilter, oh no you can define a sheaf on it. Call bar X the associated topological space, any covering of relatively compact subanalytic open subset has finite subcover. Therefore to work on fibres one needs to work on sheaves with global sections defined on neighbourhoods of ultrafilters of globally subanalytic subsets so that on ehas enough points, this is the spectral topology. See Prelli's paper, his theorem states that 6 functor formalisms works for sheaves on subanalytic sites.

Objects of two categories are the same, homsets so everything in Sigma is isomorphic to 0. K^oa-mod is K^o-mod/Sigma, localisation of category K^o-mod by Serre subcategory Sigma. Think back on example of localisating at a prime ideal, here you localise at Sigma, K^o/m is almost zero module, become 0 at K^oa-mod, but here you can actually compute, important the almost functor K^o-mod to K^oa-mod has right adjoint pushforward and left adjoint pullback? M_* are almost elements. Notation comes from topology, j inclusion, then j^* has exceptional pushforward j_! as left adjoint (pullbacks are limits, right adj preserve limits so j_* is right, j_! is left) by analogy think of almost functor as restriction of sheaves to opens. j_! is exact, so show that N_! is exact. Serre subcategory Sigma is ideal for tensor product, this almost category K^oa-mod has internal hom. https://arxiv.org/pdf/1108.6020, Grothendieck-Verdier categories, also called *-autonomous categories, notable example is derived category of constructible sheaves. *-autonomous category or Grothendieck-Verdier cat is monoidal category M, K dualizing object, for every Y in M X -> Hom(X otimes Y, K) is representable by DY in M, i.e. tensor hom adjunctions is representable by mapping into K using object DY in monoidal category, D is a duality functor. These are exploited to great effect by Kashiwara and Schapira. This dualizing object is global in somse sense.

Key principle is derived structure is no more than infiniteismal thickening of underived object, every simplicial ring can be expressed as composite of homotopy square 0 extensions of a discrete ring.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/2010.09216, compact closed categories in homotopical algebra. The idea is that compact closed categories are *-autonomous categories, whose tensor unit is the dualizing object, and admits the a left and right dual. The prototypical example is the category of finite dimensional vector spaces. Others include fg proj modules over a comm ring, or fg proj modules over a compact group. I was thinking that maybe duality fails over a noncommutative ring due to the failure of ergodicity or the semigroup condition, and commutativity or abelian results in some sort of compactness condition. Example: see figure, categories and start with free coonstruction, get coherently commutative monoidal categories, then end up with coherent compact closed categories. Folklore results and give aspects of duality.

https://people.math.harvard.edu/~landesman/assets/class-groups-notes.pdf  distribution of class groups notes, (1) upper bound on class number using discriminant, solution use Minkowski bound; (2) to define Cohen-Lenstra conjectures, one must define the odd part of the class group and then see what Aut(G) works on it; (3) to determine objects with frequency, solution is to normalise by 1/card of auto group; (4) want output independent on input, solution is universality as a theme in arithmetic statistics; (5) how much test functions can be used, not all test functions can be used; (6) how to use localisation to study Z and Q, solution localise at smaller geometric spaces; (7) study Pic(C)/infity using F_q geometry, use Deligne-Katz equidistribution and Grothendieck-Lefschetz trace formula; (8) how to put topology on Galois group, solution use etale fundamental group; (9) Zariski opens are way to big for covering spaces to be disjoint unions of copies, solutions need to use Grothendieck topology and etale topology; (10) to count G_q points by Galois cohomology, use Grothendieck-Lfeschetz trace formula, action of Frobenius, smooth and proper base change, as well as Artin comparison; (11) parameterise extensions of Gamma K / F_q t, solution is to use Gamma-Hurwitz spaces; (12) understand number theory, use Gal(bar Q/Q) and look at their inertia subgroups and Frobenius elements.

Sheaf cohomology is meant to prove the Riemann Roch formula. g = dim GlobalSections(C, canonical line bundle), k alg closed, C projective smooth (or equivalently proper normal curve). Riemann-Roch is proved in 3 major steps, first step is long exact sequence on sheaf cohomology by snake, next prove for any line bundle on proper normal curve, dimension formula for zeroth and first homology, then prove Serre duality. https://arxiv.org/pdf/math/0207210, on the resolution property: (1) definition is by Theorem 1.2, a stack has the resolution property if the algerbaic space B is a scheme with affine diagonal like a separated scheme. https://arxiv.org/pdf/2011.14234, ten fold way: idea is to have super vector space as direct sum of even and odd parts of vector spaces. Start with quaternion as division algebra and one can represent this with groups. Real irred reps is R, C and H. Quantum physicists uses complex vector space, and can generalise to super vector spaces, so elementar particles come in 3 kinds where it is R, C and H. Over the reals there are 10 possible options. We have R where e^2 = +- 1 giving 2, then C where e^2 = 1, ei = ie, and we have e^2 = 1, ei = -ie, and e^2 = -1, ei = -ie giving 3 options. Lastly we have H, e^2 = 1, e^2 = -1 giving 2 options then the remaining 3 options is A_1 = 0 over R, C, H. Recall that in super vector space, you construct from normal vector space by adjoining some sort of square root.

https://webhomes.maths.ed.ac.uk/~v1ranick/papers/deligne.pdf  spin groups are connected double cover over special orthogonal group, SO(n). https://arxiv.org/pdf/1309.1968, Grothendieck-Teichmuller. Equivalence of categories between Riemann surfaces and fields of meromorphic functions. Categories carry action of Gal(Q bar/Q), absolute Galois group, define the action of the absolute Galois group on graphs embedded on surfaces (dessins). Maps are graphs embedded on surfaces. When a group acts faithfully on something, it is an embedding in some automoprhism group, this leads to the Grothendieck-Teichmuller group, this is a profinite or inverse limit of GT(n), where GT(n) is a certain subgroup of Out(H_n). The origin of the subject is the study of maps or graphs embedded on surfaces in some way. Use quantum algebra. Glue discs to bipartite graphs to obtain a topological surface, called a dessin. Two permutations determine a dessin. There is a regularity condition, and importnatly there is a Galois action with concrete calculations. Gal(Q bar/Q) embeds faithfully into the Grothendieck-Teichmuller group. These are called maps, however these are the dessins. Dessins are graphs embedded on surfaces. Topologists may say a CW complex structure on a surface, however this equivalence of categories must be proven. Dessins are intimately related to finite sets with certain permutations. Bipartite graphs are defined. Black vertices, white vertices, and darts. This gives a bigraph.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/0903.4067, Drinfeld associators, idea is they define braided monoidal categories, and there is a universal way to construct braided monoidal categories out of Lie algebra, and it can be used to build quasi-Hopf algebras, then one can show that associators over Q exits, using the Grothendieck-Teichmuller group. This can be used to solve the Kashiwara-Vergne conjecture b showing solutions of a pair of equations exist for the Camper-Baker-Hausdorff series, which is useful to solve for Lie algebras exp(X) exp(Y) = exp(Z)

https://qchu.wordpress.com/2020/11/01/meditation-on-the-sylow-theorems-i/, story of Sylow theory. Idea is to construct Sylow p-subgroups of the symmetric group and partition blocks, then into p^2 block and cyclically computing these, exponent is p^floor(n/p^k) then this is a Sylow p-subgroup. You can get it fairly short with a simple fact about group actions of p-groups: If a p-group acts on a set, then the number of fixed points is congruent to the size of the set mod p. **Theorem 1** Now for a p-subgroup P < G, let P act on G/P by left multiplication. Then the fixed points are N(P)/P, so [N(P):P] = [G:P] (mod p). Induction (which can be done since p-groups are nilpotent) and Cauchy's theorem applied to N(P)/P then gives existence of Sylow p-subgroups. **Theorem 3** If P is a Sylow p-subgroup, then [N(P):P] = [G:P] mod p means that [G:N(P)] is 1 mod p, so the number of conjugates of P is 1 modulo p. **Theorem 2** Let P and P' be two different sylow subgroups. Then P acts on G/P' by left multiplication. Since the number of fixed points is not 0 mod p, it is nonzero. So there is a g with PgP' = gP' In other words g^- P g = P'

The simplest example of weight 2 Hodge correlators are computed. Then this integral can be worked out by the dilogarithm or the Block-Wigner function. This is very good, I finally understand what they are about and how to work with them. Recall that the Fargues-Fontaine curve which has played such a central role in p-adic Hodge theory, the twistor approach is the Archimedean version of that. So basically the Fargues-Fontaine curve is the non-archimedean version of Hodge correlators.

This is a pt[1] simplest, 1-shifted point. Example, 1-shifted points are 0-shifted sympletic stacks. So Symp or sympletic stacks which is the Weinstein (infinity,2)-category whose objects are sympletic manifold is contained in 2Fuk(pt[1]). This is how higher Fukaya categories are speculated to be; (2) page 4 and 5 give induction steps;

Definition of category of geometric objects is one that admits all fibre products i.e. finite limits. Study functoriality of l-adic cohomology. Single out collection E of !-able edges in C, (C,E) is geometric setup. Associate each morphism a compactly supported pushforward f!, and a symmetric monoidal pullback f*. Get compatible isos on brackets. Satisfy base change, projection formula, admission of right adjoints. category of correspondences gives definition. I realise correspondences and cocycle categories are very similar in structure. A six functor formalism is a lax symmetric monoidal functor Corr(C,E) -> C_\infty, so that tensor, f!, g* all admit right adjoints. Recall homology is computed on chain complexes and gives you abelian groups. Functors take computations in domain categories and gives you a series of invariants valued in another category. Likewise 6ff is an invariant valued in C_\infty computed in the category of correspondences where one does all the calculations. Example, homology is functor from chain complexes to abelian categories. I want to ensure morphisms of chain complexes have kernels and cokernels so that maps exist and behave well. Similarly, I want the formalism. Rigid analytic geometry an important example. https://iwoat.github.io/2018/slides/Ravenel.pdf  long slides by Hill-Hopkins-Ravenel on stable homotopy theory. MUST READ. AMAZING SET OF NOTES. Spectrum is originally defined as sequence of pointed spaces or simplicial sets with structural maps of suspensions of the n-th cell onto the higher cell n+1. A map of spectra is a collection of pointed maps f_n compatible with the structural maps. Strict equivalence if each pointed map is a weak equivalence. Stable equivalence either requires (1) stable homotopy groups of spectra, require pi_* f to be isomorphism, or (2) functor of Sp to Sp where (Lambda X)_n is homotopy colimit meanning mapping telescope, then require Lambda f to be strict equivalence. Classically equivalent but fails in general cases. Differ in category of symmetric spectra of Hovey-Shipley-Smith. Quillen model categories, fibrant cofibrant replacement, Bousfield localization, enriched category theory. Quillen model category, three classes of morphisms, weak equivalences, fibrations, cofibrations, each of which include all isomorphisms, with bicompleteness, M has all small limits and colimits, prods, coprods, pullbacks, pushouts, M has initial and terminal objects. Bicompleteness make it feel like abelian categories, two out of three axioms, exact sequence, if any f, g, gf are weak equivalences, so is the third, retract of weak equivalence is again a weak equivalence. Lifting axiom, A to X, B to Y, for cofibration A to B, and trivial fibration X to Y, morphism h lifts. Diagram shifts for trivial cofibration and fibration on the other side. Factorisation axiom, morphisms can be functorially factored as trivial fibration * cofibration or fibration * trivial cofibration. Factorisation hardest to verify. As usual classical example is in category of compact generated weakly Hausdorff topological space, weak equivalences are maps inducing isomorphisms of homotopy groups, fibrations are Serre fibrations with right lifting prooperty, cofibrations are left lifting property. An object is cofibrant if unique map from initial object (empty set) to X is cofibration, object is fibration if unique map from X to point is fibration. All objects in T and Top are fibrant, cofibrant objects are CW complexes. This makes CW complexs very natural to consider. MC5, these maps from initial object to terminal object have factorisations (not unlike first iso theorem) for cofibrant and fibrant objects weakly equivalent to X. That is the point, CW approximations or topologic approximations. Kan recognition theorem give conditions for generating set of trivial cofibrations. CW complexes example, so easy to think of simplicial stuff? Too technical for talk for Kan recogition theorem. Bousfield localisation, basic idea, enlarge weak equivalences in some way, keep same class of cofibrations, define fibrations as right lifting property with repsect to newly defined trivial cofibrations. More weak equivalences, more trivial cofibrations, fewer fibrations, fewer fibrant objects, fibrant replacement functor becomes much more interesting. (Absolutely no clue what is mentioned). Consider topological space with usual Quillen model structure, 5 axioms, bireplacement, factorisation, 2-3, lifting, diagram shift for fibration cofibration. Choose an integer n, fibrant object are spaces with no homotopy above n, so fibrant replacement is n-th Postnikov section. Recall section means cross-section, choose prime p, map is weak equivalence inducing isomorphism in mod p homology, fibrant replacement functor is p-adic completion (if simply connected), now choose generalised homology theory, pick h_*f as isomorphism, SLIDE 121 GIVES CONCRETE EXAMPLES OF BOUSFIELD LOCALISATION. So anytime you need to impose isomorphisms on weak equivalences in homology, use Bousfield localisations. Need enriched category, symmetric monoidal structural, natural iso making tensor products abelian, unit object 1, so (V = V_0, otimes, 1), this is symmetric monoidal structure. Pointed topological space or category of simpicial sets are standard examples. Enriched over V, morphism object, and composition morphisms of morphism objects with underlying categories. Simplicial category enriched over simplical sets. Symmetric monoidal category is closed if enriched over itself. Each pair of objects, exists internal hom object. Internal hom means closed. Definition of Yoneda spectrum, define spectrum as enriched functoir from indexing category J^N, spectrum was originally defined in terms of sequences of pointed spaces. Spectrum X is enriched functor from indexing category S^n-m n>=m, * otherwise to T, so spectrum can be thought of as a generalisation of a diagram! This is super insightful. Yoneda spectrum - S^-m, m >= given by J^N(m,n), S^-0 is the sphere spectrum! S^-m is formal desuspension. Warning J^N is monoidal but not symmetric monoidal. Yoneda functor, T is symmetric monoidal, twist isomorphism, but morphism not in image of Yoneda spectrum. No twist isomorphism in J^N. Category of spectra defined in this way has no convenient smash product, so bad definition. Headache in subject for decades. 1993, fixed. Hovey-Shipley-Smith, enlarge indexing category to symmetric monoidal category. Objects are natural numbers, bigger morphism objects. Wedge of copies, symmetri cspectrum is T enriched functor over J^Sigma -> T. Category of pointed topological space T is closed symmetric monoidal, indexing category is symmetric monoidal and enriched over T. Day Convolution comes into play. V be closed symmetric monoidal category, J be symmetric monoidal V category, functors of J to V is also closed symmetric monoidal, i.e. has internal hom and symmetric monoidal structure. This works by constructing a left Kan extension. Replace enlargement of indexing category by orthogonal analogue. Only the first one is very bad, again we want the Yoneda spectrum defined on last few pages.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.07305, coherent sheaves have three important short exact sequence, the Euler sequence, ideal sheaf sequence, normal sheaf sequence, it has a nice six functor formalism, direct image, pullback, tensor products. Projection formula and flat base change work only for specific classes of coherent sheaves like locally free sheaves. There is something more to say. Category of cochain complexes. Example, apply Hom(-,O_C) to ideal sheaf sequence, check intersection numebr using Euler characteristics. Homological algebra proposes to replace sheaves by adapted resolutions, derived category of sheaves will be more suitable here. Adapted resolutions, define category of cochainc mplex, objects are sequences, morphisms are maps of sequences for commutative diagrams. Main point is d^2 = 0, so one can take cohomology of any object. Map of complexes induces a map on cohomology H*(f*), if this is an isomorphism then f* is a quasi-isomorphism. An abelian category of complexes together with a functor from Com(A) -> D is called the derived category of A if Q(f*) is a isomorphism fro any quasiisomorphism, any other functor which maps quasi-isomorphisms to isomorphisms map uniquely through this universal derived category. To prove existence, require something like the homotopy category. Homotopy category consists of objects complexes of objects in abelian category, morphisms maps of complexes modulo homotopy. Definition of derived cateogry concretly requires roofs, key ingredient is quasi-isomorphisms form a localising class of morphisms inside Hot(A), to invert this requires Verdier localisation. Can specialise to abelian category of coherent sheaves on a noetherian scheme. Fourier-Mukai transforms, let X and Y be smooth projective varieties, denote two projections p, q from their product, let D^b(X x Y) containg P*, then the induced exact functor Phi: D^b(X) -> D^b(Y), taking Eps* to Rp_*(q* Eps Ltensor P*) is the Fourier Mukai transform with Fourier Mukai kernel p*, no need to derive q^*, so q^* is some sort of pushforward? I don't know it looks like the projection formula somehow or mixing Rhom and Ltensor. Global Torelli theorem is important.

https://www.math.columbia.edu/~chaoli/slides/Nelson.pdf  orbit method, microlocal analysis and applications to L-functions, many examples but hard to understand without background in Lie theory; (1) gives definition of coadjoint orbits and examples; (2) gives idea behind microlocal analysis, and microlocal support; (3) to quantify how automorphic forms of pi correlate with sigma, one defines a branching coefficient L(pi, sigma) to be some sort of global projection = coeff local projection^2 using integral norm; (4) to study integral solutions, arithmetic quantum ergodicity and random matrix heuristic, one wants to estimate special values of L-function individually (subconvexity) or over a family (moment problem); (5) to define a sympletic volume form, one uses a lie algebra structure on g to define a sympletic structure on coadjoint orbits; (6) to describes spheres are like SO(3), one must define coadjoint orbits is O in g^ for Hom(g, iR) for g the Lie algebra of g, G lie group, so the coadjoint orbits of SO(3) are spheres; (7) to describe the character oif a representation of a coadjoint orbit, one uses the Kirrilov formula of pi, which is some integral; (8) to describe O_pi and pi, one uses the orbit method which is some sort of bijection; (9) to understand a basic example of the Kirillov formula, one looks at SO(3), Irr(G), and find that the Kirillov formula holds for sphere of radius T + 1/2, and volume is 2T + 1 which is dim(pi), or study PGL_2(R); (10) to study P concentrated at specific tau in O_pi, one takes a vector microlocalised at Tau an dP its microlocal support; (11) to study microlocal calculus, one works with microlocalised vectors, pullbacks to Lie algebra, and then take the Kirillov formula, calculate a microlocalised basis and use a orbit method; (12) to define stability, one takes O_pi and preimage(O_sigma) ot be some sort of energy invariant aka a H-torsor (closed orbit, trivial stabiliser). This might not unique up to homotopy, so the original statement needs to be amended to homotopy colimit; (2) idea is pick small collection of generating objects, find an object in the category such that maps from elements of R to this (terminal?) object are the same as the functor applied to (generating) elements of R. Then one take a big coproduct of elements of R, mto make F of it really large, then attach or glue things to add relations.

https://ncatlab.org/nlab/files/Makkai-GabrielUlmerDuality.pdf  Gabriel-Ulmer duality, theorem: (1) LEX is a category whose objects are 0 cells or categories with finite limits, 1 cells are functors preserving finite limits, 2-cells are all natural transformations, LFC is a category with smal limits and filtered colimits. Sets with 2 adjunction between LEX^op to LFC, C in LEX, C* in LEX(C; Set), where counit eps_C from C to C^*#, Gabriel Ulmer duality gives equivalence of category. Explained from slides 1 to 4;

https://www.math.ias.edu/~lurie/278xnotes/Lecture10-Giraud.pdf  Giraud's axioms as explained by Jacob Lurie: (1) Theorem 3 is striking, X is a category, and X is a topos iff there is a small category C and a fully faithful embedding X into Fun(C^op, Set), admitting a left adjoint where L preserves finite limits, so X is not a canonical Yoneda self embedding, normally the Yoneda embedding is a functor C -> Func(C^op, inftyGrpd), basically it says X can be extended to Yoneda embedding whose left adjoint preserves finite limits; A topos is a category that can be extended to a Yoneda embedding whose left adjoint preserves finite limits.

https://mathoverflow.net/questions/293031/gabriel-ulmer-duality-for-infty-categories, Denis Nardin's proof of Gabriel-Ulmer duality: (1) describe the Gabriel-Ulmer duality in terms of infinity categories; (2) factor it into 3 fucnctors, ^op, Ind(-),  and equiv, the first functor sends idempotent complete, finitely comnplete infity-category to its opposite, this is op or equiv of infinity 2 categories, second is inverse of equivalence picking kappa = omega, using HTT.5.5.7.8, third is HTT.5.5.3.4 sends it to itself, sends every functor to right adjoint, this is a restriction. All three functors are equivalences; A Gabriel-Ulmer duality in infinity categories, is a contravariant ind-completion of the infinity category of finitely complete and idempotent complete infinity categories (i.e. finite limits with idempotent logic) and the infinity category of locally finitely presentable infinity categories.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/2308.11967, duality of clans. (1) page 2 gives the best description of Gabriel-Ulmer duality, it is a duality of theories and models. Small finite limit categories are viewed as theories, so for theory T, T-Mod is locally finitely presentable. FL is small finite limit categories, LFP is locally finitely presentable categories. Models are left exact (preserve finite limits) functors of essentiallly algebraic theories (has all finite limits). A Gabriel Ulmer duality is a functorial equivalance of small finite limit categories, which are theories, and locally finite presentable categories, which means we can describe them finitely.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/math/0509111, Tannaka family of flat connections: (1) section 2 on page 5 onwards describes a construction of Tannaka categories via the fibre functors; (2) the easiest example, let f from X to Spec(K) be smooth, geometrically connected, variety over a field of char 0, K contains k is a field extension, and x in X(K) is a rational point. There is the Tannaka category of bundles C(X/K), objects are bundles (i.e. locally free coherent modules [fg and fg submodules is fp]) these are not neutral Tannakian categories, bundles are a form of representation theory, so this is Tannakian; A Tannkian category is a category with Tannaka duality or a fibre functor such that there is a functor of points from the Tannakian category to points a etale fundamental group of the Tannakian category that is omega compact in finite sets to be an equivalence of categories.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/1204.5787, Tannaka duality over ring spectra, gives the best explanation of etale pi 1 using Tannaka's story: (1) history given in page 2, Galois categories are categories C with a fibre functor such that C is equivalent to the category of representations of a profinite group then we have a statement of C -> pi_1(C, omega)-FSet for finite set endowed with an action of pi_1(C, omega) or a fundamental group over a base point an equivalent of categories known as Galois duality, this is the etale fundamental group!; (2) Tannaka duality (neutralised) is clearly explained for T an algebraic fundamental group (NOT JUST A CATEGORY). A neutral Tannaka duality is a functor T -> Rep(pi(T, omega)^alg) from k-Tannakian category T, to representations of the etale fundamental group of affine group schemes as an equivalence of categories, where pi(T, omega)^alg is an affine group scheme.

The p-group fixed point theorem was done for case l prime, l not equal to p, reduce to l, then by contradiction, enlarge ring A for action of G to be defined, use Nullstellensatz to get a polynomial Q (with a maximal ideal) and repeatedly enlarge ring A, then use hypothesis of field A/m not eual to p, apply part a to get contradiction. This is an example of replacing autos with sets with more structure like p-group structure.

Furstenberg has horocycle flow on Gamma\SPL(2,R) compact, is uniquely ergodic, Haar measure is unique invariant probability measure. I want to understand this to understand how to generalise or simplify the condensed formalism. Basically ultrafilters are some sort of measure on fuzzy interval so one can do measure theory with it instead of using subobject classifiers. Makkai conceptual completeness is an functor giving a small pretopos to be equivalent the category of ultrafunctors from the category of models to the category of sets. An ultrastructure is a family of isomorphisms that gives a Stokes' formula indexed by objects of ultracategories, and ultrafilters on an index satisfying coherence conditions. Sheaves as left ultrafunctors? Is Makkai duality or Godel's completeness a form of Chu construction or *-autonomous category? The precursor is Stone duality after all and can be constructed that way. The construction from Boolean algebra B to Spec(B) determines a fully faithful embedding. Stone duality is a Chu construction of syntax and semantics in propositional logic. Models are point on the spectrum of Boolean algebras of irreducible elements. Makkai generalised it to coherent logic replacing Boolean algebra with a small pretopos, Boolean space with category of models. The Stone-Cech compactification is the maximal compactification giving rise to ultrafilters. See definition of ultrafilters given by Leinster in some other blogpost.

https://golem.ph.utexas.edu/category/2011/07/definitions_of_ultrafilter.html, ultrafilter is a set with property for all partitions (remember thermodynamics), finite number of subsets, precisely one i such that X_i is in U. This means it is some sort of selection. It works for n = 3, so X = X_1 coprod X_2 coprod X_3, one i such that X_i is in U, U is a set of subsets in P(X). Hadwiger's theorem, measure the size of subset, ultrafilter is a function P(X) to {0,1} such that phi is a valuation.

Once it is a module it has a relative internal hom, on page 9; (7) ind completion a key step, I saw Scholze used this to define Gestalten, the idea is one has full subgcategory of compact objects, then take ind-completion by formally adjoining all filtered colimits, but one can explicitly take the category of all exact functors (C^comp)^op -> Spectra, describing colimit preserving functors is easy; (8) on page 8 Gaitsgory exaplined that if an object is compactly generated it is dualisable, the dual is its ind completion. This is why ind-completions are so good; (9) Verdier duality (D-mod(X))^comp has finitely many cohomologies related to t-structure all are coherent D-moodules, Verdier duality gives auto-equivalences due to finiteness, for compactly generated Verdier duality extends well, where the compact pushforward and exceptional pushforward are Verdier conjugates page 9 and 10; (17) motivation for ind-completion is non-complete stable category, produce canonically a cocomplete one by adding all filtered colimit on page 57; (18) page 59 gives dual of compactly generated objects by Ind to be a Verdier duality; (19) rigid monoidal categories is unique to Gaitsgory, see page 71; (20) page 20 gives a concrete example of a heart on page 79; (21) note that there is no known way to define G-equivariant derived category of sheaves using only the derived category of sheaves, need constructions at chain level; Kraft inequality is because the main observation is a set of prefix free binary code words corresponds to binary tree and oriented paths from root to one of the leaves, binary tree, used induction over subtree. https://webusers.imj-prg.fr/~pierre.schapira/LectNotes/MuShv.pdf  microlocal sheave theory; Sato introduced algebraic analysis and microanalysis. Idea is to use categories and sheaves to study analysis by getting a manifold and look at its cotagent bundle and treat objects living on the manifold (istributions, hyperfunctions, differential operators) as the projection of the manifold living on the tangent bundle. This intuition can be made explicit with the expoenntail map. Microlocal theory of sheaves was developed by Kashiwara, associate a (derived) abelian sheaf F on a real manifold M. This is a co-isotropic subset SS(F) on the cotangent bundle T*M. D^b(k_m) be bounded derived category of sheaves as k-modules.

Find an auxiliary equation such that the root of your given equation they are all rational functions, i.e. root alpha gives alpha(x) and so on. How do I know x? x is a solution of another equation. P(\alpha) = 0, Q(x) = 0, just take all polys k[x] over field you are interesting, then you divide it by k[x]/Q(x), x satisfies Q(x) = 0. If there is any algebra relation you can imagine, it will hold. Amazing formal way, becomes a center. Define all rational operations between the roots of an equations. There are other relations.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/0805.2568, theory of ambiguity, this is what Galois called Galois theory. L = K[a,b,c,...] set of rationally known quantities form a field K, the set obtained by adjoining to K all polynomial expressions in the roots of equations is a field extension, the Galois group is group acting on set, subgroup of permutation group of the roots a, b, c. Gal(L/K) := Aut_K L. Emergence of structures like groups or infinity groupoids/topoi, emphasis on operations (six operations), principle of Galois correspondence, turning obstructions into mathematical objects. Do ambiguities form a nuisance, no they form a group! Fix base field, let normal extension K/Q vary, get projective system of finite Galois groups, then the Gal(Q bar/Q) is the compact profinite group of automoprhisms of the field Qbar of all algebraic number. This encodes all of algebraic equations with rational coefficients. Galois theory for coverings. Universal covering and Poincare fundamental group. Group of automorphisms of fibre functor on the category of finite coverings. Geometric approach to Gal(Q bar Q), Grothendieck-Teichmuller group by Drinfeld. Grothendieck's program, X = C\{0,1} as moduli space of four points on projective line, Gal(Q bar/Q) should be studied via its action on the Grothendieck Teichmuller tower of configuration spaces of any number of points. It is a geometric upper bound for Gal(Q bar /Q) in some sense. Ramis' theorem, three types of Galois ambiguities, formal monodromies, exponential scalings and Stokes' matrices generate a Zariski densed subgroup of Gal(L). Period its conjugate gates, a Galois group which permutes them transitively. Theory of motives. Algebraic varieties and linear invariants, cohomology. Motives are intermediate. Existence of mixed motives. Motivic Galois group to be the group scheme of autos of the restriction of otimes-functor H_B to <M>. Galois theory for periods. Galois ambiguities in GFT. Reg-dim perturbation of renormalisation of a QFT. Universal situation given by U(1,2,3,...), canonical one parameter subgroup. Birkhoff decomposition. Get Cartier's cosmic Galois group. Type I monodromes, type 2, exponential scaling.

https://pbelmans.ncag.info/notes/etale-cohomology.pdf  list of properties of Weil cohomology type theories. This is a good summary, worth reading and digesting, I read it once and realise. A list of topologies is listed on page 11; elementary six functor formalism in page 14; compactification always exists for separated scheme of finite type over a field, definition leads to cohomology groups with compact support;, then this motivates Verdier duality; purity is explained on page 21 with Gysin maps; SURPRISNGLY GOOD DEFINITION OF GROTHENDEICK TOPOLOGY, Grothendieck topology consists of sets of morphisms, if V -> U, singleton {V->U} is cover, if {U_i -> U} is cover, V -> U is morphism, all fibred products U_i x_U V exists, set of induced projections is again a cover, U_i -> U a cover, V_ik -> U_i cover then the compositions is also a cover. Canonical example is category of open sets, objects are open sets, arrows are inclusions, collection of open covers, fibred products of inclusions are intersection. Jointly surjective if set theoretic union of images is equal to U. small Zariski site is category, full subcategory of Sch/X of objects U -> X that are open immersions equipped with a Grothendieck topology by defining a cover to be joint surjective set fo open embeddings. Big Zariski site is full (Sch/X), there is small etale versions, full subcategory of SchX of objects U -> X that are etale (flat and unramified) and locally of finite presentation, equipped with a Grothendieck topology by defining a cover to be a jointly surjective set of etale morphisms. No difference in cohomology groups. Now one defines sheaves on Grothendieck topologies, presheaf, separated presheaf, and sheaf. If C is equivalent to category of sheaves on some site we get Grothendieck topos. big fppf site, jointly surjecitve set of flat morphisms that is locally of finite presentation, fpqc site, let X be a scheme, a representable functor on Sch/X is a sheaf in the fpqc topology, so sheaves in weaker topology, especially Zariski topology, the topology such that every representable functor is a sheaf is called canonical. Warning, do not have euqlaity of projections of U_i x_U U_i -> U_i for general sites. Many non-subcanonical topologies due to involving proper morphisms. First result from Milne is that Zariski topology is good enough for coherent cohomology. For smooth group schemes, need etale topology to get true cohomology groups. Etale cohomology, inverse function theorem fails in Zariski topology, another thing is X is irreducible, F be a constant sheaf, H^i(X,F) = 0 for i >= 1, Zariski cohomology groups of constant sheaf is not what we want. Consider Frobenius endomorphsim on affine line, not in char k, etale everywhere except origin, nowhere near a local isomorphism in Zariski topology, inverse function suggests that open set that is the complement of nice curve from 0 to infity to construct this inverse function is not open in Zariski topology, only the analytic topology, idea in the definition is that etale morphisms, we add those inverse functions. By adding enough inverses via proposition 19, one can define small etale site, apply sheaf cohomology, this becomes the etale cohomology. Define direct and inverse images of sheaves to obtain six functor formalisms, classicaly, inverse image functor is exact, but pushforward is only left exact, derive pushforward to obtain higher derived images. To compute higher direct images, use a spectral sequence. The main result is that we can approximate the higher direct image (derived) using the composition of the higher direct images. So derived categories let us use composition as an approximation. That is the purpose of the spectral sequence. Galois cohomology is a special case of etale cohomology, let K/k be a Galois extension of fields, tensor product gives product of K for g in Gal(K/k). Or in more geometric terms, the fibre product of Spec K x Spec K in k is isomorphic to the coproduct of Spec K over each g in Gal(K/k), isomorphism is made explicit as x otimes y -> (xg(y))_g in Gal(K/k), iterating this construction gives intersections from the theory of Cech cohomology of Grothendieck cohomology, bar resolution from group cohomology, Cech complex and standard complex agree, Galois cohomology is etale cohomology for X = Spec k. Looking for Weil cohomology theory, contravariant functor from smooth projective varieties over a base field k to graded algebras over a coefficient field K of char 0 satisfying this property. Existence of Weil cohomology proves a large part of the Weil conjectures, fd each cojhomology group is fd K-vector space, vanishing, if X is n-dimensional then H^i(X) = 0 for i not in {0, ... 2n}, orientation, top cohomology H^2n(K) is isomorphic to K, Poincare duality, nondegenerate pairing H^i x H^{2n-i} -> H^2n iso to K, Kunneth formula for tensor products to fibre products, cycle maps. Lefschetz is not axioms, but relate to the Riemann hypothesis part of the Weil conjectures. Four classical: l-adic, singular with analytic, algebraic de Rham with Zariski, crystalline with divided power thickneings of Zariski. l-adic is th eonly one obtained by using etale-topology and constructible sheaf. Important, l-adic is not just etale cohomology for constant sheaf Q_l. Constructible sheaves, locally constant sheaves fail for pushforward along proper morphisms all or closed immersions, again consider smallest subcategory of sheaves that contains the finite constant sheaves satisfying preservation along proper morphisms. Constructible sheaves are sheaves represented by etale algebraic space of finite type. Finite if F(U) is finite for all quasicompact U, finite stalks if F_x is finite for all geometric points of x. Espace etale represents sheaf on topological space as another topological space, with a morphism. Six functors, used to cut up category of sheaves on small etale sites and paste it together again. Exceptional pushforward is extension by 0, important result, set to 0 outside of opens for open immersions, very nice when open set is closed, it is proper, i^! is subsheaf of sections with support in Z. First theorem is proper base change. In SGA4_3, simplified by Artin ALB73 using representability and algebraic spaces. Theorem 27, pi: Y -> X proper, F be constructible sheaf on (Sch/Y)_et R^i pi_*(F) is constructible sheaf on (Sch/X)_et for all i >= 0. Proper base change to a fibre, one of the derived functor reduces to cohomology groups, get something like corollary 29. Group of sections with proper support. Functor is left exact, not right exact, derived functor still fails, but need open embedding on complete variety, compactification. Cohomology groups with proper support, is ordinary cohomology on compactification of X separated scheme of finite type over field, with exceptional pushforward / extension by 0 on sheaf. Torsion to be prime to char of residue field, need to define characteristic of a scheme, image of the canonical morphism to the final objec tSpec Z. Torsion of F is prime to char X if multiplication morphism is injective for all p in char X \{ {0}}. Smooth specialisation theorem. Acyclicity of taking affine space over scheme also requires smooth base change. Purity, define twisting operation for coherent cohomology in Z/nZ modules.cohomological purity. This meant to mimic Gysin exact sequence which is a special case of Serre's spectral sequence. Poincare duality requires full power of derived categories, not just derived functors, SGA4_3 required. Direct image with proper support as functor. Lei Fu, Etale Cohomology as budget SGA, SGA41/2, SGA4_3.

https://math.berkeley.edu/~willfisher/papers/Lie_Algebra_Cohomology.pdf  lie algebra cohomology in 3 pages, best 3 pager in Chevalley-Eilenberg algebra of g. Unique simply connected Lie group integrating g, so lie algebra determines lie group, one can defined cohomology of a Lie algebra. Differential form takes the group action, so differential forms are group actions or homomorphisms on permutations on Lie group, manifold with left G-action, G Lie group, g* a = g, g* means pullback along diffeomorphism induced by action of g, pullback has consequence, invariant forms of M form a sub-dg-algebra nd de Rham complex, provided that G is compact, averaging forms on M over their G-translates give relation betweentwo complexes, inclusion of invariant forms of M as a sun-dg algebra into the de Rham complex induces an isomorphism on cohomology grovided, G is compact, connected as Lie group, M is compact as manifold. dg is G invariant, int dg is 1, p(a) = a, p(a) in invariant forms, pullbacks commute with d there is a chain map. Assume G connected and M compact, ip is chain homotopic to id. Specialise to the case of the manifold M = G, G acts on itself, invariant forms on G are determned by their value at the identity. Prop 2.2. says de Rham cohomology of G is bijective with exterion Wedge^k g* algebras which is given the definition of he Chevalley-Eilenberg algebra, using d as the dual algebra. The differential D given by the exterior derivative is the dual of the Lie bracket, proven by Cartan's magic formula. Construction is functorial, d^2 in CE(g) is 0 is precisely the condition the Lie bracket satisfying the Jacobi identity. Fully faithful functor LieAlg_k -> dgAlg_k^op whose essential image is precisely those algebras whose underlying complex is exterior algebra of some space, this gives an alternative definition of Lie algebra of fully fiathful functors. So a Lie algebra is an object in the category of the Chevalley-Eilenberg functor from the category of Lie algebras to the category of dg algebras with underlying complex exterior algebra of vector space. CE(g) depends only on iso class of g. Suppose G is compact connected Lie group, cohom ring H*(G,R) only depends on associated Lie algebra, H*(G,R) is isomorphic to H*(CE(g)), CE(g) = (wedge^comp_k g*,d), d is dual of Lie bracket. Extensions, G0-invariant forms, Chevalley-Eilenberg complex gives a resolution to compute right derived functors of left exact functor Hom_Ug(R, -), R is trivial module. Given a Lie algebra g over R, g-module M, the cohomology og g with coefficients in M, is defined to be H*(g,M) = Ext^*_U,g(R,M), so one can use homological algbra. Weyl's theorem means g be a semisimple Lie algebra over field of char 0, every finite dim g-module is semisimple. I have seen the proof of this in another set of slides.

https://web.ma.utexas.edu/users/allcock/expos/nullstellensatz3.pdf  Daniel Allcock's Nullstellensatz paper. Another intuition from Nullstellensatz that I learnt is that one can treat evaluation as a quotient example evaluate f(c) is equivalent to f(X)/(x-c). This makes the notion of kernels make sense as evaluation on closed points and is the basics of Zariski geometry. Recall that ideals can now not just be thought of as something quotiented out as a coefficient system, but also as an evaluation. This gives rise to the perspective of counting autos by taking quotients as an evaluation if one has an evaluation. So the visual intuition is that of a hairbrush, the bristles is the whole space of complex plane, the point is the handle of the hairbrush. So evaluation at a point uses the topology and cohomology of C^\times in an essential way to shrink it down to the point, this is also the Eisenbud video of the fundamental theorem of algebra. For polynomials, the operation of evaluation is equivalent to the operation of considering the projection mod a prime ideal. Namely, f(x) evaluated at c, denoted f(c) is the same as the image of f(x) under the projection mod <x-c>. It can’t be emphasized heavily enough that commutative algebra uses how objects act upon (or can be acted upon *by*) other objects as a basis for defining and conceptualizing them. This is critically important, because it allows objects to be defined intrinsically, without dependence on external details that might not hold in greater generality. So, in this case, I would say that it’s not so much that ideals can be realized as points so much as it is that *points* can be realized as ideals. That this can be done in the classical affine case then motivates and justifies interpreting ideals of more general rings as points, even (and especially!) in situations  where the classical geometric picture might no longer make sense.

https://pbelmans.ncag.info/notes/spectral-sequences-examples.pdf  simplest notes of spectral sequences. Krull's ideal theorem intersecting with hypersurface drops at most dim 1, let A be a ring, M be module over A, regular sequence of x_n if (x_1, ... x_n)M is not equal to M, x_i is a nonzero diviosr in M/(x_1,... x_i-1) for all i in 1,...n. Most classical regular sequence is A = k[x,y,z] field, M = A, describe a point using x,y,z. Check that x, y(1-x), z(1-x) is a regular sequence. Order of regular sequence is important, for local rings permutations of regular sequences are again regular sequences. One now sees with analogy of regular lcoal rings, if maximal ideal is a way of generating the maximal ideal with minimal number of generators such that dim A = n, then A is regular, regular local ring of Krull dimension is generated by regular sequence in ideals of same dimension, permutations of dimensions appear. Where does S_n appear in the theory of regular sequences? Depth can be understood. Depth of an ideal such that IM != M (prevent Nakayama's nonzeroness), for M be fg module over A, I ideal of A, A be ring, depth of an ideal with respect to M is the minimum {i such that there is nontrivial Ext group Ext^i_A(A/I, M) != 0}. Depth is known as homological codimension. Rees has this giving it geometry. Consider local ring (A,m), M be fg A-module all maximal regular sequences, such that x_i in m have depth length = depth_m(M), depth measures how much we can cut down things using hypersurfaces, Krull's ideal theorem means cut down intersection 1, drop dim 1. Homological dimension is the correct notion of dimension in the absence of regular local rings. This yields bound depth_m(A) <= dim A, dim A is Krull dimension associated with dimension of reg sequences, depth_m(A) is homological dimension. Inequality is only equal for Cohen-Macaulay rings. Full ring is Cohen-Macualay if localisation is CM for all prime ideal in Speec A. Krull dimension measures things from bottom to top: we start from the zero-dimensional parts and go up. Depth on the other hand is characterised by Rees in terms of regular sequences: these cut the whole ring down using hypersurfaces. So Cohen–Macaulay rings are those rings for which it doesn’t matter in which way we measure things: depth and height are complementary.

https://loganhyslop.github.io/files/AviSlides.pdf  prismatic cohomology slides, I did not learn very much from these slides, perfectoid rings are analogues of perfect rings for Z_p-algebras, p-complete Z_p rings that are also mod p, Frobenius is bijection, p-th power dividing p + some other technical conditions. Main interest, tilting correspondences, R perfectoid, perfectoid R-algebras <-> perfectoid R^b algebras, R^b = lim_phi R/p. Problem, fix perfectoid condition since it is too restrictive, take covers by perfectoid spaces and tilt, keeping track of covers, this leads to the theory of diamonds. Idea, if perfectoid spaes are analogous to perfect F_p-algebras, how to drop perfect. Surjection phi: A_inf(R) -> R with kernel xi principal. Perfect prisms, lift of Frobenius, perfect prism is pair (A, I), A is delta ring, such that Frobenius is isomoprhism, I is principal ideal such that p in (I, phi(I)) and A is derived (p,I)-complete. Inverse functors defining an equivalence between perfectoid rings and perfect prisms. Basic examples would be structure sheaf, (A,I) -> A to (A,I) -> A/I for Hodge-Tate sheaf, (A,I) -> I (Hodge-Tate ideal sheaf). These are coefficients for cohomology, and get relative prismatic cohomology, absolute prismatic cohomology, relative Hodge-Tate cohomology. The analogue for mixed-characteristic rings is perfectoidization. Prismatic cohomology also meant to generalise, crystalline, Hodge, de Rham cohomology. Quasisyntomic site. https://legacy-www.math.harvard.edu/theses/senior/lehner/lehner.pdf  Riehl all concepts are Kan extensions. (1) a Kan extension is an extension of the preimage category from a base category (as a natural transformation) respecting representables, a left Kan extension is the left adjoint, a right Kan extension is a right adjoint. a Kan lift is an lift to the preimage category from an embedding to a base category respecting representables. If the base category is terminal, left Kan extensions turn the preimage extension into a colimit over a terminal base, right Kan extensions turns the preimage embedded into a limit over a terminal base. A functor can be thought of as a Kan extension with a represented base category, the left Kan extensions are left adjoints, right Kan extensions are right adjoints.

(2) to solve the problem of converting reductive groups over the complex numbers to be described as numbers, rings or categoriues, one can use geometric Satake; (3) to understand geometric Satake, one can work out the Cartan decomposition of the Grassmanian of a reductive group Gr_G = G(K)/G(O), then the combinatorics of the representation theory of the reductive group encodes convolution as tensor product, and MV cycles as weight multiplicities; (4) to define perverse sheaves, one uses category theory to say that perverse sheaves as abelian categories are equivalent to the dual of the reductive group; (5) to study the basic example of the Grassmannian of a reductive group, one studies GL_1, then Gr_G is isomorphic to Z as a topological space, we care only about the order of the pole, then the perverse sheaves are assigned a finite dimensional vector spaces i.e. Perverse sheaves of GL_1(O) of Grassmannian Gr_G is isomorphic to Z-graded vector spaces which are the representations of the reductive group; (6) to do G(O)-equivariant geometry on Gr_G for GL_1, replace it with GL_1(O)\Z or a groupoid, which is C^cross or S_1, and replace Z with C_P; (7) to find a new Satake category, the Z is replaced with copies of CP^\ifty which are differential graded C[u]-modules; (8) to do the spectral side, one maps graded dg C[u] modules to be equivalent to graded dg C oplus C modules; (9) to describe the main example from Drinfeld: the set of all G(O) equivariant sheaves on the grassmannians is equivalent to G-dual-equivaraint dg C oplus C gV[1]-modules, this is very complicated; (10) to do homological mirror symmetry or harmonic analysis on locally symmetric spaces, one uses the geometric satake; (11) to define locally symmetric spaces, one take Bun_G(C) or the space of all principal G-bundles on C, G = GL_1, then Bun_G(C) is the space of all line bundles n C; https://www.advgrouptheory.com/wrbp2024/Slides/parker.pdf Parker slides on fusion systems, rare that p-subgroup can be Sylow p-subgroup of finite simple groups. Fusion system, p prime, x is a group, S is Sylow p-subgroup, define Hom_X(P,Q) = {c_g | g in X, P^g subgroup of Q}, c_g is conjugation map x -> g'xg, P = Q, write Aut_X(P) = Hom_X(P,P) isomorphic to N_X(P) / C_X(P), know S, info about N_G(P) is lost when we make a fusion system is C_X(P) by quotient. Fusion system has objects all subgroups of S, morphisms from P to Q satisfying the above Hom_X(P,Q) relation, for Q subgroup of X, Aut(Q) is reduced to N_X(Q)/C_X(Q). If S is a Sylow p-subgroup of X, the fusion system is saturated (max sense). Abstractly saturated fusion systems on a p-group S capture the properties of the fusion systems just defined with S a Sylow p-subgroup of G.

Compact opertors, diagonal operators are elements of l_infty acting on l^2 ny multiplications, compact operators are sequences which tends to 0, by pigeonhole averageing they are infinitesimally small. Every ideal in a ring can be thought of as a collection of infinitesimally small elements, since they are one step away from bengn zero. Compact operators on Hilbert space can be considered infinitesimal compared to all bounded operators, forming an ideal where elements are small in the sense that they can be closely approximated by finite rank operators, eigenvalues tends oto 0. Ideals correspond to geometric shapes, higher order terms in ideal represent thickness. Quotienting by ideal turns elements in that ideal into an infinitesimal element i.e. not killing off the coefficient entirely. Example <x^2>. Bounded sets are mapped to totally bounded ones by compact operators.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/1209.3606, codensity monad of a functor, monad induced by G and its left adjoint even when no such adjoint exist. This is given by the coYoneda lemma, take inclusion of finite sets into category of all sets, codensity monad (see Riehl) is the ultrafilter monad. So the mere notion of finite set gives rise to ultrafilters, and the notion of compact Hausdorff spaces. Probability measures, ultrafilter is a finitely additive probability measure in which every event has probability either 0 or 1, the elements of an ultrafilter are the subsets that occupy almost all of X for a set X, other sets are null. Integration operator, can integrate against ultra filter, let U be an ultrfilter on a set x, function ffrom X to R with finite image, obtain element int f dU of R, it is the unique element of R, whose f-fibre belongs to U. Integrating agaisnt an ultrafilter has intuition of taking the mode. If elementsof U is large then integrating f dU is the unique value of U taken by a large number of elements of X. In a sense it is like an average! Think pigenohole principle? A functor is eithe rcodense or not. Finer grained information can be obtained by calculating the codensitymonad of G. Canonical topology, effective descent topology, fpqc is not canonical. Some topologies fine r than canoical topology are relevant because one can compute their points (or rather, their local rings). v-topology, covering map if valuation rings of X lift to valuation rings of Y. Arc-topology, similar but for rank 1 valuation ring, Arc-descent applies in prismatic theory. v0topology stronger than fpqc appeared in Picavet's work. Any finite covering of regular scheme is canonical, hence effective descent morphisms. Flatness lemma is crucial.

Lurie's theorem is important, for C a stable presentable category, TFAE: C is dualisable in Pr_St (category of stable presentable categories and colimit preserving functors), C is a retract in Pr_St of a compactly generated category, the colimit functor colim : Ind(C) -> C admits a left adjoint y^ : C -> Ind(C), the colimit functor is left adjoint to the Yoneda embedding yo : C embed Ind(C), which is fully faithful. Calkin category is defined as the kernel of the colimit functor. Then Lurie's theorems can be reapplied to the Calkin category. https://www.math.columbia.edu/~chaoli/tutorial2012/knots-and-primes.pdf  knots and primes: best dictionary on page 3; quadratic reciprocity means primes are knots in3. manifolds and this is a statement of linking numbers of these knots. Nullstellensatz means evaluating at elements and maximal ideals that vanish at the point. Label complex line as Spec C[t], set of prime ideals of C[t]. Used trancendence degree as measure of dimension or you can use Krull dimension i.e. supremum of strict chain of prime ideals. Key idea is between knots and primes is to use etale cohomological dimension, Spec F_p is an arithmetic analogue of S^1. Spec Z satisfies Artin-Verdier duality, etale pi 1 of Spec Z has cohomological dimension 1, so Spec Z is a natural analogue of R^3, turns out correct analogue of S^3 is Spec Z union prime at infinity, just as S^3 = R^3 union infinity. Analogy of knots and primes, loops are Frobenius autos, universal covering of loops become separable closure. Linking number is related to Legendre symbol. Symmetry of Linking number gives quadratic reciprocity law (q/p) = (p,q), (p,q) congruent to 1 mod 4, then the (p-1/2)(q-1/2) where does this appear in linking number. Alexander Fox theory is analogous to Iwasawa theory. Tubular neighbourhoods are p-adic integer rings, boundaries are p-adic fields, 3-manifolds are number rings, knots are rational primes. Knot is image of embedding of S^1 into S^3, knot type is equivalence class of embeddings under ambitent homotopy groupoid. K, remove a point S^3 not contained in K, projection of K onto a plane in R^3 is regular if finitely many multiple points, all of which are double points. Connected sum of two knots. Prime knots are knots that cannot be expressed as connected sum of two nontrivial knots, composite otherwise. Knot group. Complement fo S^3\int(V_k) is the knot exterior. Boundary of disk D2 in V_K. Wirtinger presentation. Fermat sum of two squares, odd prime p = x^2 + y^2 iff congruent 1 mod 4. I proved this analytically using Wilson's theorem and pigeonhole. Key observation is check whether -1 is quad residue mod p.

https://math.stanford.edu/~vakil/725/class21.pdf  line bundles by Vakil; (1) a total space of a line bundle is a complex manifold with a map to the line bundle as a base; (2) there is a trivial line bundle from the total space of the product of X and complex C to X, global sections are analytic functions on the manifold; (3) sections of line bundle form a sheaf, this is an invertible sheaf since the cocycle condition follows from descent, it is an invertible sheaf on a variety which is a structural ring module on open covers of the variety with local isomoprhisms and descent; (4) a line bundle has an invertible sheaf as a sheaf of sections; (5) a meromorphic section is a rational section is a section of a line bundle over a nonempty open set, this generalise meromorphic functions; (6) a tautological line bundle is a line bundle that assigns a point in projective space to the corresponding line through the origin in affine space of a higher dimension; (7) the tautological line bundle on the project line is not isomorphic to the trivial sheaf, one constructs the meromorphic section corresponding to the plane where x_0 = 1 in the affine n-space, it blows up at x_0 = 0 in projective n-space and nowhere else, cover the projective line with two standard opens, first standrrd open s0 = 1, it has no zeroes, the second standard open has  coordinate s (z_0;1) points on the line are (z0s_1, s_1) therefore there is a simple pole at z_0 = 0, sum s of its orders of vanishing is -1, not 0, so it is not the trivial sheaf (I cannot remember this proof at all);

https://ncatlab.org/nlab/files/StableChromaticGlossary.pdf  stable chromatic glossary, good rapid introuduction to stable chromatic homotopy theory, I think I will find the time to digest this: (1) A_infty ring spectra is a ring spectrum whose multiplication is associative up to all higher homotopy, think associative sphere spectrum algebra; (2) E_infty ring spectru, an commutative sphere spectrum algebra; (3) a sphere spectrum module is indexed by an infinite dimension real inner product space as a module of a sphere spetrum over smash product of L-spectra which are algebras for a monad from the linear isomoetries monad, it has a strictly symmetric monoidal smash product; (4) a symmetric spectra is a sequence of spaces with operation of the symmetric group and composite maps such that composites of sequences of spaces with the circle increases its term in the seqeunce, and it has composition that is equivariant under the product of symmetric group; (6) a spectra is a sequence of spaces with an operation of a group and a smash product; (7) an orthogonal spectra is a sequence of spaces using the orthogonal roup with maps increasing by 1, and equivariance of orthogonal groups under composition; (8) actuallly a good definition of the Grothendieck topology can be defined as covering families preserved under base change and composition, canonical example is etale topology of schemes.

http://wwwf.imperial.ac.uk/~buzzard/xena/pdfs/liquid_tensor_experiment.pdf  Liquid tensor experiment, I have finished reading this, gives a very rapid introduction to the topic. (1) key theorem, let p and p' be the norm order in the unit interval of reals, let S be a profinite set or an countable inverse limit of finite sets; let V be a p-Banach space, let M_p'(S) be the space of p' measures on S, then the Ext-group in condensed abelian groups of the space of p' measures on profinite set S for i greater than or equal to 1 vanish; (2) compact generated weak Hausdorff spaces embed fully into condensed sets with internal homs i.e. T -> Hom(X,Y) is functorial to T x X -> Y or condensed sets are locally cartesian closed, or form a topos up to kappa-smallness page 1; (3) motivation is p-adic annulus T in C_p, modulus T = 1, extra p-power roots, infinite tower of covers, pass to limit object as a perfectoid space to have tilting to relate to characteristic p, the main idea of the pro-etale site; (4) condensed sets are sheaves on the pro-etale site of a point, covers are given by finite collections of jointly surjective maps; (5) motivation is compact Hausdorff spaces serve as spaces that admit surjections from a finite set i.e. fibres are cardinality at least 1; one can then recover T as the quotient of S by R in the diagonal product S * S; (6) decimal expansions form a surjection from a profinite set onto the interval which is a diaognal product of {0,1,2,...9}, so the interval is a quotient of a profinite set; (7) exact sequence of discrete to typical topology has trivial kernel and cokernel in TopAb, but quotient of continuous maps valued in R mod out by locally constant maps valued in R; (8) compact proj generators are extremelly disconnected profinite sets, and is tied to axaiom of choice, non-principle ultrafilters; one can do homological algebra; (9) locally compact abelian groups in condensed abelian groups as a category has trivial ext groups for i >= 2, nontrivial Ext^1 which agree with the category of locally compact abelian groups, normal homs or Ext^0 are usual continuous maps, and is prove by Breen-Deligne resolution; (10) completions to solidity is the key idea, a condensed abelian group is solid if for any profinite set S (countable cofiltered limit of discrete space) any map from S to M (M is the base, S is the fibration) extends uniquely (Kan extension like) to Z[S]^solid, where Z[S] solid is the inverse limit of Z[S_i]. This is the hom of continuous maps from S to Z, or the Z-valued measures on S, any maps gives a unique map sending a measure to the integral over the measure, there is a map of Dirac measures; (11) discrete groups are solids, limits, colimits, internal homs, tensor products have to be resolidified, one can do p-adic functional analysis; (12) a condensed liquid space is p-liquid if for any profinite set S, and for any p' < p, any map admits a extension of measures over p' for S to V, one cannot naively use solidification Z[S] for the reals since reals have solification 0, directly take union of compact Hausdorff spaces of signed Radon measures as a condensed abelian group, so liquidification is for the case of solidification of R; (13) reals are not locally profinite, bounds on real numbers leads to instability in addition;

https://simon-pepin.github.io/notes/six-functors.pdf  six functor formalisms on abelian groups: (1) examples on page 5; (2) there is a canonical isomorphism for a projection formula under derived hom (as opposed to tensor product), this is known as formal local Poincare-Verdier duality, proof is due to adjunction, projection formula, and the Yoneda lemma, see page 10;

https://ncatlab.org/nlab/files/LurieChromaticHomotopyTheory.pdf  notes on chromatic homotopy theory; (1) the Atiyah-Hirzebruch spectral sequence is a computation on multiplicative cohomology theory for H^p(X ; E^q(*)) -> E^p+q(X); (2) example is let X be infinite dimensional projective space, its ordinary cohomology groups are Z[t], where t in H^2(X;Z) is a generator;

https://people.mpim-bonn.mpg.de/scholze/Complex.pdf  Complex Geometry by Peter Scholze and Dustin Clausen, I need to reread this again with more background in analytic geometry, functional analysis, Banach and Stein stuff, and background in condensed math from the previous two sets of notes: (1) page 6, a sheaf of holomorphic functions has transition maps on open discs convergent power series with transition maps compatible with evaluation at points, so holomorphicity can be checked locally, once locally defined it gives a global convergent Taylor series expansion with vanishing higher sheaf cohomology; (2) page 7, existence of sheaf of holomorphics come from Cauchy's integral formula, the analyticity of holomorphics as special smooth functions, use bump functions to see sheaf vanishes on higher cohomology; (3) idea of analytic ring is topological ring with topological A module such that A pullbacks from the completion of N with infinity extends uniquely to a pullback to summable sequences, more concretly S is profinite, the A-module is cA-valued measures on S, these sequences are sending elements to measures in the A-module, with a unique extension to an integral, page 8; (4) N and infinity is in general profinite set with a countable inverse limit, page 8; (5) an anlytic ring is a topological ring A, with a module of measures as a topological A-module (example Radon-measures) for any profinite set (which are Dirac deltas), this analogies fails due to R being funky, page 8. (6) topological abelian groups do not form an abelian kernel, since the map from the discrete topology to the natural topology has trivial kernel, cokernel and is not an isomorphism; (7) condensed mathematics is motivated by the exact sequence of the discrete topology to the natural topology, which forms a quotient of continuous functions quotiented out by the local constant functions, where this quotient is a condensed abelian group functor; (8) page 10 gives a preliminary definition, analytic rings are condensed rings with a condensed A-module of measures for any test space a profinite set, with maps from profinite sets to condensed A-module of vector space "Dirac measures" with a full subcategory of M_A-completed condensed A-modules admitting a unique extension as an abelian subcategory or a "heart" of Cond(A), stable under extensions; (9) page 11 gives motivation to promote the sheaf of holomorphic functions to an abelian tensor category; (10) Inverse limits of finite discrete spaces with a basis of clopen sets (used compact Hausdorff here) form a category of compact Hausdorff totally disconnected topological spaces, with a canonical choice for the inverse limit disgram to be indexed by set of disjoint union decompositions into finitely many nonempty clopen sets, with partial order given by refinement, this canonical index diagram is cofilterd, so this inverse limit is cofiltered inverse limit of finite sets, page 11; (11) a covering is an induced map from a cofinite disjoint union of maps from a common codomain lifted to a full cofiltered inverse limit of finite sets is surjective. This is called the kappa-restricted finitary Grothendieck topology on the category of profinite sets, or a cofiltered kappa-small (card less than kappa) inverse limit of finite sets; (12) on page 12, an element in the category of kappa-condensed set is a contravariant functor of points of restricted kappa-small disjoint union of joint surjective maps, with the contravariances taking finite collections of Prof^op_kappa (kappa small boolean algebras) to finite products; on page 13 pullback functors from a category with a base of large cardinals exist, the inclusion on profinite sets exists as Yoneda class of cardinals, the functor is fully faithful under a cofinal class of cardinals; (13) a quasicompact condensed sets is that for all collections of maps of condensed sets, for all surjections from a base of disjoint opens to a condensed set, there exists a finite indexing subset for this surjection, every profinite set is quasicompact, so the existence of the surjection and a profinite set for the base of the surjection, this gives a cleaner definition; (14) a condensed set is quasicompact if and only if it is the quotient of a profinite set; (15) non-Hausdorff behaviour is ruled out by qusiseparatedness, a quasiseparated condensed set if the diagonal map to the product is quasicompact, form a quasicompact product of profinite set as a condensed set; (16) subcategory of quasicompact quasiseparated condensed sets are basically compact Hausdorff spaces (drop locally), quotietns of profintite sets by closed equivalent relations; (17) a condensed set X is projective if for all other condensed sets Y, the induced surjective pushforward to the condensed set as a base (or target) admits a section i.e. Y ->> X surjection has a section; (18) for every condensed set S, the Stone Cech compactification of the profinite set is a projective condensed set that admits a induced surjective pushforward on the condensed set as a base. The Stone Cech compactification serves as a universal generator for the category of condensed sets; (18) a condensed abelian gbroup is an abelian group object or a sheaf of abelian group of kappa-small cofiltered inverse limits of finite sets, this forms the abelian category. Since the Stone-Cech construction gives enough quasicompact projective profinite objects, there is better exactness; (19) free condensed abelian group on quasicompact projective objects is a compact projective object on the category of ocondensed abelian groups, and is a representable hom functor to the category of abelian groups from condensed abelian groups. (20) liquid vector spaces are condensed abelian groups that are complete which means infinite sums exist and are unique, with integrals of continuous functions as measures; (21) a measure assigns a Borel subset a real number with countable additivity and bounded variation, specify measures for clopen subsets of profinte sets with finite additivity and bounded variation, the signed Radon measures as condensed ring modules being maps from formed from disjoint unions of maps of clopen sets to the real line with zero measure, finite additivity with bounded variation, alternatively one can think of the signed Radon measures as a condensed ring module or the disjoint union of a filtered inverse limit as a embedding to the filtered inverse limit of R^oplus S_i (finite dim vector space over R induced by the disjoint union of profinite sets), i.e. the disjoint union of closed balls of bounded variation of radius C centred at the origin with respect to the Manhattan norm on R^oplus S_i. One can switch the Manhatten norm to any other norm, but p < 1 means closed discs are no longer complex, we have nonconvex functional analysis; (22) page 18 gives signed Radon measures as a module of condensed rings which are the unions of filtered inverse limits of discrete sets of bounded l^p variation that have kappa-small cardinality; (23) IMPORTANT, let V be a condensed abelian group with fixed p for norm, V is p-liquid if for every condensed set and direct images of condensed sets into the condensed anelian group, there is a unique map of condensed abelian groups from the signed Radon measure as a condensed ring module with bounded variation under norm < p which is the disjoint union of closed balls centred at the origin for all l^p norms less than p, such that this unique map is the unique extension of the map of condensed sets into the map of condensed abelian groups. I call this the unique p-liquid map extension.
