https://webhomes.maths.ed.ac.uk/~v1ranick/papers/deligne.pdf  basic structures in Lie theory is either real, complex, or Hamiltonian, octonions give the exceptional structures, work on one categorical higher than you get associativity, spinors are vectors in unitary representations of spin groups and they can be written in terms of Clifford algebras, majorana spinors have real structure, Dirac spinors have complex structures, and symplectic majorana spinors admit a quaternion structures. https://arxiv.org/pdf/1001.2282, algebraic K-theory is something which is defined e.g. for any presentable stable infinity category. It inverts Morita equivalences, preserves filtered colimits in the infinity categorical sense, and sends exact sequences to cofibre sequences any functor that has these properties is a so called localizing invariant, and there is a universal such functor U_loc with target the stable infinity category of noncommutative motives. Extend this,  "continuous K-theory" in terms of large localizing invariants for dualizable stable infinity categories (where you no longer have generation by small categories of compact projective objects) and there is a similar universal characterization by Efimov and Clausen and others, this perspective is clarifying because there are loads of other localizing invariants which one also considers in practice (not least because they are easier to compute than algebraic K-theory) and this universal property automatically produces traces from algebraic K-theory to any other localizing invariant. 

https://math.mit.edu/juvitop/old/notes/2015_Fall/NotesF6.pdf. Algebraic K-theory also essentially encodes everything about special values of L-functions; this is what the conjectures of Beilinson and Bloch-Kato are concerned with, which imply the BSD conjecture among other things

https://arxiv.org/pdf/1706.10100, proof relies on new results in the Gromov-Witten theory of elliptic curves and K3 surfaces. We show the generating series of Gromov-Witten classes of an elliptic curve are cycle-valued quasimodular forms and satisfy a holomorphic anomaly equation. The quasimodularity generalizes a result by Okounkov and Pandharipande, and the holomorphic anomaly equation proves a conjecture of Milanov, Ruan and Shen. We further conjecture quasimodularity and holomorphic anomaly equations for the cycle-valued Gromov-Witten theory of every elliptic fibration with section.

https://www.nikhef.nl/~t58/CFTBW.pdf  Conformal field theory has been an important tool in theoretical physics during the last decades. Its origins can be traced back on the one hand to statistical mechanics, and on the other hand to string theory. Historically the most important impetus came from statistical mechanics, where it described and classified critical phenomena. Mainly after 1984 the subject went through a period of rapid development because of its importance for string theory. In addition there has been important input from mathematics, in particular through the work of Kac and collaborators. One can distinguish yet another separate origin of some ideas, namely from work on rigorous approaches to quantum field theory.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.00478, inhomogeneous spin q-Whittaker polynomials are a family of symmetric polynomials which generalize the Macdonald polynomials at t = 0. In this paper we prove that they are orthogonal with respect to a variant of the Sklyanin measure on the n dimensional torus and as a result they form a basis of the space of symmetric polynomials in n variables. Instrumental to the proof are inhomogeneous eigenrelations, which partially generalize those of Macdonald polynomials. We also consider several special cases of the inhomogeneous spin q-Whittaker polynomials, which include variants of symmetric Grothendieck polynomials or spin Whittaker functions.inhomogeneous spin q-Whittaker polynomials are a family of symmetric polynomials which generalize the Macdonald polynomials at t = 0. In this paper we prove that they are orthogonal with respect to a variant of the Sklyanin measure on the n dimensional torus and as a result they form a basis of the space of symmetric polynomials in n variables. https://arxiv.org/pdf/2306.08644, integration kernels consist of modular tensors, built from convolutions of the Arakelov Green function and its derivatives with holomorphic Abelian differentials, combined into a flat connection. https://ncatlab.org/nlab/files/LurieChromaticHomotopyTheory.pdf  algebraic topology is to study topological spaces by means of algebraic invariants (such as homology or cohomology). There is a balance to be struck here: we would like our invariants to be simple enough to be tractable and computable, but rich enough to convey interesting information about topology. In this course, we are going to study one example where both of these demands can be satisfied. This is the so-called “chromatic” picture of stable homotopy theory, and it begins with Quillen’s work on the relationship between cohomology theories and formal groups.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/1312.7394, cohomology theory known as Tmf, for “topological modular forms,” is a universal object mapping out to elliptic cohomology theories, and its coefficient ring is closely connected to the classical ring of modular forms. Along the way, we produce a natural way to restrict to the cusps, providing multiplicative maps from Tmf with level structure to forms of K-theory.

In this paper, as an analogy, we introduce the concept of “topological monodromies of splitting families” for degenerations of Riemann surfaces, and their “monodromy assortments”. We show that the monodromy assortments of barking families associated with tame simple crusts act as a pseudo-periodic homeomorphism of negative twist on each irreducible component of the main fibres. Using the Selberg trace formula and a detailed analysis of the unipotent orbital integral, we show that the eta and zeta functions defined by the relative traces are regular at the origin so that we can define the eta invariant and the regularized determinant.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/1405.0356, first part is an introduction to 1-summability. The definitions rely on the formal Borel transform and the Laplace transform along an arbitrary direction of the complex plane. Given an arc of directions, if a power series is 1-summable in that arc, then one can attach to it a Borel-Laplace sum, i.e. a holomorphic function defined in a large enough sector and asymptotic to that power series in Gevrey sense.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/1702.04148, quantum mechanical systems with a discrete spectrum. We show that the asymptotic series associated to certain paths of steepest-descent (Lefschetz thimbles) are Borel resummable to the full result.

But— since this is the first of the “Hirzebruch Lectures” that are now planned to be given at each future European Mathematical Congress

https://math.uchicago.edu/~shmuel/L%5E2%20cohomology%20readings/Kammeyer%20Introduction%20To%20_%5E2-%20invariants.pdf  algebraic topology is to study a space via an associated chain complex. https://arxiv.org/pdf/2209.15039, it naturally generalizes Kirwan’s classical partial desingularization algorithm to the context of derived algebraic geometry, preserves quasi-smoothness, and is a derived enhancement of the intrinsic stabilizer reduction constructed by Kiem, Li and the third author.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/math/9901053, q-deform a construction of Kazhdan and Kostant from 1970's which produces quantum Toda Hamiltonians by considering the action of Casimirs of a simple Lie algebra on Whittaker functions on the corresponding Lie group. We also give the affine analog of this generalization. This is done by extending the notion of a Whittaker function to quantum groups and quantum affine algebras. We compute the q-deformed Toda Hamiltonians for Lie algebras of type A and show that they coincide with those known in the theory of integrable systems.

https://www.math.columbia.edu/~woit/twistorp1.pdf  remarkably, the twistor P1 occurs as a fundamental object in both four-dimensional space-time geometry and in number theory. In Euclidean signature twistor theory it is how one describes space-time points. In recent work by Fargues and Scholze on the local Langlands conjecture using geometric Langlands on the Fargues-Fontaine curve, the twistor P 1 appears as the analog of this curve at the infinite prime.

https://www.claymath.org/library/academy/LectureNotes05/Lodaypaper.pdf  survey of some of the nice properties of the associahedron (also called Stasheff polytope) from several points of views: topological, geometrical, combinatorial and algebraic, this is very good paper worth working out in all concrete details. (1) to define associahedron, it is in one to one correspondence of polytope with parenthesizing of 0 to n+1 words i.e. brackets; (2) to define it as a polytope it is isomorphic to Stasheff polytope; (3) to classify spaces homotopically equivalent to loop spaces, one define partial order on Stasheff polytope then read off the eucldiean realisation; (4) to use algebra, one can endow the vector space of planar binary trees with associative algebra using a partial order with a dendriform algebra structures; (5) to construct the associatehedron one enlarges over conics, the coordinates of the edges are important and gives it its shape; (6) to have Stasheff complex, one consider elementary homotopies of putting brackets; (7) to define the covering order of the partial order of the Stasheff polytope, one puts a Tamari order; (8) to define a vertices with permutations, one defines a permutohedron; (9) to define the order of the permutohedron, one uses the weak bruhat order; (10) to define shuffles, one uses the faces of the permutohedron; (11) to study formal power series by replacing integers n with planar binary trees, one use the dendriform series and the dendriform algebra of a Stasheff polytope; (12) to define a commutative dendriform algebra, one defines the Zinbiel algebras; (13) to correspond to Zinbiel algebras, one correspond this to commutative algebras, to correspond to associative algebras, one looks at Lie algebras; (14) to study the trefoil knot, one can relate it to the associahedron by the path of a centre of a square; (15) to define real points of M_0(R) or moduli space of n labeled puncutres of Riemann spheres, one tiles with associahedrons;

We end with two explicit examples of such homomorphisms, one in genus zero and one in genus one.

https://chrisjelliott.github.io/Motivic_L_functions.pdf  exposition on motivic L functions, good paper it is very short and explains a lot, I did not understand the last part though. (1) to count points of varieties of finite fields, one appeal to the Weil conjectures; (2) to prove for Weil conjectures for all curgves and abelian varieties, Weil sued the Tate-module as a linearisation to reduce to linear algebra; (3) to understand linearisations of an algerbaic variety, one produces a number of cohomology theories that remember as much structure as possible, so one develop etale cohomology one can prove the Weil conjectures as per Deligne; (4) to identify the definition of cohomology theories on topology, one must use the Eilenberg Steenrod axioms; (5) to define cohomology theories in algebraic theories, one must resort to thinking about the Weil cohomology theory, and that it does not exist over the rational numbers; (6) to prove that there is no Weil cohomology theory when the field is Q, one uses a super singualr elliptic curve; (7) to define a universal cohomology theory, one will need a theory of motives; (8) to define an intersection product, one enlarges the homset of the category of smooth projective varieties and then choosing rational equivalence to have a Chow ring, then enlarge the homset to include all former morphisms and define the pushforward as the composition law to the intersection product to X x Y x Z and then pullback to X x Z, forming a category Corr(K); (9) to make Corr(K) abelian, one uses idempotent completion or formally splitting idempotents to ensure they have kernels and cokerenls aka Corr(K) is an abelian category; (10) to create the category of pure motives, one must have every Weil cohomology functor factor through realisations to preserve the grading, and formally adjoin inverses tho the Lefschetz motives to produce the category of pure motives; (11) to generalise the zeta function, one defines an L-function which is a holomorphic ufnction defined in C that encodes number theory or scheme of finite type or a motive; (12) to prove that there are infintiely many primes, one can use the fact that zeta has a pole at s = 1, and the product formula of primes; (13) to understand an L-function, one epxresses it as an Euler product, then it has factors from the arithmetic behaviour, then prove theorems on meromorphic continuation and the funcitonal equation; (14) to generalise zeta functions from primes to prime ideals, one will write down the Dedekind zeta functions to get a product formula using a generalisation of the absolute norm of an ideal; (15) to calculate the residue of the pole at s = 1 akin to infinitude of the primes, Hecke proived the analytic class number formula with the classical regulator; (16) to encode the density of the roots of untiy, one defines the regulator and uses Dirichlet's unit theorem; (17) to concretely understand a motivic decomposition of L-function, one studies the basic example of Dedekind zeta functions; (18) to define analogue of primes of number ring for scheme of finite type over Z, one uses closed points to generalise norms of ideals; (19) to fill in missing factors by choosing a finite set of primes S, one needs to study elliptic curves to understand the bad reduction resulting in the incomplete zeta function; (20) to count points over finite fields of prime powers, one uses the Lefschetz trace formula; (21) to make the zet afunction independent of the choice of model E, one must define the inertia group at a prime p to be a kernel of a homomorphism of galois groups, then one can write the zeta factor in a model independent way which is a huge mess; (22) to generalise taking L-functions of Galois representations, one define the Artin L-functions; (23) to define the full subcategory of motives generated by zero dimensional objects, one can find it to be equivalent to the category of Galois representation with the motive mapping to the regular representation of Gal(K/Q);

(1) to give condition by covering of contractible open sets and to weaken it, one may define weakly locally contractible to be embeddings into U, adn contractible in U; (2) to compare sheaf cohomology and singular cohomology, requires semi-locally contractible and paracompact (admits locally finite refinemenet so points belong to finitely many members  of U') then singular cohomology is isomorphic to sheaf cohomology; (3) to ditch paracompactness for this isomorphism, one may use Ramanan's method; (4) to use Ramanan's method, the idea is to define direct limit of complex surjections to form a flasque resolution;

Morava discovered Morava K-theory spectrum K(n). K(0) is rational cohomology, pi K_n = Z / p[v_n^pm 1] where |v_n| = 2(p^n-1), this is related to Lubin Tate BP ring which seems to be a motivation. Chromatic because each column displays periodic families of element, like spectrum in astronomical sense (Miller-Ravenel-Wilson). Bousefield localisation appears here, nice if short exact sequence above were the BP_* homology of a cofibre sequence of spectra. Moore spectrum, how to invert v_1 in the next step? Suppose we have a generalised homology theory, this can be represented by a spectrum E. We say a spectrum Z is E-local if, whenever, f : A -> B is an E_*-equivalence, that is a map inducing an isomorphism E_*A -> E_*B, then induced map f* : [B,Z] -> [A,Z] is also an isomorphism. With this definition, one can define a coaugmented functor L_E such that for any spectrum X, L_E X is E-local, map X -> L_EX is an E_* equivalence. E and X are both connective, L_E X can be described in arithmetic terms, either obtained from X by inverting some set of primes, or it is p-adic completion of single prime, so connectivity means somehow universal stuff and you can take simple algebraic constructions to do the Bousfield localisation? So Bousfield localisation in 1979 is a generalisatioon of inverting set of primes or p-adic completion. Hypothetical spectrum M_n can be constructed from inductively constructed N_n using Bousfield localisation, logical choice for E appeared to be Johnson-Wilson spectrum E(n), closely related to fancier Morava spectrum E_n. Both lead to the same localisation functor. Two spectra or Bousfield equivalent, if they have the same class of acyclic spectra that is E_* C = 0 and E_*'C = 0. The Bousfield equivalence class is denoted by <E>. Information, {E} >= {F} if E_* gives at least as much informations as F_*, maximal Bousfield class is the sphere spectrum, minimal one is that of a point. Wedges and smash products of Bousfield classes are well defined (as one can check). Formal properties of Bousfield classes include W -> X -> Y -> Sum W is a cofibre sequence, <X> <= <W> smash <Y> meaning f is smash nilpotent: f^{^k} : Y^{^k} -> (Sum W)^{^k} is null for some k. For self-map, denote its cofibre, find mapping telescope. Some Bousfield equivalences, sphere spectrum is <SQ> wedge wedge for all prime p <S/p>, this is the prototype example, SQ is rational Moore spectrum, S/p is mod p Moore spectrum. Another example is <BP> >= <H/p> wedge wedge all positive <K(n)>, so <BP> as the graded ring as a result of the formal group llaw contains as much information as <H/p> which is the mod p Eilenberg-Maclane spectrum and full wedge <K(n)>, K(n) is the Morava K-theory. <E(n)> = <E_n> = wedge K(i), smash product of any two wedge summands on the right is contractible (not sure what this means). Localisation functor is determined by Bousfield class <E>. When is map from X to L_infty X an equivalence, if is say X is harmonic, these are the chromatic tower of a p-local spectrum. L_infty X is harmonic localisation. It is dissonant if L_infty X equivalent to point means Morava K-theory K(n)_* X = 0. It follows from definitions that there are no essential maps from a dissonant spectrum to a harmonic one. Ravenel paper, every p-local finite spectrum is harmonic, p-local connective spectrum X is harmonic when BP_* X has finite projective dim as BP_*-module, mod p Eilenberg-Maclane spectrum H/p is dissonant. (1) to do some local langlands dual reductive group, one extends it to Hamiltonian G and Ghat-actions, these index objects and numerical measurements on both sides; (2) to ignore trivial divergences without regularisation, one discards smaller orbits; (3) to investigate numerical duality in the local Langlands duality, one defines toric dual to give a natural combinatorial definition; (4) to evaluate the action of the algebrraic group scheme, one uses an eigenmeasure which is a differential form of top degree on th esmooth locus; (5) to investigate the preimage of integer powers of Frobenius inside the etale fundamental group, one will need to study Weil groups; (6) to normalise local class field theory, one needs to send the geometric Frobenius element to 1/q where q is the size of the residue field; (7) to discuss orbits of toric varieties, one needs to define fans of toric varieties, which are collections of rational strongly convex cones; (8) to investigate T-orbits in X, one uses the fact that cones in the fan are in bijection to T-orbits in X as hom-sets; (9) to investigate Langlands duality, one can investigate the toric duality between the Langlands torus T and its toric dual; (10) to investigate the weak numerical duality between toric varieties, one can look at Deligne-Mumford stacks wihich are global quotients of a toric variety by a finite group; (11) to investigate unramified twists of the L-function, one can restrict to unramified orbits, where unramfieid orbits of the regularised automorphic period corresponds to the L-function;

(1) to study commutative fields with elements division algebras, one studies Brauer groups. (2) to describe Brauer groups, these are central simple algebras up to Morita equivalence. (3) to formulate the theory of local rings, one can define 0 != 1, and inv(x) or inv(1-x) where inv means exist 1 = xy; (4) to define Henselian in coherent way, one can describe strictly Henselian instead; (4) to define Zariski spectrum in a logical way, define it as universal support of R to Z(R), where supports are maps R to M to bunded distributive lattice M satisfying ideal axioms; (5) to define unramifiable, one means the polynomial has a simple root in some extension of the residue field; (6) to define Azumaya algebras, it is algebra that is projective of finite type, and tensor prod A otimes A^op to End_k(A) be isomoprhism; (7) to give constructive proofs in Azumaya algebra theory, one use tree inductions.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/2508.15971, Deninger foliated dynamical systems, good paper with one of the best introductions, but very hard to read in the middle. (1) to study motivic L-functions, Deninger proposed to attach arithmetic cohomology to arithmetic schemes with the formula playing Lefschetz trace formulas; (2) to do foliations of dynamical systems on arithmetic schemes, Deninger invented the rational Witt space; (3) to do functional analysis of the Riemann zeta function, Connes introduced the adele class space; (4) to define the adele class space, it is the space of leaves of the idele class groups; (5) to do non-commutative geometry, one must come up with a theory of foliations; (6) to connect Deninger's foliated dynamical systems, and Connes-Consani adelic spaces, this paper gives a map of interest with an arithmetic linking homomorphism; (7) to discover the geometric monodromy around the orbit, the idea is to define an arithmetic linking homomorphism with an homomorphism of etale fundamental groups, then class field theory describes how primes are linked with each other in arithmetic topology, the linking number is the Legendre symbol, (8) to define the Connes adele class space, it is a dynamical system equipped with action of idele class group, then it is a noncommutative geometric model for the spectrum of a number ring O_K; (9) to define Deninger systems, one takes a rational Witt vectors as a ring as a functor of commutative rings, then do a lot of work I do not understand; (10) global fields like Q can be categorised as fields that sit discretely and cocompactly in locally compact spaces.

https://sites.math.rutgers.edu/~ctw/quotients.pdf  notion of quotient in geometric invariant theory introduced by Mumford, good set of notes in revising Lie group theory. (1) finite dimensional versions of Harder-Narasimahan and Jordan-Holder filtrations, one technique can be using convexity of Kempf-Ness functions; (2) moment polytope computation, one can use some sort of qantisation; (3) time one flow at the identiy, use exponential map of the Lie algebra (which is left invariant vector field Lie action) on the Lie group where group multiplication is a smooth math. (4) make Lie group a vector space on it, define left action as smooth map with associativity and identiy, then one has K-manifold, and if works like a linear algebra, then it is K-equivariant; (5) remember sign of Lie bracket, due to left invariant vector fields, these generate right action, while actions are defined on the left; (6) to stabilise a fixed point x, define the stabiliser and the orbit, where orbit is constant is stabiliser; (7) to define de Rham operators, one needs graded derivations or a product rule on D(a0a1) = D(a0a1) + (-1)^d|a0| a0 D(a1); (8) to distinguish complex linear transformations from classical groups of linear symplectomorphisms, sympletic manifold with sympletic closed non-degenerate two form defined; (9) to define conserved quantity, one defines symmetries of a Hamiltonian dynamical system with energy function, and Noether's theroem; (10) to remove restriction on symplectomorphisms since must be same direction, one uses a Lagrangian correspondence or Lagrangian submanifolds to remove the dimension restriction; (11) to generalise symplectic geometry, one defines Poisson geometry but not compatible with Lagrangian correspondences as morphisms; (12) to define orgdinary momentum in symplectic manifold, one defines a moment map using Hamiltonian maps which lifts to equivariant map of Lie algebras and comoment maps; (13) to calculate area of the two sphere, one can do a moment map calculation this can be done to obtain the area of the cylinder between the two z-values; (14) to do linear algebra, exterior algebra, pullbacks, interior products, one defines a Hamiltonian K-manifold; (15) to generalise Darboux theorem, one use Morse theory to define an equivariantly closed differential form, this is introduced in Atiyah and Bott; (16) to interpret linearisastion of an action, the moment map can be thought of in terms of an infinitesimal linearisation or a lift to a vector bundle; (17) to ensure that quotients are well defined, where free actions means symplectic structure is admitted, one defines symplectic quotient as preimage of moment map on 0, quotiented out by the Hamiltonian K-manifold; (18) to give an example of symplectic quotient; the product of unit two spheres with diagonal action of K = SO(3) is the moduli space of closed n-gons with lengths rescaled by lambda or eigenvalues, where the moment maps sends each point to some sort of trace; (19) to describe compatibility with complex structure, one has Kahler manifolds see page 18; (18) orbits of points and stabilisers of points are a thing on page  3; (19) a Hamiltonian dynamical system has a sympletic manifold X and an energy function H in C^\infty(X), if K is another function like component of engular moment, {K, H} = 0L_K^# H = L_H# K, H is invariant under the flow generated by K# iff K is conserved in time, this is the content of Noether's theorem, for avery symmetry of a Hamiltonian system, there is a conserved quantity; (19) sums, products and duals are natural on sympletic manifolds, symplectomorphisms must have same dimension.

https://srln.se/presentation.pdf  notes on Galois theory with an introduction to etale pi 1, extremely amazing introduction to Galois categories and etale pi 1: (1) to prove the fundamental theorem of Galois theory as a fucntor: (1) one has the opposite category of finite etale k-algebra, etale is isomorphic to finite product of separable extensions K_i of k, then the opposite category to finite left Gal(k)-sets taking A to hom_k(A, k_s) so this is an antiequivalence of categories; (2) to choose an absolute Galois group, one must choose a separable closure; (3) to define the fundamental theorem of Galois theory, one must use limits of finite etale algebras or union of all finite separable extensions; (4) to study the fundamental group and its action, one must study the monodromy action of pi_1(X, x) acting on the fibre preimage(x), this will be a path from y to another point in the fibre over X, defining a permutation of the fibre; (5) to study topology of Galois theory,one can have the main theorem of covering theory where coverings of topological space X is equivalent to left pi_1(X,x)-sets; restrict to finite covers means these are continuous left profinite completed left pi_1(X,x) sets; (6) to define Galois categories, one uses the fundamental covering tehory with a functor called the fibre functor lifting the category to G-sets; (7) to define the etale fundamental group, one defines etale_pi^1(X, bar x) to be G or automorphisms of the fibre functor; (8) to study anabelian schemes, they must be reconstructibel from their fundamental group under various definitions of smoothness, this is the content of anabelian geometry; (9) to define Galois categories, these are continuous left G-sets on a profinite group, then G is the fundamental group of the Galois category, with a fibre lifting from G. = Aut(Fibre functor) from C to G-sets;

https://web.math.princeton.edu/~kollar/FromMyHomePage/modbook-final.pdf  Kollar's book on varieties, 462 pages, main objects are stable curves, these are proejctive curves that singularities are notes and K_C is ample. Stable varieties generalise this, to projective variteies where singularities are semi-log-canonicla, K_X is ample. Moduli theory, need to describe families to understand. Flat, projective morphsims whose fibres are stable curve. Too many flat projective morphsims whose fibres are stable surfaces. Stable curves with weighted points are also important. Divisoral part is not necessary flat, so pair (X, delta) is stable, singularities are semi-log-canonical, K_X + delta. is ample. Rational maps ar edefined on dense set, contraction is proper morphism g: X -> Y such that pushforward g_* O_X = O_. Variety is integral scheme of finite type over a field. Stable variety or local stable variety is reduced, pure dimensional, but possibly reducible. What does reducible means? Schemes are noetherian and separated.
 
https://people.math.osu.edu/katz.60/slides/KatzHTICIMPA.pdf  Hodge theory combinatorial in 93 slides, char polynomial of a subsapce, use inclusion and exclusion to express intersection of V in k^n+1 as linear combination of intersections with L_I, L_I is coordinate subspace. Example: V be generic subspace, then inclusion exclusion principle gives formula Euler char sum (-1)^i[V cap L_i], motivic expression. Need to be careful to prevent overcounting, definition of flat is motivated, a subset I in {0, ... n} is a flat if for any J containing I, V cap L_J not equal V cap L_I. Rank of flat is codim(V cap L_I in V). Then write for sum choice sum of flats to prevent overcounting (-1)^rho(I) nu_V is always positive. These notes are super hard. Characteristic poly is some cursed sum expresion equiv to mu_0 q^r+1 + ... (-1)^r+1(mu_r+1) or poly in q with highest degree r+1, evaluation of classes. Generic subspace case.

https://app.icerm.brown.edu/assets/332/2350/2350_2572_Robles_020120211000_Slides.pdf  15 pages on linear structure on Hodge theory, quite good in linking sl(2,F) to Hodge theory, Hodge structure is given by Hdoge decomposition on complexification by tensor product with C i.e. base change. Teh latter is a direct sum of H^p,q with conjugation property bar(H^p,q) = H^q,p. First arose in complex Kahler manifolds. Schnell's introduction to Hodge theory. Hodge structur eis effective H^p,q = 0 implies, p,q >= 0. Can write Hodge diamond. All Hodge structures are effective. Pure Hodge structure of weight w if H^p,q = 0 for all p+q not equal to w. http://higeom.math.msu.su/people/taras/talks/2018Nsk-lecture1.pdf  toric varieties in 27 slides, cohomology of toric manifolds, complete regular fans. Algebraic torus isomorphic to product of C
times n copies, contains compact torus as Lie (but not algebraic) subgroup, toric variety is normal complex algebriac variety containing algebraic torus as Zariski open, acts on variety with dense orbit. Toric variety appeared as equivariant compactification of algebraic torus. Convex polyhedral cone appears as inner product of measure values with basis in R^n. Rational if can be chosen from integer lattice. This is a much better defintion. Strongly convex if does not contain a line. Simplicial if generated by part of basis in R^n, regular if generated by part of basis in Z^n. Fan if finite collection of strongly convex cones such that every face of a cone belongs to collection, intersection of two cones is a face of each. Cones are affine varieties, complete fans are compact varieties, normal fans of polytopes are projective varieties, regular fans are nonsingular varieties, simplicial fans are orbifolds. This translation does not make sense to me.

https://www.andrej.com/assets/slides/laughable-theorems.pdf, statement of Lawvere's fixed point theorem, if e: A -> B^A is surjective then every f: B -> B has a fixed point. Proof: there is a x in A such that e(x) = lambda.f(e(y)(y)). Then e(x)(x) = f(e(x)(x)) i.e. without loss of generality every f has a fixed point e(x)(x). Now take contrapositive, prove that there is no sruekction from A to Omega^A.

https://terrytao.wordpress.com/tag/bolzano-weierstrass-theorem/, bounded sequence has convergence subsequence in R, by transfer principle, its nonstandard extension x_K for any infinite K in *N\JN is a nonstandard real number that is infinitesimally closed to a unique standard real number as a limit point of the sequence. Given a bounded sequence, take an infinite K in nonstandard naturals, set L = st(x_K) the standard part, subsequence x_n_k mapping to indices infinitesimally closed to K  converges to L or the standard part. In fact, key ingredient to construct infinitesimal nonstandard reals.

Tangent category theory, tangent functor has endorfunctors, projection, 0 and addition of tangent functors. Vertical lift, canonical flip. The basic example to copy is multivaraible calculus. This is very synthetic but it is unclear if these are the right axioms. The idea si so that the tangent space at M is defined by a pullback for a tangent category. https://c-keyes.github.io/files/definitions.pdf  list of algebraic geometry definitions in 46 pages, MUST READ everyday. Abelian categories has internal hom as abelian group, biproducts, distributive composition, and satisfies the first isomorphism theorem with canonical factorisation of monomorphisms and epimorphisms. But semiadditive categories are more natural, morphisms take coproducts to products. Semirings are more natural. Laurent Lafforgue, abelian category with extra structure this is wrong! An abelian category is just a category where natural morphisms from coproducts to products are isomorphisms. An object is F-acyclic for left exact additive functor i.e. right derived functors R^i F exist if higher right derived functors vanishes, it also works for left derived functors, F contravariant, injective (projective) objects are acyclic. Acyclic resolutions are used to compute derived functor. Additive functors are functors of abelian categories where induced map on homsets is homomorphism of abelian groups. Adjoint if there is a natural bijection of homsets. Left adjoint if acting on preimage category, right adjoint if acting on postimage category. Affine if isomorphic to Spec A for some ring. Map of schemes is afine if inverse image is affine open subscheme. Affine local means sufficient to check on affine covers. Affine space is vector space of n-tuples. As a scheme, closed points are classical points where k is algebraically closed. Ampleness means to generate enough sections to separate points and jets i.e. enough sections to define morphism to proejctive space, asymptotic positivity. L^otimes embeds into projective space depends on projective embedding. Ample, let X be projective (proper is sufficient) A-scheme, L a line bundle, L is very ample if exists n+1 sections with no common zero such that linear system defines closed embedding X into P^n_A. Very ampleness implies base point freeness, equivalent definition is that X is isomorphic to Proj S_*, S_* is finitely generated graded ring over A, L is sheaf of rings O(1). If pi: X -> Spec A, then this is pi-very ample. Line bundle L on X is ample if L^otimes is very ample for some n > 0 or for all n >> 0. Ampleness and properness are the central properties for a lot of geometric arguments. Base points: let X be a k-scheme and L be a line bundle on X, a base point P in X of L, is defined the be the point which all elements of the global sectiosn Gamma(X, L) vanish, line bundles as coefficient of global sections? Base point free if no base point. Base point free locus is complement of base locus. Base point freeness means line bundles is globally generated. Use this for n+1 dim subset of global sectiosn Gamma(X, L) as morphism X - base locus -> P^n_k, map is given by evaluating a basis of sections. Canonical bundle, suppose X is a smooth k-variety of dimension n, canonical sheaf is taken to be the top wedge power of Kahler differential Omega_X/k, which is an invertible sheaf i.e.a. line bundle on. X. This is also the determinant of the Omega_X/k. When X is projective (proper is sufficient), this canonical bundle or sheaf is a dualizing sheaf. Useful in genus computation and cohomology, and is central in Riemann-Roch and Riemann-Hurwitz. Cartier divisor, global section of the quotient sheaf of total quotients K^\times i.e. sheaf of invertible meromorphic functions, by O_X^\times or sheaf of invertible regular function, equivalent to collection of pairs of open covers and f_i meromorphic on U_i such that f_i / f_j is a unit in O_X(U_i cap U_j). Cartier divisors easy way meromorphic/regular, easy way is take effective divisor to be a closed subscheme D include in X so that the ideal sheaf of D is an invertible sheaf of X, the sum of such divisors can be thought of ideal sheaf. Carteir divisors are principal if it is in the image of the map of global section section(X, K_mero) -> section(X, K_mero/O_reg). Define equivalence relation on Cartier divisors, D equiv D' iff D - D' is principal divosr, get Cartier divisor class group. Category of opens, objects are opens, morphisms are inclusion of opens. Cohomology is obstruction to solving gluing problems or integration or some sort of summation, so this is why there is the derived functor formalism. Cech cohomology, let F be a sheaf of abelian groups on quasicompact separated scheme, with finite open cover, Cech complexes are defined with respect to this cover to form sequence 0 -> prod sect(cap U_i, F) -> ... note that sheaves are used as coefficient on global sections. Maps in complex is given by + or 0 in restriction map, sign given by position of additional index to ensure it is a complex i.e. boundary^2 = 0, i-th Cech cohomology with respect to chosen cover is cohomology. nontrivial fact that it does not depend on chosen cover. Continuous map is closed if pi(K) or image is closed for all closed subsets K in X. Universally closed if for all Z -> Y, induced map on fibre product X x_Y Z -> Z is closed. Lcoally closed if intersection of open and closed subset. Closed embedding, affine morphism of schemes is closed embedding if for every open Spec B in Y, preimage of Spec B is isomorphic to Spec A and induced map from B to A in surjection, this is so that one can have A isomorphic to B/I for ideal I. Coherent, fg A-module M is coherent if for any map (not necessarily surjective) A^n -> M if the kernel is fintiely generated, coherent implies fp implies fg, A is Noetherian then these three notions coincide so coherent is only considered in non-Noetherian case? Wait A is Noetherian as module? Cohomology, i-tho cohomology of complex is ker del^i / im del^{i -1}. Given a map of complexe,s check induces map of cohomology, short exact sequences of complexes induces long exact sequence on cohomology. Presheaf cokernel of a map of presheaves given by (coker phi)(U) = coker phi(U) satisfying universal property of cokernels in the category of presheaves on X, F and G are sheaves, presheaf cokernel is not in general a sheaf, cokernel of phi as sheafification of presheaf cokernel. Completions are not defined in this notes, can be inverse limit of A/I^n, mimicking definition of exponentiation. Complexes are sequences of objects A^i for i in Z with maps delta^i from A^i -> A^o+1 such that consecutive boundary maps vanishes, or im delta^i in ker delta^i+1 (not equal, be careful). Condition (*), seems to be a bunch of common conditions for things to work, Noetherian, separated, integral, regular in codimension 1. Space of functions on the fibre abvoe p. Degree of diviosr, if D as combination of n_i p_i is Weil diviosr on regular projective curve C/k, degree of D is deg D = sum n_i deg p_i, for degree of points is given as above. Degree of line bundle, let L be invertible sheaf of projective curve C/k, degree of L is Euler char, chi(C,L) - chi(C, O_C), importantly when D = div s for section of L, we have agreement that deg D = deg_C L. If D = div s for section of L i.e. L isomorphic to O(D), then deg D = deg_C L. Degree of coherent sheaf, if F is coherent sheaf of integral projective curve, deg of F is chi(C,F) - (rank F) chi(C, O_C). Degree of line bundle when rank F = 1, so agrees. Degree via hyperplanes,  X embed in to P^n as projective k-variety, intersect X with dim X hyperplanes in general position and counting point, no multiplicity due to geneal position, degree via Hilbert polynomial, degree is (dim X)! the coefficient of the m^dim X term, not an intrinsic invariant, depends on the category. delta-functors, collection of functors along with boundary morphism for every short exact sequence. Derived functors, abelian category with enough injectives, F be left exact to another abelian category, right derived functor is cohomology of functor on injective resolution, left derived functor is cohomology on projective resolutions, enough projectives. Determinant, top wedge power of locally free sheaf of rank r, this is a locally free sheaf on rank 1, calculated locally on r-th exterior power, most famous determinant sheaf is canonical sheaf which is the determinant of Kahler differentials do not understand determinant, diagonal morphisms is unique map to fibre produced of schemes induced by identical mapping X -> X on schemes, well defined in any category with fibre product, in category of schemes this is locally closed embedding. Divisors represent geometries of zeroes of sections, recall that Nullstellesatz gives ideals as representative of evaluation maps, divisors lets us study these maps in more detail. Differential, module of relative Kahler differentials generated by symbols da coming from differential relations, so it satisfies a sum rule, a product rule and db = 0 for all b in image. Dominant, a rational map is dominant if open U in X such that U -> Y has dense image. Dualising sheaf, let X be projective (or proper) scheme over field k of dim m, dualizing sheaf for X is coherent sheaf omega on W, with trace map, such that we have the pairing on Hom(F, omega) x H^n(X, F) -> H^n(X, omega) -> k by trace map, inducign isomorphism Hom(F, omega) -> H^n(X, F), so coherent sheaf with other coherent sheaf F, dualizing sheaf is part of proof of Serre duality. Effaceable, additive functor is effaceable if for each object A, there is a monomorphism u: A -> M such that F(u) = 0, coeffaceable defined dually with epis. Not sure what is regular, or regular local ring iff dim A = dim A/m m/m^2, Noetherian ring is regualr ring if regular local ring for all primes, point p where O_x,p is not regular local ring is singularity. Relative proj, schem obtained by gluing together affine opens, not trivial. Do not understand relative proj. Relative spec, universal property somehow, representable by Spec A or functor of points of X-schemes to sets. Do not understand relative spec. Representable, exists F(X) such that F(X) = Mor(X, Y), F is isomorphic to h_Y, core of it is very Yoneda. Ringed space is pari of top space and sheaf of rings known as structural sheaves, isomorphic if homemorphism of top and iso of structural sheaf, locally ringed space if all stalks are local rings. Local rings have unique max ideals or reflects invertibility. Section, sections of opens U under sheaf F is F(U), sections of full top space X itself is global section. Schemes are ringed spaces that are locally affine for every point there exists open neighbourhood that is locally isomorphic to affine schemes. Scheme over A, scheme X such that sections of structure sheaf are A algebra, restriction maps are maps of A-algebras, data of scheme with map to Spec A by universsal property, makes schemes into a category. Scheme-theorectic closure is locally closed embedding of scheme theoretic image of pi. Scjeme theorectic image, intersection of all closed subschemes with inclusion Z -> Y give map pi: X -> Y, image lies in Z if composition J_Z/Y -> O_Y -> pi_* O_X is 0. Segre embedding, closed embedding of P^m x P^n into P^mn + m + n by formal group law is Segre embedding. Separated, morphism of schemes is separated if diagonal morphism is closed embedding, do not understand this definition. Scheme is separated over A if structure map X -> Spec A is separated. Scheme itself is separated if map X -> Spec Z is separated. A (generically) finite morphism of integral schemes is said to be generically separable if dominand and induced extension of function fields is separable extension of fields i.e. can turn linear, auto in char 0, hypothesis in Hurwitz theorems, Sheaf is presheaf with identity or separation, and gluing or descent. Sehaf associated to A-module, sections of bar(M) are locally constant functions from U to disjoint union of localisations M_p, restriction maps areo ones restricting to smaller neighoburhood, do not understand sheaf associated to A-module. There is also a projective version of this and associated sheaf. Sheaf cohomology functors are functors from category of sheaves of abelian groups to category of abelian groups, are right derived functors o fglobal section functor, these are the i-th cohomology groups, and detect obstructions to global sections. Sheaf hom is functor given by U -> Hom(F|U, G|U), do not understand this definition of sheaf hom. Sheaf of ideals is subsheaf of O_X such that J(U) in O_X(U) is ideal for all open U in X. Sheaf of O_X-modules, is sheaf of abelian groups for every affine open, section F(U) is O_X(U)-module. Sheafification, sheaf initial in category of sheaves with maps from presheaf. Universal property. Smooth, k-smooth of dim d, if exists cover of X by opens of the form Spec fg poly ring/ideal of fg relations, Jacobian has corank d at all points, k-smooth if cotangent sheaf is locally free of rank d, smooth of relative dimension if some open cover condition somehow that I do not understand. Stalk is colimit of sections running all opens containing a point. Stalk of a point of a scheme, is stalk of structure sheaf at p. This turns out to be interpeted as p as priem ideal of A if X = Spec A. Support of sheaf of abelian groups is set of points with nonzero stalks, if F quasicoherent of finite type (eg locally free of finite ranj), Supp is closed subset. Symmetric algebra, elements of the form m_1 otimes ... m_n - m_sigma(1) otimes ... m_sigma(n) for any permutation sigma in symmetri cgroup S_n, universal property can be defined. Define locally the symmetric algebra if one looks at quasicoherents. Cotangent space, quotient m/m^2 for loal ring, vector space over A/m, dual vector space is Zariski tangent space of (A,m) whcih appears as homs from m/m^2 to A/m. IF X is a scheme, Zariski ko tangent space appears as vector psace over fraction field of point. Elements of Zariski cotangent space are differentials or like rulers, elements of Zariski tangent space are tangent vectors. Cotangent sheaf are conormal sheaf on diagonal. Do not understand, tangent sheaf is taken to be dual. Tensor algebra. n-th tensor product is n-fold tensor product, not commutative, satisfies universal property similar to free object, can define tensor algebra locally for quasicoherent, must check it glues. Tensor product is covariant functor from B-module to A-modules satisfying universal property of factorising bilinear maps. To show it exists, can do A-linear combinations of simple tensors. Hard part is sheafification, tensoring is right exact, does not preserve limit in sheaf axioms. Tor is tor functor, left derived functor of M otimes -. Tor(M, -) is lefte xact, induced long exact sequence as kernel terminating in proj res where tor0 is tensor product. Tor vanishes for all N, equiv to flatness. Total quotient ring is localisation of S^-1 A, A be ring, S multiplicative subset of non zero divisors. Closest thing to field of fractions. Sheaf of total quoptient ring. Twist of variety is another variety such that becomes isomorphic upon base extension, prototype example is quadratic twist of elliptic curve. Twisting sheaf, sheaf is taking degeree n functions, transition fucntions is multiply by n-powers, unsure about this definition. Universal if for any delta functor, not sure what his is meant to mean. Unramified, formally unramified and locally of finite type, Formally unramified if sheaf of relative differentials is 0. Ramification locus is support of sheaf of relative differentials. Valuation is group hom from field - 0 -> G satisfying v(x+y) >= min(v(x), v(y)). Vlauation ring is subring of elements with nonnegative valuation and 0. Vanishing scheme, given scheme and global section s in Sect(Y, O_Y), vanishing scheme is closed subscheme. Variety, affine scheme reduced of finite type over field k (need not be algebraically closed). Vector bundle, locally free sheaf. Vector bundle istop space with map to X with fibres vector spaces, continuously varying. Veronese, given P^n and very ample line bundle O(D), have embedding P^n -> P^N, Veronese embdding. N = h^0(P^n, O(d)) - 1 or (n+d Choose n) - 1. Elliptic curve intuition is a smooth projective curve of genus 1, it looks like a torus of C/L as a complex manifold. An elliptic curve (over a field k) is a smooth projective curve of genus 1 (defined over a field k) with a distinguished (k-rational) point, IMPORTANT, it must at least have one rational point. The projective plane is the set P^2(k) of all nonzero tripples, modulo relation (x,y,z) equiv (lambda x, ...), affine points, there are points at infinity. Examples of plane projective curves of Q. Plane projective curve homogrous poly with coefficients in k, degree of C_f is degree of f(x,y,z). K-rational points of C_f form the set, point is singular if all first derivatives vanish at P. C_F is smooth if there are no singular points. The number of handles is a genus, genus can be defined algebraically over any fiedl, not just in Riemann spheres and C. Newton polytop of a polynomial is the convex hull of the set. Weierstrass equations, 4A^3 + 27B^2 nonzero, char(k) not equal 2 or 3, this was the example that John Tate gave. Rational points in genus 0. three points on a line sum to zero, this is the elliptic curve group law, the set becomes an abelian group, point (0: 1: 0) at infinity is the identity 0, points have inverses (x : y : z) and (x : -y : z). Commutativity if obvious and associativity holds, P + Q = R is purely algebraically, 2P := P + P, 3P := P + P + P, can do scalar multiplication, it is a Z-module, or homomorphism from Z to End(M). Pick k = C, E(C) iso to C/L or addition of complex numbers modulo lattice, k = Q more interesting Mordell's theorem shows that it is finitely generated E(Q) is isomorphic to T oplus Z^r (this is very important). torsion group of E(Q) is well understood, isomorphic to either Z/nZ or Z/2Z oplus Z/2mZ. Rank of E(Q) not well understood, largest r known for r >= 29, bound on rank using arithmetic statistics, average rank of all elliptic curves over Q is less than 1. E(F_p) is necesssarily finite, cardinality by Hasse, # E(F_p) = p + 1 - t. E/Q be an elliptic curve defined by y^2 = x^3 + Ax + B, p be prime that does not divide discriminant delta(E) = -16(4A^3 + 27B^2), good reduction. Sato-Tate conjecture, E/Q be elliptic curve WITHOUT COMPLEX MULTIPLICATION, x_p has semi-circular distribution. Birch and Swinnerton Dyer, relationship between infinite sequence of integers a_p associated to elliptic curve E/Q. BSD E/Q rank r, then L(E,s) = (s-1)^r g(s) for complex analytic function g(s) with g(1) != infty, 0. ECM is invented by Lenstra.

Banach Gelfand triples are triples of spaces, a Banach space with norm dense in some Hilbert space which in turn is contained in a dual of B. Define CW complex as some sort of viltration X = union of all filtration elements, X^0 is discrete set, X^n is inductively obtined by a bunch of n-cels X^n = X^n-1 gluing coproduct of D_^n disks, f_a is map S^n-1 -> X^n-1. X has the weak topology of the {X^n}, map out of X is continuous if and only if restrictions to X^n are continuous, X^n is the n-skeleton. Think of it is decompositions. Theorem, any manifold is CW-complex, using R^n as the n-skeleton. RP^n is a CW complex, attach upper hemisphere and glue boundary to RP^n-1, nontrivial CW complex. Riemann spheres can be constructed via CW complexes as well.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/math/0212397, Mike Hopkins on topological modular forms, (1) two ways of studying stable homotopy groups of spheres, first way is to directly inspect maps leading to the Hopf invariant one problem, the second way is to investigate stable homotopy theory, which can be defined using Freudenthal's suspension theorem; (2) to do surgery, Poincare needed to do framed cobordisms; (3) to develop a universal theory of framed cobordism, one needed to study the stable homotopy groups of spheres; (4) to classify Riemann surfaces, Pontraygin basically needed to introduce framed surgery to do it; (5) to understand the splitting of the J-homomorphism, one needed to understand Spin cobordism i.e. Bott periodicity and the 10 fold way; (6) to study the group of cobordism classes, one is led to investigate Thom spectra as well as MO or the unoriented cobordism, MU is the unitary or complex cobordism; (7) to generalise the tensor product of abelian groups, the smash product was invented and looks like some sort of addition; (8) to have a universal theory of Chern classes for complex vector bundles, MU is the universal theory; (9) to approximate a space whose stable or unstable homotopy groups are known, one uses the Adam's spectral sequence; (10) to refine rings to ring spectra, topological modular forms is used to refine the theory of elliptic curves and modular forms, recall from Lurie that the top 5 facts of algebraic geometry, elliptic curves form a group with infinity as an identity; (11) to uniquely find a homotopy fixed point spectrum, one needs to think up a ring of topological modular forms, whose surjectivity of the map is proved by Hopkins-Mahowald; (12) to understand the cell structure of complex projective space, one can compute the cohomology of spectra for CP^\infty valued in Z; (13) to express ideas in terms of spin cobordism, one may use the Atiyah Bott Shapiro map to this; (14) to understand spectra, one needs to study E infty ring spectra; (15) to find the homotopy type of spectra, Bousfield and Kuhn constructed a functor to get a natural equivalence

Can view M as a functor, since it is an invariant! The most general kind of invariants are functors, family of functors keeping in line with nerve of category. Lemma is used to define operads and algebras: Vect-Sigma with compsoition o is monoidal category, not symmetric, unit of this structure is the symmetric sequence (0, k, 0, ...), a monoidal category (Vect-Sigma, o, e), a linear operad is a monoid in this category. Device that digests m input and gives back one output, this can be done withs tring diagram notation. V is vector space, Endomorphism operad is the n-ary part of vector space End(V)(n) = Hom_k(V^otimes n, V). Operad is by insertion of homomorphisms. operad Com has Com(n) = k for all n with trivial Sigma_n-action, iterations of canonical identification k otimes_k k isomorphic to k. G0invariant measure dx on X, example C^\infty is smooth rep of G, L^2 is unitary rep of G, harmonic analysis decomposes reps to irred reps. Circle group, irred modules are 1dim, spanned by functions exp(it) -> exp(int) on torus, n in Z and admits decomposition into Fourier series with basis f_n, for G. = R, again irred unitary reps take f_n from t to exp(ixt) so integral Cf_x d\chi. https://personalpages.manchester.ac.uk/staff/Nicola.Gambino/pssl-101/Blechschmidt.pdf  use internal language of topos, sheaves of modules are treated as plain modules, schemes are sets, reduced rings are Noetherian and in fact fields. Without loss of generality, any reduced ring is Noetherian and a field. Topos has finite limits (continuity), cartesian closed (inner hom prod? exp object), and has a subobject classifier (this is hard). WARNING, subobject classifier does not exist for Top, so Top is not a topos, Set is the category of sets, Sh(X) is set valued sheaves, Zar(S) is big Zariski topos. A topos is a category rich enough to support internal language. A ring is local iff precisely one maximal ideal. BEST DEFINITION OF LOCAL, a homomorphism is local iff it reflects invertibility. Reflective means inclusion under homomorphism has a left adjoint called LOCALISATION, reflectivity works on local objects. Structure sheaf is reification of all the stalks. Transfer principles, structure sheaf inherits all first order properties stable under localisation. Any noninvertible element of structure sheaf is nilpotent. This was the key point for Schur's lemma for me as well. If A is reduced, structure sheaf is reduced, field, anonymously Noetherian in that any ideal is not not finitely generated, has double negation stable equality. Finite limits means I can have continuity, cartesian closed means I have internal hom. Very few categories work as topos, set, Sh(X) and Zar. Transfer principle means structure sheaf ineherits all first properties of A stable under localisations. Uniquely, any non-invertile element is nilpotent.

https://www.mimuw.edu.pl/~gael/xxi/files/slides_posters/Blechschmidt.pdf  internal language of topoi in algebraic geometry on 49 slides. MUST READ Worth reading if you have the time. Topoi, petit and gros Zariski topoi, internal perspective, structure sheaf look slike ordinary local ring, vector bundles look like locally free modules. A topos has finite limits, cartesian closed i.e. internal hom in some sense and a subobject classifier (very hard condition). Formal definition is misleading, comprehensive definition is locally cartesian closed, finitelyc omplete and cocomplete Heyting category which is exact, extensive and has a subobject classifer. Intenrnal point of view turns objects and morphisms of topoi to sets and maps of sets, language of set is the usual mathematical language. Objects of topois feel more like types rather than sets. For each object, tehre is a power object and char function to get membership. Embedding theorem, weaker variant of internal language that works in abelian categories, using it lets us pretend that objects are abelian groups instead of modules, constructing morphisms by appeaking to axiom of unique choice which is a theorem, one does not have to check linearity. Internal langauge of sheaf. Kripk-eJoyal semantis, work for any topoi, finely concerted to make crucial properties of internal language to be true. Two further rules to make internal langauge true, U in True, U = U, U in false means U empty. negation sends it to false. Alternate definition will not be local. Exercises, pen and paper. Simplify the rules. Extend the language, example look at Shulman's stack semantics. Internal language of topoi ahve two crucial properties, locality means if U is union of U_i if U_i has property for each iff U has property. Soundness, means no p and not p, not not not p implies p, and no axiom of choice. Interpretation U has not not (f=0) iff f - 9 holds on dense open susbet. Internal logic of most topoi is constructive. Curry-Howard, constructive truth of formula is equivalence of existence program of certain type associated with the formula, used implicitly in HoTT all the time. Can do synthetic differential geoemtry. Rumours, contradicition is forbidden, but not true, it is just a proof of the weaker statement not not phi. Deny law of excluded middle, not true. One merely does not use the law of excluded middle. Deny and not use is different. Only in concrete models one can refute this i.e. internal inverse of sheaf otopos on non-discrete topological space. petit Zariski topology is category of set valued sheaves, structure sheaf looks like ordinary rings, sheaf of O_X modules looks like ordinary modules. Sheaf of modules, locally of finite type if internally it is finitely gneeraetd, locally finitely free if internally it is a finitely free modules, notions of algebraic geometry are notions of commutative or linear algebra internal to SH(X), F is coherent if internally Sh(X)... build dictionary between external and internal notion. Prove the equivalence once, use it arbitrarily often. Therefore the notion of a petit topos is useful. SES of O_X modules, if F' is F'' locally of finite tyep so is F, reduce to constrcutive linear algebra, SES of modules if M' and M'' if fg, then so is M. Need elimination rule for exisstential quantifier. Idea, basic statements of algebraic geometry as intenral language of petit Zariski topos are more conceptual. Sheaf of rational functions, K_X si total quotient ring of O_X. Tensor product of O_X modules is sheafification of presheaf of global sections, reduce to usual tensor product of ordinary modules.  Exterior powers can be treated, define and work with Koszul complex. O_X is local ring for sheaf, O_X is almost a field x nilpotent, scheme is reduced if internally it is a reduced ring iff O_X fulfils the field condition. Be careful, field bifurcates to other notions. Rank of sheaf of modules, X reduced, F is locally free if rank is locally constant, F is locally free on dense open subset. Let M be fg module over a field, M is free iff number above i.e. rank is ordinary natural number. M is always not not free. Locally free on dense open, changes to not not. So density has interpretation as double negation. Important hard exercise 13.7L. Quasiompactness is not a lcoal condition, but has consequences on the meta properties of the internal language, similar. Directed set and idrected family of O_X modules, F := colim F_i locally of finite type, X quasicompact, exist index i in I such that canonical map F_i -> F is epimorphism, constructive linear algebra, directed set directed family of O_x modules, if M := colim_i M_i is fg, holds, surjective. Char of quasicompactness and check that F_i -> F is surjective is indeed monotone. Gros Zariski topos, Sh(Sch/S), functor opf points (Sch.S)^op -> Set, S-scheme X functor of points is object of Zar(S), feels like set of points of X, internally affine line looks like a field. Working in Zar(S) amounts to incorporating philosophy of describing schemes by their functor of points. Affine line fulfils axiom every function from affine line to affine line is polynomial for Zar(S). Group schemes, internal definition is affine line as additive group, G_m is affine line x invertible, mu_n is x^n = 1 GL_n is M in A^1^nxn, M invertible. Group schemes look like ordinary groups in gros topos, there is also the functor of points dictionary in page 40. Open immersions, decidable if p or not p holds, double negation stable if double negation of formula implies truth holds, morphism is open immersion if p is injective and x in image of p is open. Section internally understood as set of all maps to affine line, global sections of inner hom object Hom(X, A_s) corresponds to global sections of O_X. Open problems is toe xpand this dictionary. Math is internal to topos technical device for simplifying routine proof, exploring exciting alternative universe. Okay there is one subtle point, why is double negation required for density, negation only maps U to the interior Int(U^c), souble negation maps to Int(bar U), this is all of X iff U is dense. Op(X) must be interpreted as Heyting algebra proof by universal property of W in S, S in X arbitrary, then W in S iff W in Int(S), since S in X arbitrary, this is universal property.

https://www.speicherleck.de/iblech/stuff/algar-lecture-notes.pdf  curious world of constructive math, in 36 pages, basically double negation may fail to imply the original statements, this is related to negation mapping a set to its interior complement rather than the complement outright for topological space. Also, Heyting algebra admits an embedding into Op(X) as a category. Construction is related to the boundary of the topological space, if boundary is nontrivial then constructive and unconstructive are not the same. This sort of reminds me that the boundary of a boundary is zero for homology and cohomology. Principle of double negation elimination, the condition for this to work is dense. Sheaves for the dense topology is precisely the double negation sheaves.

http://web.science.mq.edu.au/~street/CT90Como.pdf  Joyal Street Tannakian, recall classical Pontryagin duality, character is continuous homomorphism from G -> T, multiplicative group of modulus 1, G^dual given topology of uniform convergence of compact subsets, G^dual is locally compact, with inner product acting as inner hom from G^dual * G -> T (looks like inner hom?) with canonical homomorphism of double duals, Pontrjagin duality states this homomorphism is isomorphsim of topological groups. Fourier transform can be defined as F : L^1(G) cap L^2(G) -> L^2(G^dual), with integral f(x) <s, x> dx. Plancherel's theorem, with correct normalisation of Haar measure on G^dual f to Ff extends uniquely to an isometry, measure associated to dx, so that total mass of G = 1. Note, failure of general duallity for noncommutative locally compact groups, simple algebraic groups, duality fails badly.

Category of cocycles, objects are all pairs of maps in span, morphism is commutative diagram mapping cones. Prototype example is Galois theory. total space x_G Sp(L) to point and BHH is cocycle on Galois group in algebraic group H in traditional sense. Cocycle category appear as hammock localisations. Cocycle category has case of span with cone X x I, projection to X, and homotopy to Y. This is a generalisaition. Homotopy classification of torsors. Verdier hypercovering theorem, Y is locally fibrant, inclusion h_pi(X,Y) of subcategory of cocycle category, objects of cocycles with projections as hypercovers as local fibration and local weak equivalence in h(X,Y), then Bh_pi(X,Y) is weakly equivalent to Bh(X,Y) for classifying. Corollary is Verdier hypercovering theorem, Y locally fibrant direct limit of pi(U,Y) -> [X,Y] is bijection.

Example L/k finite Galois extension, Galois group G, Sp(L) -> Sp(K) is covering for etale otpology, iso of simplicial sheaves C(Sp(L)) iso EG x_G Sp(L) = B(E_G Sp(L)) for etale topology, sheaf of groupoids E_G Sp(L) is Galois groupoid for L/j, map EG x Sp(L) is local equivalence for simplicial sheaves on etale site, Sp(k) represents point on their site, where is the geometric morphisms? Time honoured method, Quillen's model category construction, formally invert local weak equivalences. Cocycles, construct elements [X,Y] or morphisms from X to Y in any homotopy category, construct for simplicial sheaves, give picture of cocycle X <- U -> Y, with projection g and f, g to X local weak equivalence, g is inverted, so (g,f) -> f g^{-1} defines function from pictures to elements [X,Y] i.e. cocycles are elements of [X,Y]. Produce cocycles (g,f) as objects, diagrams of local equivalences between spans of U and U' are morphisms h[X,Y], this the cocycle category. Morphisms in homotopy category Ho(Cat) are isomorphism classes of anafunctors, motivating cycle is cocycle associated to prinicpal G-bundle. Not surprising, just need composition of cocycles use pullbacks in ordinary category of spans, need pullbacks of any weak equivalence to be a weak equivalences.

https://math.ucr.edu/home/baez/cohomology.pdf  Baez lectures on Galois theory, study k sit in big field extension, track symmetries of K that fix k, so Gal(K/k) in Aut(K). Generally can be applied to not just Galois groups, just need some arbitrary extensions. Turn Galois group into the group of decked transformations and branched covers. Generalised it to fibrations of n-categories, classifying fibrations using the principle of tracking symmetries of K that fix k to study k in K so that Gal(K/k) in Aut(k) will lead to cohomology. Nice connected space has connected covering space that covers all others, the biggest deck transformation group or pi_1(B), Galois theory means connected covering spaces of Bis classified by subgroups H in pi_1(B). To reverse this, the Eilenberg-Maclane space is invented as the best space with G as the fundamental group, feels like inverse Galois somehow.

http://www.stat.ucla.edu/~ywu/2025cohomology.pdf  simplest introduction to Hochschild cohomology cohomology points on unit sphere, find fix points on self maps, least number of contractible opens, experimetns, measurements, data storage, or corrections. Endow stored data with extra structure. Cocycles appear in nature as deformations of algebra, consider A[eps]/[eps^2] a new product a * b = ab +f(a,b) eps, this is deformation theory, for this algebra to be associative, the cocycle condition must hold, i.e. a f(b,c) - f(ab,c) + f(a,bc) - f(a,b)c = 0. Z^2(A) is collection of coycles, set of infinitesimal deformation to first oder on Z^2(A) are bijective, two different cocycles can give equivlaent deformations, cocycle is trivial if it is of the form f(a,b) = ag(b) - g(ab) + g(a)b
b equal to trivial one where a*b = ab, B^2(A) is space of such cocycles, this is the coboundaries, one can derive the Hochschild cohomolog HH^2(A) = Z^2(A)/B^2(A). Replace explicit cocycles with elements of kernel in d^2 maps, then define cochain complex, and coboundary maps.

https://math.mit.edu/~lguth/polynomialmethbeamer.pdf  polynomial methods in 37 slides, need to read this again. Idea comes from algorithms about solving polynomials. Take points in vector space, find polynomial of lowest possible degree that vanishes the points, use this polynomial to attack the problem. Let F be a field, let p_i be points, nonzero poly that vanishes it, reduced to linear algebra. Nonzero poly degree of degree d that vanishes at all points if and only if the evaluation map (recall this is similar to nullstellsatz) has nontrivial kernel (representabilty of points) i.e. kernels represent evaluation of points, by first isomorphism theorem, recall that in Schur's kernel is subspace, so this is similar. We can efficiently find a poly that vanishes at each p_j. Nikodym conjecture in finite fields was solved with the polynomial method. See Dvir's paper. Space of polynomials is alrge, d^3 for V(d), vanishing lemma or behaviour of polynomials along a line is rather restricted. The basic case is where n = 2, evaluation map give  V(1) = {ax_1 + bx_2 + c, a,b,c in field}. Lemma, nonzero poly in 1 variable has almost d zeroes, this is fundamental theorem of algebra as asymptotic case, and reduce to basic case over nonalgebraically closed fields. If P and Q are polys of degree d in one variable, either P(x) = Q(x) for <= d values of x or P = Q, compute P_1 - P_2, either <= d zeroes or equal 0 by lemma. The interesting lemma is this on slide 9. Example for correupted data, begin with problem of some points, F agrees with P for 51% or 1$ of values of x, find, P, graph of F and G in C^2, C a field, find poly of low degree that vanishes this point, zero set of Q contains the graph of P, y - P(x) divides Q(x,y). Finite field Nikodym conjecture is Kakeya problem, each Nikodym set in F^n has at least c_n q^n elements. Slide 33 give finite field Nikodym problem solution. Polynomial method is like algebraic geometry. Key facts about polynomials, V(d) has d^3, if p_1... in F^3, exists nonzero poly that vanishes on all th epoints, deg < s^1/3. Poly of degree <= d that vanishes at d+1 points of a line, then P vanishes on L, this vanishing lemma can be proved by checking that the a polynomial has data of d, in a sense, polynomials are an upgrade or a generating function for discrete finite bounded sequences. This last slide was very insightful.

https://danielnaylor.uk/notes/III/Michaelmas/AC/AC.pdf  additive combinatorics in 43 pages, sumset is defined as sum of two elements ina belian group, difference set, triangle inequality or a convexity argument shows that max{|A|,|B|} <= |A+B| <= |A||B| for A and B finite, Hard to drop norm notation even if it is obvious, example |A+A| = 2|A| - 1, the inequality is strict |A+A| >= 2|A|-1, equality only holds in arithmetic progressions. Work in finite field then this statement becomes |A+B| >= 1+p. Hard to see, Cauchy-Davenport is more interesting |A+B| >= min(p, |A|+|B|-1) if A and B are contained in a common finite field or Z/pZ. Induction, assume |A| + |B| <= p+1, induction, base case trivial, use extension by last element to do a comparison over A, B' = B - ma for induction, some sort of comparison that I do not understand at first glance. The important thing is that Cauchy-Davenport only works when you have subset of finite field, not for general abelian groups or even general cyclic groups, I am no wondering where is the fact it is prime is used. Rusza distance is defined for Rusza's triangle inequality log |A-B|/root(|A||B|), some sort of convexity going on d(a,b) <= d(A,B) + d(B,C), prove by writing each d in A-C as d = a_d - c_d, produce an injective map and check against definition, doubling constant is A+A/A. Difference constant is A-A/A. lA - mA is common notation. Plunnecke's inequality, this can be proven by elementary methods using Gowers, |lB - mB| <= K^l+m |A|, this looks like some sort of convexity bound in analysis that I cannot remember, with condition |A+B| <= K|A|, for some K >= 1,  Rusza's trinagle inequality is used in the proof, not sure what is the significance of this here or what is the significance of Plunnecke's inequality. Frieman-Ruzsa, A in F_p^n, |A+A| <= K|A| implies A is contained in subspace in GF(p)^n with size of |H| <= K^2 p^K^4 |A|. Entropy methods in combinatorics is another big topic for this. Additive energy, analogy from Tao is number of additive quadraples (a+b) = (a'+b') i.e. quadraples that satisfy this definition.

https://www.math.cmu.edu/~af1p/Teaching/AdditiveCombinatorics/Tao.pdf  Terence Tao online notes on additive combinatorics, worth reading since it is a short tour, 118 pages, I only wrote down insights.  Question if closed under addition is it closed under subtraction? Closed under iterate3d addition? Kakeya is about how well various line segments pointing in different directions can overlap. By convexity of line, (a+b)/2 lies in the line segment, (a-b)/2 tells you the ddirection, Kakeya, partial sumset is small but corresponding partial difference set is large. Sum sets are related to Kakeya. Fourier transform appears, if f is supported on set A, g is supported on B, then f * g is supported on a + B f * bar(g) is supported on A - B, so Fourier analysis is needed to analyse convolutions. Three aspects of Fourier transform, analysis which is Plancherel, Hausdorff-Young, Littlewood-Paley, Sobolev and uncertainty principle, most of which is basically convex sums of exponentials, algebraic site which is basically representation theory, group actions are homomorphisms to permutation group, characters is Yoneda lemma, arithmetic site, Fourier transform measures how well a set  is closed under additionor subtraction. Arithmetic progressions are level sets of characters in Fourier transforms, and Poisson summation formula, this is how arithmetic progressions are related to the Fourier transform. Frieman isomorphism looks like actual isomorphisms except at most k additions, where is category theory? Z is vector space over rationals using tensor product i.e. bsae change. Cauchy-Davenport inequality, this is an important inequality, for A and B finite field, |A+B| >= min(|A| + |B| - 1, p), trivial case use Pigeonhole, other case use suppose we have counterexample, use Dyson e-transform, inclusion and exclusion. Minimal counterexample, then use translate. There is another one by Elon, Nathanson and Rusza that uses some sort of weird algebraic geometry of monomials, suppose for contradiction it failed. Cauchy-Davenport can be analysed with the Fourier transform, taking isometry from l^2(F) to l^2(F). Distinct frequencies, make one Fourier coefficient equal to 1, |A| - 1 other coefficients equal to 0. Plunnecke's thoerem, A and B subset of abelian group G, suppose |A+B| bounded by K|A| for real K. Deal behind this theorem if passage from A to A+B magnifies cardinality by K, then A to A+B+B should magnify at most by K^2, intuition from graph theory. Plunnecke graph, but Gowers has an elementary proof. Pullback map of edge pushforward map. Tao gives proof by Menger's theorem, consider two subsets of directed graph, maxflow (A -> B, G), max number of disjoint paths in G which connects vertex in A to vertex in B. Min cut means number of vertices needed to remove in order to disconnect A from B. Intuitive interpretation, only obstructions from A to B are cuts which disconnect A from B. Use induction, construct disjoint paths. Then consider the case of min cut. Magnification ratio. Plunnecke's theorem A is essentiallly B invariant, some subset A' that is B+B-invariant, a. could be much smaller than A, then Corollary 7.1 has a bound. Iterating Plunnecke's theorem is powerful. Sumset estimates, closure under + or 0 implies closure under other operation, and under iterates of operations as basic principle. Maximal disjoint family is an importnat tricl. A subgroup, B union of cosets of A so |A+B| = |B|, X simply selects a single element from each coset in side B, X will have cardinality |B|/|A|, generalises algebraic notion of taking quotient by subgroup, turns each coset into a single element (cost representative)? Even if a set A has lots of holes, missing half the elements of the interval, A - A is much better behave, almost no holes, cover things using A instead of A-A, loses log factor O(log N) translates. Frieman's theorem. Exploiting of pigeonhole principle in long monotone sequences, point is long monotone seqeunce must be close to a constant at some point of the sequence. Freiman's theorem, proper generalised AP are only sets essentially closed under addition in Thm 3.1. B-invariant set if |A+B| = |A|, A must has at least as many elements as B. B-invariant sets are unions of some subgroup G generated by translate of B. G must be larger than or equal to |B| but less than or equal to |A|, we can weakn this to essenntially B-invariant by |A+B| congruent to |A|. Plunnecke's theorem, A, B be FINITE nonempty subsets of an abelian group Z, suppose that |A+B| <= K|A| for some real number K>=1 then there is a nonempty subset A' of A such that |A' + B + B| <= K^2|A|, useful if tpassage from A to A+B magnifies card by K, A to A+B+B' should magnify card by at most K^2. Graph theory commutative graph with commuting square property needs to be defined.  Menger's theore, maxflow(A->B;G) = mincut(A-> B; G), covering one set by another, use closure. Compare cardinalities. Rusza's quotient lemma, A and B finiet, cover B by at most |A+B|/|A| translates of A - A. Proof is by thinking about greedy algorithm and maximum disjoint family trick.
