https://www.math.cmu.edu/~af1p/Teaching/AdditiveCombinatorics/gowers.pdf  Szemeredi's regularity lemma, given a graph G and vertex set V, regularity lemma give partition of V, for almost every pair, induced bipartite graphs behaves like a typical randomg raph of same density,  Rusza and Szemeredi deduced Roth's lemma from this regularity lemma. One needs a regularity lemma and a counting lemma. Main results are in section 6 and 8. Almost all proofs are second moment method i.e. Cauchy-Schwarz.

https://www.math.cmu.edu/~af1p/Teaching/Combinatorics/Slides/Basic-Counting.pdf  basic counting, number of mappings from n to m, give m^n, this has category theory consequences. Prove by induction. 1-1 correspondence between two finite sets, cardinality is equal by Schroder-Bernstein, proof by characteristic function or A replaced by f_A via indicator function, this is the Yoneda philosophy. Sets are naturally propositional logic, that is why 2^n appears in the exponent. In general Heyting algebras this power set conclusion fails badly. Number of odd subsets, number of even subsets = 2^n-1, proof by characteristic function union with singleton. Number of 1-1 mappings is product of m-i, from i = 0 to i = n 0 1. m < 1, get 0, m >= 1, use induction on n, get recurrence relation, form a product. Also count number of length n ordered sequences of distinct elements, permutations of [n], give n!. Prove |X choose k| = |X| choose k, proof is by 1-1 mappings from n to k, the idea is that this is a form of Yoneda, consider case k!|(X k)|, this is precisely phi_1-1(n,k), so the proof is that 1 to 1 mappings correspond to the numerator in some sense. S(m,n) is number of parititions in i_k such that sum of i_k = m, cardinality proof as imagining (m + n - 1) points in a line, choose partitions and which are points, colour them red, p0 = 0, p_n = m+1, gap sizes follow inductive formula form a seuqence in S(m,n), number of ways of colouring m indistinguishable balls using n colours. Colours are number of coloured balls as i_k, sum of them must equal to tal number of balls m. So from m balls + n colours - 1, choose n-1 colours (recall that there is already a colour.) Separating 1s on a cycle, not sure why change it to n-k-1 choose k-1 * n/k. Symmetry of Pascal's triangle gives n choose r = n choose n-r, proof by choosing r elements to include is equivalent to choosing n-r to exclude. Pascals triangle means (n choose k) + (n choose k+1) = (n+1 choose k_1), k+1 subset includes n+1 choices, or does not, so n choose k+1 choices. Theorem follows by induction on Pascal's triangle, the combinatorial argument is to select partitions. Vandermodes's identity gives sum (m r)(n k-r) = (m+n k), use case analysis of partitions similar to the two case proof. binomial theorem, expansion of (1+x)^n gives binomial coefficients, coeff x^r in (1+x)... chose x from r brackets, 1 from the rest, for each r. Binomial theorem, x = 1 give power set 2^n, x = -1 means Euler char on n choose k = 0. Then prove number of subsets of even cardinality = number of subsets of odd cardinality by composition of two cases. Differentiate both sides of binomial theorem yields stronger formula, see Zakharevich solution for other problem. Grid path problems, monotone paths are made up of segments. Drop (a,b), starting as (0,0) as universal affine point, actually it looks like some sort of counting of various partitions in L1 space, this gives PATHS(a,b) = (a+b a) as some sort of partition problem. If we fix a point finding every path that goes through (0, 1), then by combinatorics, delete bad events and ignore paths that do not go through a,b, get b-a/(a+b) (a+b a) by case analysis. Use existence of diagonal lines. Do not understand Catalan numbers from grid point problems, it drops out when considering bijective paths, but why? Multiset permutation. This uses the fact that representation theory of x -> exp(lambda x) is invertible, and increasing so {X >= eps} equivalent to {exp(lambda X) >= exp(lambda x)}.

https://www.math.cmu.edu/~af1p/Teaching/Combinatorics/Slides/Notes/Graph-Theory.pdf  graph theory in 83 slides, set of slides is good for definitions, graphs consists of vertices and edges, digraph hve direction, use vectors instead of edges. Eulerian graphs are graphs where only two vertices have an odd number of edges connecting to it? Bipartite graphs, V = X union Y, every edge is of the form (x,y), this is an easy definition. Vertex colouring, it is a map to valued in a set of colours (recall group action is group hom to permutations), v is in V gets coloured with f(v), colouring is proper if edge (a,b) in E implies f(a) not f(b) i.e. all have different colours, the chromatic number is the minimum number of colours in a proper colouring. Subgraphs are V' in V and E' in E, G' is a spanning subgraph if V' = V. There is also subgraph induced by V' i.e. pick those edges for vertices only in V'. Graph isomorphisms if there is a bijection of vertices such that edges are preserved under the bijection. Complete graph is the graph of n vertices with all edges filled in, K_m,n is the complete bipartitite graph of m+n vertices (you can always form bipartite graph?). Vertex degrees, number of edges incident with v, minimum and maximum degree globally can always be defines. Graphs admit a representation theory of permutation matrices called incidence matrices, can use representation theory to solve problems. Alternative representation is adjacency matrices. Theorem is sum of degrees of vertices is 2 times the number of edges, this is obvious because it goes on or goes out, if you have an edge it must connect two points as the basic property of graphs. The number of vertices of odd degree is even, this is shown since you need to produce 2|E| so consider subtraction by vertices of even degree to prove this. Paths are walsk with distinct vertices. Walk is closed if v_1 = v_k, a cycle is a closed walk in which each vertex is distinct except for the initial/last, closed walk means you can repeat vertex. M_k = A^k, what is this notation for M_k(v,w) in the theorem? Very confusing, slide 19. Conencted components, equivalence class a equivalent to b if there is a walk from a to b. This is the basic idea of simple graph homology, or the zeroth homology group. Breadth first search, stuck at slide 24.

https://www.ias.edu/sites/default/files/video/morse%20lecture%201.pdf, MUST READ graph regularity method by Jacob Fox in 125 slides, definition of graph, Szemeredi's regularity lemma, roughly speaking, every graph can be partitioned into bounded number of roughly equal size part so the graph is random like. Rough structural result. Most powerful tool in combinatorics. Regularity and density need to be defined essentially. Vertex subsets of a graph, e(X,Y) is number of pairs that are edges., density is defined as d(X,Y) = e(X,Y)/|X||Y|, so divide by number of vertices. Irregularity is the maximum over A in X and B in Y of |e(A,B) - d(X,Y)|A||B||. One can then define epislon-regularity via a scaling inequality, irreg(X,Y) <= eps|X||Y|. Partition irregularity, define for subsets of vertex parittions irregularities as the sum of irregularities between (X,Y). Why is this a good definition fo regularity? Szemeredi's regularity lemma, for every epsilon > 0, there is a M(epsilon) so that every graph has an epsilon-regular vertex partition, with at most M(epsilon) parts. Why is this a natural definition? Tower function, T(1) = 2, T(n) = 2^T(n-1), so it is towers of 2. Regularity lemma gives M(eps) < T(eps^-2). Gowers proved that it is arbitrary small, -c for some constant c > 0. That is quite crazy. Lovasz and others proved M(eps) = T(O(eps^-2)). The proof idea uses mean square density.

https://yufeizhao.com/211/graph_theory_notes.pdf, basic graph theory, graph has vertices and edges unordered, loop is edge on same vertex, edge is multiedge if it appears multiple times, simple if no loops or multiple edges, symmetric relation between objects gives a graph, this is why graph theory can be "first isomorphism" identified, take symmetric relation and impose it, the kernel of the map is a graph. Isomorphism of graphs if there is a bijection of vertices and a preserving of graphs in the edge next to it. Isomorphisms are equivlaence relations of graphs, one can form a group/groupoid or whatever of isomorphisms. Representation theory of graphs come in two kinds, adjaency and incidence matrices. Degree of graph is number of neighbours of vertices, vertex is isolated with degree 0. A graph with an isolated vertex can be proven to be disconnected.

https://people.engr.tamu.edu/andreas-klappenecker/csce658-s18/lovasz.pdf, Lovascz local lemma explained in 52 slides in 30 slides, the proof requires PENANDPAPER but the key idea of obtaining the condition 4pd < 1 through a bound is clear for the symmetric version, main idea is to prove the existence of a combinatorial object with certain properties, define sample space and show randomly chosen element of this space has the desired properties with positive probability. Sieve methods come into play, use union bound, the probability of intersection of complements is nonzero, this is done by appealing to definition of duality of union and intersection, as well as complement, this gives rise to the bad events. Independence Sieve can then be used if you have independence mutually and all the e events are bad, intersection formula degenerates to a product formula, actually union bound there is an intersection bound Pr(A and B) <= Pr(A) Pr(B), equal if independent? Actually we can do much better Pr(A and B) <= min(Pr(A), Pr(B)), the union bound means we nee da max i.e. Pr(A or B) <= max(Pr(A) + Pr(B))? Anyways independence sieve, all events mutually independent is very strong requirement. It comes down to thinking about conditional inequalities. Then a dependencey graph iff B_k is mutually independent of all the events B_l such that k,l is not an edge of G, so nonexistence of edge guarantees mutual independence. Lovasz local lemma states, dependence graphs, if deg(k) <= d, Pr[B_k] <= p and 4 * d * p < 1, why 4 that is strange, so 4 and degree of vertex or event is less than d, p is probability or the bound of this, and less than 1, then the Probability(all bad happening) > 0. Proof in about 4 slides, main idea is to use induction on condition probability of Pr[B_k | intersection of B_j^c, j in S] <= 2p. Symmetric is used, renumber index set, bound the numerator by conditional bound killing off the intersection condition with all other B_i^c, then by definition it is equal to Pr[B_n] so <= p by hypothesis using mutual independence. Lower bound the denominator essentually by the same argument. Put it all together,  so it is 2p. The number 4 appears when we need to lower bound the denominator, essentially the conditions come from the proof itself, use induction to show positivity. Used in Ramsey theory, used in edge disjoint paths. Please read the main uses of the technique. Essentially conditional probability, having to bound upper and lower in the proof, and 4dp < 1 is the essential bound, probability all bad happen positive once all conditions are met. The bound number of 4 is the unusual one. Very convenient since can drop independence if you do some counting.

https://math.gmu.edu/~rsachs/talks/maastirlingbeamer.pdf, Stirling's formula is meant to approxuimate log n! bound by integral of int log x dx from n+1 to n, then get n!, this is done by lower bound and upper bound integrals, this looks like root(2 pi n) (n/e)^n, I watched Gower's lecture for this. Root n will only appear if you use trapezoidal rule. (n n/2) = n! / (n/2)! (n/2)!, ditch Stirling's write it out as (n n/2)/2^n, the 2^-n trick is good for cancellations. https://www.sisask.com/Shillong/DFA_2.pdf, I did not read this set of notes, just dumping a Gowers lecture, a character is a multiplicative homomorphism to C. If chi  is a character |chi(*)| = 1, homomorphism to smaller group T, complex numbers of modulus 1.  Pointwise multiplication and inverse are also characters, complex conjugation pointwise also stays within T. Sending x to 1, trivial homomorphism is also a character. They show that the characters form themselves a ABELIAN group. Characters are orthogonal to each other. Orthogonal needs to be defined. Inner product is expect valued of f(x) and conjugate g(x) if chi_1 and chi_2 are distinct characters, the inner product has trivial inner product. Inner products can be defined with sums. To prove that distinct nontrivial characters have trivial inner product, find a u in G such that chi(u) is not equal to 1. There is a trick used for summing geometric progressions, the average over all x for chi(x) is the same as (note that we are using naturally endowed Haar mesure, locally compact), expected chi(x) = expected chi(u+x) because chi is a character and wlog chi(u) is nonzero, so can take out chi(u) expectation chi(x), implies expectation of chi(x) = 0 since chi(u) is nonzero without loss of generality. chi_1 conj(chi_2) = 0 if distinct and nontrivial, the result follows, inner product chi_1 chi_2. Gowers used the horrible notation Z/nZ, tkae group as product of finite cyclic groups of order n, if the group is that.

https://www.mathematik.uni-muenchen.de/~wehler/Talk_Summary.pdf  harmonic theory in 17 slides on Riemann surfaces, compact Riemann surface, harmonic m forms are kernel of map from H^(X, eps) -> H^0(X, eps) from 0 to m to 2, kernel of Laplace operator where delta is eps^j+1 -> eps^j, interested in case m = 1, not sure what the notation means but essentially harmonic m forms are the kernel of the Laplace operator. Main result is to prove that de Rham-Hodge H^= iso to Harm^=, and de Rham Hodge Dolbeault decomposition. This gives corollary as a Riemann-Roch formula. Complex manifold with Hermitian metric h, *-operator is a C-antilinear isomorphism defined by characteristic equation. On a compact Riemann surface, show that the kernel of the map d admits a decomposition into dH^0 oplus Harm1. Use Hodge decomposition on H0. de Rham Hodge generalises to higher dimension for all smooth manifolds, de Rham Hodge Dolbeault decomposition generalises to higher dimensions only for Kahler manifolds using the Kahler identities, on compact Kahler, Betti numbers and Hodge numbers are finite, can write out Hodge diamond. Laplacian is self adjoint, nonnegative operator that acts as a smoothness or flatness indicator, being in the kernel means the form is closed and coclosed allowing it to represent the topological cohomology classes of a smooth manifold.

Variety X over a number field K, want to know X(K) = empty, if A_K is the ring of K-adeles, number fields are global fields.We can have X(A_K) empty implies X(K) empty. This is the main point of the adelic language, it is necessary to describe schemes as per what Connes said. Hasse-Minkowski: if X in P^n is a hypersurface given by one quadratic equation then X(A) nonzero implies X(K) nonzero i.e. quadrics satisfy the local to global principle, the adeles are a locally compact ring. Global fields sit inside the locally compact ring in a way that is discrete and cocompact (i.e. quotienting the adeles by the action of the global field yields a compact space). Recall that the adeles is a form of restricted direct products of the completion of valuation rings. Not all varieties satisfy the local to global principle. There is a natural pairing X(A) x Br X -> Q/Z, such that X(K) in X(A)^Br in X(A). Skorobogatov however defined other obstructions due to torsors. https://runegha.folk.ntnu.no/iopd.pdf  Hauseng notes on operads, infinity operads are homotopy coherent algebraic structures. First is A_\infty spaces of Stasheff who invented associahedra. Multiplicative structure occurs on loop spaces. Iterated loop spaces  lead to E_n spaces, by Boardman-Voight. Operads were itnroduced by May to organise homotopy coherence data. Care more about homotopy coherent versions of fmailiar structures like associative or commutative algebras. E_n algebras are an exception but these are iterated associative algebras. Infinity-operads are merely a language. Colored operads, symmetric multicategories. Operad is for one object case. Operads is like category, morphisms in operad has a list of objects as their source. Operad is set of objects for all lists of objects, a set of Hom(Y,x) multimorphisms, composition of multiorphisms, identity, permutation symmetry such that composition is associative unital and compatible with symmetric operations. Permutation is painful to work with. Examples, commutative operad, single object, Comm(n) has a single element for all nonnegative integers. Associative operads, single object, set of orderings with free symm group action, composition is concat of orderings. CM and BM operad do not understand. Operads can arise by category of operators, this means operads can work by defining it using ordinary categories, F category of finite sets, F* category of pointed finite set, use skeleton <n>, F* is object finite set, morphism given by span, backwards map f must be injective. Operad, category of operators is category with functor to pointed finite sets, objects are lists, morphisms are single morphism of skeleton and a multimorphism from lists of objects to single object in operad, composition comes from composition in operad. So operads are functors from category of operators valued in pointed finite sets. Feels very like functor of categories, functor of points. Mapping space of objects form a functor, transposes to fully faithful Yoneda embeding P(C) := Fun(C^op, S), infinity category of presheaves on C. Straightening theorem of infinity categories is important. Then with straightening theorem, an infinity-operad is simply a functor of infinity categories from p: O to F_* that O has p-cocartesian lifts of inert morphisms in F_*, a commutative sqaure of mapping spaces to products of mapping spaces commute with homsets of skeletons is a pullback square, exists object in O_<n> with cocartesian morphisms. The biggest payoff of monads for me is this definition: a LAX SYMMETRIC MONOIDAL FUNCTOR between symmetric monoidal category is simply a morphism of infinity operads between them preserving inert morphisms, if preserve all cocart, it si a full symmetric monoidal functor. Inert + active maps form factorisation system. Important to study inert maps. Stuck at page 21.

https://alainconnes.org/wp-content/uploads/JAG2020-1.pdf  algebraic geometry summary by Connes, algebraic geometry for Segal’s Gamma rings. New Gamma ring is stalk of structure sheaf of Arakelov compactification of Spec Z, promoted a theory of schemes of finite type over F1 using the category MR obtained by gluing together the category of commutative pointed monoids with the category of commutative rings. Recall that Arakelov geometry gives a technique for compactifying Spec(Z) into. Complete space with prime lying at infinity. This process implements adjoint pair relating Mo to Ring. Let S be sphere spectrum, or the smallest Gamma-Ring. S-modules or Gamma-sets are covariant functors from Gamma^op to Sets_* between pointed categories, where Gamma^op is the small full subcategory of the category of finite pointed sets, whose objects are pointed sets, the morphisms in the category S-mod of S-modules are natural transformations. S-mod is a closed symmetric monoidal category. Develop theory of schemes of finite type over F_1 by implementing category, follows idea from Cartier, MR defines a framework which definition of variety over F_1 is applied to a covariant functor MR to Sets in the category of sets. One wants a topos Root(M), by introducing a category canonically associated to a commutative pointed monoid, topos Root(M) is dual to this category, then it will be used for the construction of the Grothendieck site spectrum of arbitrary S-algebra and encoding localisation. C(M) is first defined using relative morphisms, then Root(M) is defined as C(M)^ or the topos of contravariant functors from C(M) to the category of sets, where C(M) is the small category with one object from any element f in M, for M in object of Mo. Recall that Mo is the category of commutative monoids. Pick A a commutative S-algebra, then there is an interesting Grothendieck topology on C(A(1_+)), one can construct the spectrum of arbitrary S-algebra, localise the category of C(A(1_+)) by inverting all morphisms of the form of f^k in … then one can compute the points of Root(Z) involving the Adeles of Q and Connes’ notions of the arithmetic site. Section 5 is algebraic geometry of S, affine case, associate to a commutative S-algebra its spectrum Spec(A) it is a pair of a Grothendieck site and a structure presheaf of S-algebra, pick A = HR, for an ordinary commutative ring, Spec(A) gives classical affine spectrum Spec(R). Operations of sum of two elements can be done by repeatedly applying the associative rule to obtain the sum of n elements, then one defines a partition of unit as n elements whose sum is equal to 1. This is one of the most concrete and interesting definition of partitions of unity. Structure sheaf of spectrum of arbitrary S-algebra, idea is based on in general false that structure presheaf is automatically a sheaf, functionality.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.03888, Lawson's Bousfield localisation, Serre classes of abelian groups that is closed under subobjects, quotients, and extensions, one can ignore groups when’s studying homology and homotopy of simply connected space X by proving mod-C versions of Hurewicz and Whitehead theorems. Techniques for rational homotopy only require rational homology groups. Insight by Bousfield Kan is to use space level upgrades for this. Reinterpret homotopy theory as algebraic invariants, but rather knowledge of its localisation, completions and arithmetic attaching maps. Morava-Ravenel used localisation techniques. Quillen use map from X to X_Q as fibrant replacement, universal property, so Bousfield localisation makes this into a definition. Idea is to have collection of morphisms become equivalence under the smash product. Ordinary categories have a class of maps of weak equivalences, localisation is universal among functors sending maps inW to isomorphism, the new category is unique up to unique isomorphism. The basic example is projection being a weak equivalence, in localisation, homotopic maps are equal, so localisation factors through th ehomotopy cateogry hS. The homotopy category has a large enough special objects, localisation is done by forcibly moving objects into this subcategory. The second example is in th ectegory of nonnegatively grade cochain complexes of modules over a commutative ring, W is a the class of quasiisomorphisms, there is an injective module of complexes as a subcategory The composite functor to the localisation is an equivalence of categories. These examples are worth revisiting laters. Definition of locality, SUPER IMPORTANT: let S be a class of morphisms in A, an object in Y is S-local if for all f : A -> B, the map of homs Hom_A(B,Y) -> Hom_A(A,Y) is a bijection, so it is local in the class of morphisms, since homsets can be formed on the level of domain and codomain of maps f, the L^S(A) is a full subcategory of S-local objects, if there is only one map f (or even a canonical map), then this property is f-local. Special case of localisation is when maps in S map to a terminal object. Let S be a class of morphisms mapping to the terminal object, the localisation is the nullification of the objects, a map is es-equivalent if for all S-local objects Y, the Hom_A(B,Y) -> Hom_A(A,Y) is a bijection, the class of S-equivalences contains S by definition. A map X -> Y is an S-localisation, if it is an S-equivalence and Y is local, and under these conditions we say X has an S-localisations. If all objects in category A have S-localisations, we say A has S-localisations. Example: class of maps f: N -> Z in category of monoids, monoids M is f-local if monoid hom N -> M extends to hom Z -> M, so every element in M has an inverse, f-local monoids are groups, this is the natural transformation to its group completion. Localisation comes from making sure that there is a unique maximal ideal, focusing on a local ring. Localisations are left adjoints to the inclusion of a subcategory. Localisation can also be done using mapping spaces. Invert inclusion of S^n -> D^n+1, to have uniqueness for filling maps from n-spheres, need existence for filling maps from (n+1)-spheres. Small object argument to construct Bousfield localisations.

Strong subadditivity of entropy is less intuitive is less, we have ent[123] + ent[2] <= ent[12] + ent[23]. ent(P) <= log|set(P)|, links P with category of finite set, there is a morphism to it? https://cameroncounts.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2017/03/pgts.pdf  permutation groups, semigroup is set with associative law, monoid is semigroup with identity, group is monoid with inverses. Essentially not much difference between monoids and semigroups. Most interesting groups are ones closest to groups, regular semigroups, semigroups are when axa = a for regular elements. The full transformations emigroup is regular, proof is choose preimage s for every t in the image of a as a map, then define the regular element. Idempotent in semigroup is e^2 = e, if axa = a i.e. regular elements, then ax and xa are idempotents. Idempotents always exist in a finite semigroup. Proof is by first powers of a not all distinct usiing finteness of group, then we have a^m = a^m+r, replace with substitution a^i = a^{i+tr}, give substitution a^i = a^{2i} yield idempotence. An inverse semigroup is not a group.

https://cameroncounts.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/11/groups.pdf  symmetry groups, all elems are bijections, composition of symmetries is a symmetry, identity is a symmetry, inverse of symmetry is a symmetry. Transformation or permutations, closed under composition, identity belongs to G, closed under ivnerse transformations, implicit in third condition, elements of G are bijections. Abstract definition of groups. Cayley's theorem, there is a transformation group isomorphic to G, this is Cayley's theorem. Every group is the automorphism group of a graph. Inverse Galois problem is every finite group is automorphism group of a field which is a finite extension of the rationals. Subgroups written with partial order notation, group in its own right with restricted notation. Cayley's theorem states aby group is isomorphic to a subgroup of Sym(G). Lagrange's theorem is most important, G be finite group H subgroup, the order of H divides the order of G, use right cosets. Lagrange's theorem can hold for right cosets forming a partition of G (standard proof by RST properties) and map H -> Hg is a bijection by group properties. Lagrange's theorems has the following corrolaries, group of order n can be generated by at most log_2(n) elements. This argument works by induction on cosets, minimal prime being 2.s The number of nonisomorphic groups is at most n^{n log_2 n}. This follows by Cayley's theorem, use the fact that symmetric group has order n!. Best possible is n^{c (log n)^2} by classification of finite simple groups. Group actions follow from closure and identity axioms, inverse axioms not necessary. Equivalence classes of residue classes form orbits of the action. Stabilises means trivial action on the element. Action is transitive if only one orbit. Any action can be decomposite to transitive action, this is the first structure theorem for group actions. Second structure theorem implies that transitive action is isomorphic to the coset action onf G:H, H = G_x. Normal subgroup is where left coset = right coset, subgroup is stable under conjugation of any element in g, or closed under conjugation. First isomorphism theorem, image of homomorphism is subgroup of codomain group, kernel of homomorphism is subgroup of domain group, quotient of domain group by kernel group is isomorphic to the image group. Quotient group can be thought of as factor group. Factor group is dividing by a factor. Correspondence theorem is G/L = (G/K)/(L/K) with a lot of conditions. Third iso is K normal subgroup of G, H is any subgroup of G then HK is subgroup containing K, H cap K is normal subgroup, H/(H cap K) = HK/K.

https://cameroncounts.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2013/12/cg.pdf  classical groups, skew fields has abelian group additive group, multiplicative group, left and right multiplication is endomorphism of additive group. Recall that semifield allows noncommutative, allows addition to be monoids. Skew fields, additive group is abelian. Vandermode's theorem, inductive factorisation of f(a) = 0 for roots (x-a) gives at most n roots for x^n. For finite fields, every finite division ring is commutative, the number of elements in a finite field is a prime power, conversely if q i a prime power, unique field of q elements up to isomorphisms, so GF(q). It has additive elmentary abelain p-group, multiplicative group is cyclic, generated by a primitive (p^a - 1) root of unity, automorphism group of F is cyclic of order a, generated by Frobenius auto. Hall subgroup of a finite group is a subgroup whose order and index are coprime. Hall proved a finite, soluble group G has Hall subgroup of all admissible orders dividing |G| such that (m, |G|/m) = 1, any two Hall subgroups of the same order of a finite soluble group are conjugate. Iwasawa's lemma gives criterion for G to be a simple group.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/1703.02325, Connes, Abelian category, morphisms, semiadditive, prototype is category of modules over the boolean semifield as a replacement of the category of abelian groups, analogy between this category and the category of operators of Hilbert space. Sheaves of R_max modules do not form an abelian category. Abelain sheaf cohomology is realised as an important example. A constructive proof in a category holds true in any topos. Category Bmod is referred to in books as the cateogry of join-semilattices with least element, or idempotent semimodules. Bmod is semiadditive, it has a zero object, both initial and final, all finite products and coproducts exists, tthe canonical morphism M coprod N to M prod N is an isomorphism, one then sets M oplus N = M x N and let p_j be canonical projections, s_j be canonical inclusions. In a semiadditive cateogry the set of endomorphisms of an object is canonically endowed with a semiring structure. Bmod^s be category of bmodules be endowed iwith an involution, morphisms of B-modules commutign with sigma i.e. equivariant for the action of Z/2Z. B[s] = B oplus sB, s^2 = 1, p = 1 + s, fulfills p^2 = p, i.e. a Hodge decomposition. Section 7 has analogy to Hilbert spaces. Section 9 is crucial preliminary for constructing sheaf cohomology for sheaves of R_max-modules ona. topos. Did not read past the introduction and did not read the introduction carefully either.

Manifold has tangent space ie. pushforward, frame defines basis for tangent space, connection means can compare vectors at different points, get covariant derivative and curvature. This already is very visual using a sphere as an example. Now you can apply all this at arbitrary vector space associated with each point instead of tangent space, this is the basic idea of gauge theories. Associate each point with internal space, frames becomes gauges, basis for each vector space associated with each point, change of frame is local gauge transformation, change of basis forms a gauge group or a symmetry group. All representationss of compact G are similar to unitary representation, so we have unitary gauge to make all gauge transformations unitary, analogue of choosing unitary frame. If gauge group is not abelian, matter field is a Yang-Mills field. Defining bundles, tangent bundle is defined to be the set of tangent spaces at every point within a region of a coordinate chart, coordinate charts look like local products, atlas of charts looks like global sections? https://www.its.caltech.edu/~matilde/NCnumMot.pdf  noncommutative motives, pure motives concern smooth projective algebaric varieties of cohomology theories, de Rham, Betti, etale, universal cohomology theory means motives can be realised. For NC motives, it is smooth proper dg-categories, with homological invariants, K-theory, Hochschild, cyclic cohomology. Universal homological invariant is NC motives. dg category has composition satisfying Leibniz's rule df * g + (-1)^deg f * dg morphism sets are complexs of k-modules (recall that dg-categories are special case of A_infty categories), dg cat is category of small dg categories with dg-functors, recall Mayer-Vietoris is some sort of gluing. Varietites can be passed to dg-categories, Kontesevich invented saturated dg-categories, smooth is perfect as bimodule over itself, proper of morphism sets are perfect, saturated means smooth and perfect. One can then have derived Morital equivalences if induced restriction of scalars under derivation is equivalent as triangulated categories, cohmological invariants are derived Morita invariant. Symmetric monoidal category is homotopy category of dg-categories up to derived Morital equivalence, use tensor product to extend k-algebras to dg categories, saturated dg-categories are dualisable in Hmo, Eule characteristic of dualizable object is simply the Hochschild homology complex HH(A), work is done by Cisinki-Tabuda on the subject. One can take Hmo0 which further requires that all cohomological invariants listed are additive invariants E : dgcat -> A, E(A) oplus E(B), A additive cateogry |M| DG categories, Objects of |M| is coprouct of Obj(A) Obj(B), morphisms A(x,y), B(x,y), X(x,y) X is A-B bimodule, Hmo0 has object dg-categories, morphisms K0 rep(A,B), composition is derived tensor product of bimodules. So essential one makes tensor product as some sort of inherent composition U_a : dgcat -> Hom0, sends dg-functor to class in Grothendieck group of associated bimodule. Important: ALL ADDITIVE INVARIANTS FACTOR THROUGH HMO0. One can then define noncommutative chow-motives, the F-linearisation of the additive category, the idempotent completion of it, NChow_F(k) is the idempotent complete full subcategory generated by saturated (smooth and proper)-dg categories. NChow_F(k) objects are smooth proper dg categories and idempotents, morphisms are K0(A^op otimes B)_F corespondences, compositions from derived tensor product of bimodules. Commutative modules emed as noncommutative modules are modding out by Tate motives, this is the orbit category. Numerical noncommutative motives, allow one to define the intersection number to be the sum of the Hochschild homology classes of A with coefficients in the derived tensor product of X_i and Y_j contained in the 0-th k-theory. https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/download/topology.pdf  stacks project topology: definition of Hausdorff via diagonal is closed, proof is by taking disjoint opens of diagonals, then take product space - diagonal space, prove directly that it is open, complement is closed, converse is similar by considering that U x V is in (X x X) - diagonal, prove closed graph, graph is inverse image of the diagonal, so graph is closed, prove pushfoward is closed and relative base change is closed. For submersive maps, endow subset with induced subset topology, weakest topology for f is continuous, for preimages to remain open, for preimages remain closed. Using duality, one can endow for X to Y a surjection of sets, Y with a quotient topology, strongest topology for continuity an etc. Quotient topology is stronger, stronger topologies are more likely to converge having more opens, but less likely to be compact. Submersive if induced topology and quotient topology agree AND map is surjective. VERY DIFFERENT FROM SUBMERSION. Just use strict (induced topology and quotient topology agree) instead of submersive. https://math.berkeley.edu/~reb/courses/261/all.pdf  Borcherds' notes on Lie theory. Lie group is defined to be something that resembles invertible two by two matrices or GL_2(R). The main idea is that it is a smooth manifold and a group and these structures are compatible. Examples include real numbers under addition, in fact any finite dimension real vector space under addition forms a Lie group. A Lie group can be split into two parts, component of the identity is a connected, normal subgroup, quotient is discrete (impossible to classify), focus on connected Lie groups. GL_n(R) is the archetypal example of a noncommutative Lie group, classical groups are roughly subgroups of general linear group that preserve bilinear of hermitian forms. Kill off abelian group at the top by taking quotients, gives projective general lienar groups if you quotient by center, special means determinant 1, general symplectic group is the group of matrices that multiply a symplectic form by a nonzero constant. Projective usually means quotient out by the centre i.e. elements that commute with every other element, projective comes from the fact that the projective general linear group acts on projective space. Finally, make centre bigger by making a central extension. Example: spin groups are double covers of special orthogonal group. Solvable Lie group means can be split into abelian groups, and works as Galois theory acting on a set, it is the upper triangular matrices with nonzero determinant, subgroup consisting of matrices on the diagonal, this is a nilpotent Lie group, nilpotent Lie group means if we kill of centre or all the commuting elements we kill off the entire Lie group. Geometry should be identified with geometry, this is the Erlangen program but failed due to Riemannian geometry. General Lie group can be broken up to 1 -> G_conn -> G -> G_discrete -> 1, so G/G_conn is a discrete group. There is also the idea of a maximal connected normal solvable subgroup known as the solvable radical. Group is solvable if chain of subgroups whose consecurive quotients are abelian, Lie's theorem tells us tha some cover G_sol is iso to subgroup of group of upper triangular matrices, then G_nil := [G_sol : G_sol] is nilpotent. A general Lie group has a chain of normal subgroups that are trivial, nilpotent, solvable, connected, or the whole group. https://ncatlab.org/nlab/files/MeyerMoritaEquivalence-2.pdf  Morita equivalences, the idea is that equivalence relations are too strong, two rings are Morita equivalent if there is an equivalence of the category of left modules over R and the category of left modules over S. These are equivalent likewise for right, and categories of modules are additive equivalent, and any functor of modules yields an equivalence that is automatically additive. There are Morita equivalent properties.

http://www.tac.mta.ca/tac/volumes/20/15/20-15.pdf  duality object, idea came from Bill Lawvere, duality arises from a single boject lives in two different categories, example Boolean algebra 2 in frames with enough points, Sierpinski space in sober space. This is the common object. Johnston fixed this by working looking at the algebra for the triples/monads associated with the hom functors. A dualising object is an object in two different categories that induced duality by adjunctions that homms into the object T: A^op -> B, Hom_A(-, a), similarly B^op Hom_B(-,a), this works for Stone duality and Gelfand-Naimark duality. This is the idea of the Chu construction, and also motivates schiozphrenic objects.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/1405.4527, the arithmetic site by Alain Connes, topos is presehaf topos of functors from the multiplicative semigroup of the positive integers (max as sum, + as product BUT endomorphisms form the product over the semiadditive category). The set of points is computed over the maximal compact subring of the tropical semifield is the noncommutative space Q^\times / adele_Q / Z^\times, adele class space of Q by the action of the maximal compact subgroup Z^* of the idele class group, consider a small category C, denote C^ a topos of contravariant functors from C to the category of sets, N^times be single object End(*) = N^times. Define the arithmetic site as the topos N^times endowed with the structural sheaf that is the compactiifcation of the natural numbers, infimum and + viewed as a semiring of the topos. Recall that a point of a topos is a geometric morphism from the topos of sets to T. A cont function induces direct and inverse image between Grothendieck topoi of sheaves, so a geometric morphism is a pair of adjoint function, so that direct image preserves finite limits. If inverse image has exceptional pushforward as left adjoint instead, it is an essential geometric morphism. Since Grothendieck topoi satsifies Freyd's adjoint functos, Grothendeick topoi with all small colimits must have a right adjoint, so for Groth. topos can be defined as a functor called direct image, preserving finite limits, all small colimits, under Freyd's adjoint functor theorem. https://arxiv.org/pdf/1506.08376, one liner proof of weak , go back and digest this again. Let the domain R be a polynomial ring of n-many a_i generators over field K. Then every generator a_i is algebraic over K (Zariski's lemma). If K is algebraically cosed, then a_i is in K for all i. Without loss, by induction suppose a_1 not algebraic over K, then the remaining are in K. Each a_i is algebraic over K[a_1], implies polys f_2(a_1) ... f_n(a_1) in K[a_1] such that each a_i is integral over the domain A = K[a_1][1/f_n(a_1), ... 1/f_n(a_1)] i.e. form field extensions using inverses of polynomials of f_n(a_1) using field extension over K[a_1], thsi is similar to Liouville's theorem, R is integral over A, A is a field (using F is integral over subdomain D, then D is a field), so A = K(a_1) = K[a_1][1/f_n(a_1), ... 1/f_n(a_1)] which is absurd, (D is any PID with infinitely many primes, field of fractions is never a finitely generated D-algebra or K(a_1) is not a finitely generated K[a_1]-algebra). The prototype is that Q is not a finitely generated Z algebra. Replace all K with Q in the proof.

https://people.math.harvard.edu/~lurie/252xnotes/Lecture20.pdf  Lurie's Bousfield localisation notes. X is spectrum, define G(X) as homotopy colimit, condition small subcategory which generates C, is required to make homotopy colimit sensible i.e. indexed by a small category, typical examples is small etale and Zariski sites. Spectrum is C-local if every map Y -> X is nullhomotopic when Y in C, this is the category of C-local spectra, the full subcategory this is stable under shift andhomootpy limits. Bousfield, fix a spectrum, E, another spectrum X is E-acyclic if small product X otimes E is 0, collection of E-acyclic spectra is clearly stable under shifts and homotopy colimits, satisfying the small subcategory condition, a spectrum is E-local if every map Y -> X is nullhomotopic whenever Y is E-acyclic. Analysis shows X sits in unique cof0bre sequence G_E(X) -> X -> L_E(X), G_E is E-acyclic, L_E(X) is E-local, L_E is Bousfield localisation with respect to E.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/1704.01863, first isomorphism theorem to be developed. Theorems of group theory can be proved uding group lattices. First and seocnd isomorphisms can be developed by choosing carefuly chosen dual axioms. Abelian categories are defined by dual axioms, dual category is abelian. Semi-abelian categories (or semiadditive categories) are noncommutative analogues of abelian categories. Semiabelian categories whose dual is semiabelian is already an abelian category. Self dual axioms are reformulations of first isomorphism theorem, lattice isomorphism theorem, and stability of normal subgroup under joints, butterfly lemma due to Zassenhaus, MacLane's method of chasing subobjects, discover a single isomorphism theorem, called the universal isomorphism theorem that allows one to establish all of the others. Atomic expressions are groups, homomorphisms, composites, subgroups, inclusions direct image, inverse image. Basic axioms are associative composition of homs, subgroup inclusion is poset (reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive), if f(A) <= B iff A <= f^{-1}(B), direct and inverses can be composed, groups and group homomorphisms form a category, direct and inverse image maps form a monotone galosi connection, defines a category of groups to the category of posets and Galois connections. The universal isomorphism theorem is so poorly stated it is hard to remember.

Axiom A: for generator s in S, w in K (W,S) Coxeter system K subset of W, exists scalars such that rho(C_w) = a_s(w)C_w + b_s(w) C_ws, if w in K but ws not in k, b_s(w) = 0, pair (p, K) is an abstract Young pair. Right Cayley graph X(W,S), W vertices, directed edge w to ws, convex in X(W,S) if every shortest path between two elements of K has all its vertices in K. Let W be finite Coxeter group, V is root space, basic affine hyperplane in V has form H_t,eps = f in V <f, alpha_t> = eps, basic proper flat in V is intersection of basic hyperplanes, partition W into convex subset, called L-partition.

https://math.mit.edu/~dav/mckay.pdf  great paper on McKay groups and harmonic classification of SU(2). Consider compact group G, finite collection of complex reps of G (pi, V), graph Gamma, vertices of Gamma are irreducible reps, vertex as labelled by its dimension, edges comes in different colours, of representations pi_j. Directed edges, nonneg integer multiplicities, multiplicity of tau in rho tensor pi_k, rho is vertex, pi_j is rep, is dimension of Hom_G(\tau, rho \otimes pi_j), reducibility of fd reps of G, apply associativity formula, prove that McKay graph is undirected. McKay grpahs are just undirected graphs with labelled dimension. These ideas are simple when G is compact subgroup of SU(2). Theorem by McKay, G is nontrivial compact subgroup of SU(2), pi is 2D rep, form McKay graph, vertices of graph is irred rho of G, vertex is label by deimensions, vertex rho and tau joined by m edges if tau appears with multiplicity m in rho otimes pi. Base point vertex 1 exists, harmonic, each label is twice the sum of adjacent labels, graph is connected, no loops no edge connects vertex to itself. Proof, (1) is trivial rep, part (2) is computation, part (3) is ever rep appears asas tensor power of pi, consequence of pi being faithful and self-dual and Stone-Weierstrass approximation theorem, (4) says rho cannot occur in rho otimes pi i.e. no loops.Labelled graph satisfying McKay's Theorem is harmonic graph, which are precisely graphs of closed subgroups of SU(2). IOnly proof is to classify graph and observe they correspond to known subgroups of SU(2). Tedious. Reduced root datum.

https://www.maths.usyd.edu.au/u/geordie/perverse_course/lectures.pdf  perverse sheaves, read chapters 1 to 3. Find the right picture, half the problem! Geometric introduction to pervere sheaves, this is probably the best introduction. Definition of manifolds as self-diffeomorphism of a connected manifold acts transitively. A stratification of an algebraic variety is a decomposition into finitely many strata such that each strata is connected and locally closed in the Zariski topology, the closure of any stratum is a union of strata. Local normal triviality is means Thom's notion of topologically stratified space is needed. Equisingularity along strata requires stratum satisfies local normal triviality. Stratification does not guarantee equisingularity along the strata. Counterexample is Whitney umbrella. Singular surface X given by x^2 = zy^2.Witney umbrealla looks like an umbreall with four leave clover. Better definition is to include Whitney's condition X = X reg coprod (X_sing \ {0}) coprod {0}. Use normalisation (uv) -> (uv, v, u^2). This is a Whitney stratification. Morphisms of algebraic varieties. There are two examples: birational maps and regular maps from Borcherd's notes. Take map from z in C to z in z^m, it looks like collapsing staircase projecting downwards. Birational maps, archetypical example is blow up of a point, blow up turns projective space into a hairbrush Bl_0. Family mean some projective surjective map f from X to Y, Y is small, fibres of f are varying families parameterised by f, impose flatness or tensor product exact sequence to ensure fibres do not jump too much. Homotopy theory is the case where every map is a fibration, algebraic world means almost all maps will have singularities. Recall that blow ups when projected down give maps of singularities (not sure where I learnt this). Smooth maps is what topologists call submersion, any smooth family will be diffeomorphic. Sheaf is a local system if locally constant and finitely generated, can check that it is a category (subcategory of sheaves). Local systems are very different from vector bundles, U be subset over vector bundle, trivial, automorphisms are GL_n(O_U), n is rank of vector bundle, but local system is trivial over connected set U, autos are GL_n(k), smaller group. Local system is vector bundle with distinguished notion of a flat section. Exercise becomes obvious since Loc(X,k) is abelian subcategory of Sh(X,k) since Loc(X,k) is a special version of vector bundles. Category of local systems on X is equivalent to the abelian category of representations on finitely generated k-modules for X connected and x in X is a basepoint Rep(pi_1(X,x), k). Canonical example of constructible sheaf: f from X to Y is morphism of algebraic variety, consider sheaf on Y associated to presheaf U -> H^i(f^{-1}(U), k). A sheaf is constructible if stratification exists so that it is a local system witn singulartiies. But the title of local system with singularities is best reserved for intersection cohomology complexes. Complexes good, cohomology bad, study a complex up to quasiisomorphism than its cohomology groups. Start with Poincare duality, Verdier duality is a local theory of Poincare duality needed to work with exceptional pushforward and pullback with compact support to get sheaf and cosheaf duality.

Curves of genus 1 is different from elliptic curves One can assign j-invariant to curve of genus 1. B irred varieties, pi, X -> B is family of irreducible varieties. X seems to be a collection of elliptic curves, fundamentla group _1(B_sm, b) acts on cohomology H^k(p^{-1}(b), G) or fibres with coefficient group the abelian group G. image of pi_1 in the automorphism group of the cohomology gorup is called the monodromy group. https://arxiv.org/pdf/1511.02888, read first 15 pages, tropical geometry matroids, matroid is closure operator defined on all subsets of finite set satisfying exchange property, I of E a not in closure of I, if a in closure of I union {b}, b in closure of I union {a}, replace this with colimits so it is like exchange of colimits of independent sets, matroid is loopless with empty subset is closed, combinatorial geometry if in addition all single element subsets are closed, closed subset of E is flat o fM, every subset of E has rank and corank in poset of all flats. Matroid feels like extending slowly by exchange of singleton colimits. Rota defined arbitrary matroid characteristic poly, so linear combination over independent set Euler char * lambda^corank(I), sequence of absolute value of lambda is log-concave as conjecture. I in E is independent in M if no element in I is in the closure of I\{i}, so closure fails by removing singletons. Construct cohomology ring of M satisfying hard Lefschetz and Hodge-Riemann relation i.e. working with complex analytics, build polyring ring S_M := R[x_ | F is nonempty proper flat of M], define show ring to be S_M/(I_M + J_M), I_M is ideal generated by quadratic monomials x_F1x_F2 for incomparable nonempty proper flats of M, J is ideal generated by linear forms sum_i1 x_F - sum_i2 x_F. Validity of Hodge-Riemann relations for Chow ring S_M/(I_M + J_M) Need strict submodular, Hard Lefschetz, multiplication by l gives Poincare duality, multiplication by l gives Euler char (Hodge-Riemann). Matroidal flips for induction. Sect 4 and 5 can be read immediately after.

https://math.uchicago.edu/~may/REU2021/REUPapers/Niemiro.pdf  derived functor to sheaf cohomology in 12 pages, there are many examples, tame sheaf cohomology for abelian categories, presheaf tracks data attached to opens with locality, presheaf demand functorality with respect to inclusions, contravariant functor from Open(X) -> D, sectiosn of F over U, for each U, an object F(U), elements of F(U) are sections, elems of F(X) are global sections, for each inclusion of opens, restriction maps F(U) to F(V), then there are restriction morphisms. Sheaf has identity or separation condition for each U_i in U, giving s = t, gluing means it agrees under Cech cocycles or pairwise cocycle condtion. Sheaf of continuous function, Presheaf fix at two points, agree on overlap but disagree at p and q, do not glue to global, so not sheaf, constant presheaf, restrictions are identity homomorphism, cover empty set by empty union, F is not a sheaf, so only constant presheaf. Presheaf of constant functions, vaciously agree on intercept, but s and t do not glue to constant function, so not a sheaf. Presheaf of bounded function, glue to g unbounded, not a sheaf. Abelianc ategory have zero object, so one can always define kernels and cokernels, there is a snake lemma, and there is represnetability with a first isomorphism theorem. Finite coproducts and finite products exists and work.Bilinear composition of morphisms, has all kernels and cokernels, as first iso thoerem, need PA2 preadditive, A to be additive, A and AB2 is abelian. Functors between abelian categories fail to preserve exactness, the Ext and Tor groups and sheaf cohomological groups, take derived functor of global sections sheaf to produce sheaf cohomology. Left exact, right exact, easy to remember. Define desirable properties, acyclic resolution if R^i F(A) = 0 for all i, proof isomorphism of R^iF and sheaf cohomology H^i(F(A)), prove by induction observe split sequences and injective objects, define split by some snake lemma. Enough injectives if X admits monomorphism into injective object for every object X in category, same with projectives. Define sheaf of O_X-modules, O_X-mod is abelian, zero module assigns trivial module to every open, sum of morphisms of O_X modules add two maps induced on modules, kernel is kernel of induced morphisms, cokernels are less straightforward, only defines presheaf, but take sheafification, so surjective sheaf morphisms more nuanced, not just injective as module homomorphism, surjective is every section, there is an open n'hood V in X of x such that S_V = phi_V(t) for section in F(V), requires sections to lift locally, crucial, surj : F -> G, section of G(X) may not lie in image of local surj_X, models many local to global obstructions.

https://www.jmilne.org/math/Documents/DeligneArcata.pdf  etale cohomology starting points Deligne. Continuity on f is local, if continuous on sufficiently small open, then continuous on whole of X, to formalise local property use sieve, a set of opens is s seive if for every open in the set and every subopen inclusion of the open, we have this subopen in this set. This sieve is a covering if it is union of the opens belonging to it equals (why not overcover) to the full space X. Sieve is generated by a family opens. A property is local if property is true on opens iff property is true on subopen inclusions. Sieve point on view, use Cech point of view, let Z be coproduct of basis of opens for sieve, give function sieve-locally give function constant on the fibres of the natural projection of the coproduct on the topological space, the continuous functions form a sheaf. Now for stacks, instead of form a coproduct of opens, a sieve is generated by a family of opens, giving a vector bundle sieve-locally means giving a vector bundle E_i (total space) on each subopen inclusion generated basis elements of the family (basis elements, I am just going to call them subopens), an intersections of subopens gives a fibre product, an isomorphism of total spaces such that for Cech cocycles of triple fibre products, the relevant diagram commutes. So in a sense, stacks (or champs) means we are trying to make bundles work locally and glue them togethe. So a notion of a vector bundle is local like continuity being local. Zariski topology is not fine enough for the study of nonlinear problem, the above gives the Zariski topology. Descent techniques appear as localisation techniques (because you are trying to maek things local).

https://www.jmilne.org/math/CourseNotes/LEC.pdf  Milne etale cohomology, MUST READ, Tohoku gives the definition, category of sheaves of abelian groups is an abelian category with enough injectives, so one can define the cohomology groups of the sheaves on a space as right derived functor functors taking a sheaf to its abelian group of global sections after Ext, recovers cohomology of fixed coeff groups by considering the constant sheaf. Accepted defintiion of cohomology group. Fix topological space but consider all sheaves on the space, constant sheaf gives sheaf cohomology. Deligne's approach requires constructible sheaves.

https://web.ma.utexas.edu/users/slaoui/notes/Sheaf_Cohomology_3.pdf  basic primer on sheaf theory, sheaves require assignment compatible with taking restrictions and going from local to global sections i.e. I can take pushforwards of sheaves. Sheaf cohomology generalises more conventional cohomology theories, leading to six functor formalism, with the constant sheaf being the prototypical example of a coefficient system. Smooth manifolds as ringed spaces, so you can do this as an interlude before sheaves.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/1808.02923, Conway's knot is not slice, good for definitions concordance is a four dimensional extension, a knot in S^3 is trivial in concordance if it bounds an embedded disk in B^4, knot is slice if it bounds a smooth embedded disk in B^4, topolocally slice if it bounds a lcally flat disk in B^4. A knot trace is a four manifold obtained by attaching a 0-framebody handle to B^4 with attaching sphere K, K is slice if and only if a knot trace smoothly embeds in S^4. Trick is to recall a dualizable patterns construction, reinterpreted by Picarillio. Khonvanov link invariant. Khonvanov differential. This gives a Lie homology. Rasmussen's s-invariant, Heegard-Floer correction. Positive mutation preserves 3D invariants of knots, preserving Alexander, Jones, Homfly, Kauffman polies, S-equiv class, hyperbolic volume, 2-fold branched covers. Conjecturally, Khovanov homology, and delta-grade knot Floer groups also preserve this.

https://arxiv.org/pdf/2203.07082, introsurvey to representation theory. Fun introduction to modern representation theory. Groups, what are you made of and how do you behave (this is the Galois point of view). Monster group relates largest sporadic group with modular functions. The two most beautiful groups in the world. Subgroups of GL_n are called linear groups, subgroup of S_n are called finite groups. Subgroups have special names. Summary, GL_n(F_q) is deformation of S_n, how to change GL_n into S_n and vice versa. Most important idea came from Tits, S_n = GL_n(F_1). Field of one element does not exist. Geometry over F_1 can be solved by studying this. q-deformation of S_n gives PGL_n(F_q), almost the correct candidates. Tits explained if G is a Chevalley group scheme over Z, the cardinality of the group of points in G in F_q satisfies some formula. If PGL_n(F_q) is quantum deformation of S_n, determinant is quantum deformation of sign function!. A_n is kernel of sign homomorphism corresponds to PSL_n(F_q). SL_n(F_1) = S_n implies using Barratt-Priddy-Quillen, that K_i(F_q) the higher K theory of F_q must be the stable homotopy groups of spheres, p-canonical basis could potentially be understood using categories. Observe that S_n is subgroup of GL_n(F_p), each permutation w corresponds to w' a permutation matrix. If B is the set of upper triangular matrix, then GL_n(F_p) can be partitioned to a disjoint union of double cosets parameterized by S_n. Proof is by Gaussian elimination, modified to become the Bruhat decomposition. Bruhat decomposition turns questions about GL_n to questions about SL_n. Equation 2 stays true by replacing F_p with C. S_n and GL_n are two faces of the same coint, use group algebra, or vector space or elements of symmetric group form C-basis of group algebra C[S_n], C is complex numbers. Schur-Weyl duality give duality between C[GL(V)] and End_C[S_n](V^otimes n), guiding principle in representation theory. Maschke's theorem, every rep of S_n over C is direct sum of irreducible representations. Instead of making S_n act on GL(CX), permutation rep, make it act on flag varieties. This gives Specht modules. Vector space by n! vectors, but highly linearly dependent. SL_n(F_q) is a deformation, a partition, a dual strongly related to the symmetric group. Steinberg proved that all irreducible representations of SL_n(F_q) can be obtained from the irreducible algebraic representations of algebraic group SL_n(bar F_p). by restriction, algebraic closure of finite field. Algebraic rep is group hom from SL_2(k) to GL_n(k). Turn the [a b; c d] into something harder. Subset S u {s_0} in S_n gives structure of Coxeter system on bar S_n. Seocnd is geometric Satake equivalence, mirkovic and Vilonen. Need affine grassmannian or ring of infinite polys where fraction field is field of formal Laurent series. Iwahori subgroup is preimage of upper triangular matris under map GL_n(O) -> GL_n(C), O is ring of formal powers. Generalises Bruhat decomposition. Next ingredient is perverse sheaf. Good category to compute stuff in. Coxeter system is a group W with a generating subset S in W, W admits presentation by generators s in S and relations (sr)^m(s,r) = 1, W is Coxeter group, S is simple reflections. Reduced expression, Bruhat orders. Quantum Schur-Weyl duality should mean, Iwahori-Hecker algebra, PGL_n(F_q) is deformation of Coxeter group W = S_n, problem PGL_n(F_q) is not one, using classification of finite Coxeter groups. Use representation theory and work over group ring modules. Use quadratic relations s^2 = 1, and braid relations srs ... = rsr ... deform this algebra is easy, but do not deform the braid relations, deform the quadratic ones. Strand diagram notation picture element of symmetric group. Soergel bimodules to diagrammatic Hecke categories goes through topology. Braid group is the third most beautiful. Vaughn jones, get knots and links, discovered new representations of the braid groups to get the Jones polynomial. Correct deformation of the braid group is super hard. Shimura defined Hecke rings, G is group H subgroup such that [H : (H cap x H x^-1)] is finite i.e. finite index, then take some free Z-module generated by double H x H classes of G, enriched with associative algebra. Shimura call this the Hecke ring because G = SL_2(Q), H = SL_2(Z), get Hecker opertors for modular forms. Hecker algebra of Coxeter system is Z[v, 1/v] algebra with generators h_s for some quadratic and braid relations, v comes from slight normalisation. Relation of Hecker algebras to GL_n(F_q) comes from braided monoidal category. Kazhdan-Luszztig polynomials live inside Hecker algebra. Most interesting Lie groups and Lie algebras are rigid. 8.2.1 10 line introduction to algebraic geometry, Sl_n(C), take Lie algebra sl_n(C), take enveloping algebra U_C(sl_n) take quantisation U_q(sl_n). Qunatum group has ugly list of relations. Analogy between S_n and GL_n, U_q(sl_n) and SL_n(F_p) at root of unity, these are analogies. No maps between 3 and 5 (except for natural order) but tons of maps between 3 elements and 5 elements, and 3D vector space and 5D vector space. Categorified Lie groups, give right setting for quantum gravity? Categorifcation of Lie groups appear as A(sl_n), 2 Kac-Moody algebra! Categorical action is Soergel-bimodules.

Smooth proper curve over scheme S is morphism of schemes f: X -> S which is flat (tensor products) of finite presentation, smooth and proper of relative dim 1 with f_* O_X = O_S, for 2 - 2g < 0, consider functor of points, schemes represented by groupoids of smooth proper curves X -> S of genus g. More generally 2 - 2g - n, functor of points of smooth proper curves of genus g n sections. Canonical morphism forgetting n+1 point is universal curve. Prestable curve is flat, finite presentation, relative dim 1, with at worst nodal singularities see Pandharipande notes, prestable curve of genus g > 1 has no rational tails/bridges precisely if omega_X/S is ample on inverse image of open for some open U in S. A stable curve over a scheme S is a prestable curve, copy def. X_s has no rational tails/bridges for all s in S. It has finitely many automorphisms, recall Picard and valuation rings, stability condition required for compactification M_g to be represented by a Deligne-Mumford stack. Use Schwartz-Zippel, Alice sends over Ax, Bob determines Ax = Bx, determinant of adjacency graphs are polys over finite field. Graph theory, cut is partition of vertices. Minimum cut is size of smallest cut across all pairs of sets of vertices. Karger's minimum cut algorithm, iteratively reduce number of vertices via edge contractions, choose random edge and merge the two end points into single vertex, preserving multiedges. Graph disconnected, always return correct min cut, now suppose graph consists of two components connected by single. Algorithm success as long as it avoids the single edge, good chance we may never contract the single edge, so bad cases is when there is a lot of connections.

https://www.cl.cam.ac.uk/teaching/2122/RandAlgthm/lec23_concentration_handout.pdf  slides on Chernoff bounds, concentration is the phenomena where random variables are very close to their mean, Chernoff bounds are strong bounds on tail probabilities of sums of independent randomly variable, boundes decrease exponentially compared to polynomial decrease compared to Markov and Chebyshev, downside is that it requires independence. Used in PAC learning, Markov P[X >= a] <= E[X]/a, Chebyschev is substitute X as X-E[X], use first and second moments. Chernoff bound does all moments but uses independence Bernoulli. Easiest Chernoff bound to derive, bound is complicated and hard to apply. Coin flip. three steps, consider independent random variables, get mgf apply Markov, general method compute upper bound for E[exp*(lambda X)], optimise lambda for best bound. Page 12. Hoeffding's extension lemma helps in cases in which you do not know MGF. Then one can get Hoeffding's inequality. Basic ideas, good in expectation + Chernoff -> high probability optimality.

https://emilyriehl.github.io/files/context.pdf  Riehl's in context, the only definition of preserve, reflects and creates. Preserve means limit (property in domain) implies limit in codomain, reflect means property in image of cone implies cone with limit in A, create means limit in codomain implies unique limit in A. Example, an object is W-local if it reflects invertibility in W. Localisation loses a lot of structural differences ensuring isomorphism, but the payoff you get is you get an isomorphism or you can isolate the differences on that particular local part. Careful, forgetful does not preserve and reflect exactness, exact is only monadic over Ab (by Freyd-Mitchell or by abelian categories yoga).  Kan extensions, consider C over base C' as functor p, Kan extensions extends functor F from C to D to define functor from C' to D. Left Kan extension is cocone, pick C' as *, then nat transform to Kan extension of functor F since cocone. Right Kan extension is cone, pick C' as *, then nat transform from Kan extension of functor F to point, RAPL, LAPC. NOTE, pointwise is weighted limit or colimit pointwise for each point, must exist, local Kan extension is for single functor, global Kan extension is for all possible functors, so iso class/possibilities. Point means limit or colimit. Functor from C to D, left adjoint case is consider F: C->C, base is D, left Kan extension. Right adjoint case is consider F from D->D, C as base, then right Kan extension. Derived functors, Kan extensions along localisation functors F: C -> D, pick base as HoC and HoD. Left derived is nat to F, right derived is nat to HoC -> HoD. Kan extensions can be done squarewise. Yoneda lemma is pointwise Kan extension, is equaliser diagram, by Kan extension computation pointwise, and descent of computation via the pointwise computation of the limit formula c/C -> C -> D, c/C is the base as an equalizer, one gets the Yoneda lemma due to pointwise nature of c/C. The coYoneda lemma uses the left Kan extension of itsself along id_C, colimit formula gives Fc iso colim(C/c -> C -> D), C/c is the base, pointwise to full Kan extension due to descent gives the coYoneda lemma via coequaliser diagram. Yoneda density theorm can be proven. Any functor locally small, any functor C to Set is colimit of some diagram with subcategory of rep functors as a base in the form of the colimit formula. Apply coYoneda lemma to prove this. Representable functors form a dense subcategory as a result of the coYoneda lemma and the Yoneda density theorm. Codensity monad is the right Kan extension of the functor from D -> C along itself, with base C. This is monadic. Field included to C-ring, no left adjoint, but has codensity monad of fraction field Frac(A/p), this is why it is codensity, density in rationals, inclusion of Fin into Set gets the ultrafilter monad, so the set of ultrafilters follows a limit formula. Giry monad is codensity monad of subcategory C into Meas, C is finite powers of unit interval equipped with Borel algebra. Codensity monads are super cool. Today I really mastered Kan extensions and the colimit formula + better understanding of Grothendieck's relative PoV and sheaves on a base. Definition of pointwise essential! If right Kan extension can be computed pointwise by a limit formula. How to remember limit formula, it is always lim(d,K -> C -> E), base is always a comma category, take d computed pointwise in category D, D is base, C -> D is the functor in question K represents d, extend right functor C -> E. For right Kan extension, it is a limit so source is morphism d, image is functor K, because you need to form a cone with d, so it is d mapping to the functor, see computation proof of Theorem 6.3.7, that is the essential content. To summarise, define comma category d,K, source is im d, target is im K. Then define limit formula Ran_K F(d) nat iso to lim( d/K -> C -> E) for right Kan extension of functor F: C -> E along base D for d in D. Prove by computation and Yoneda lemma. Then prove stronger Yoneda lemma using equaliser descent. Dually, coYoneda lemma is proven, can be used to prove density theorm, existence of field of frac monad from Z inclusion into Q, ultrafilter monad from FinSet inclusion into Set. Left Kan extensions, nat map into functor, right Kan extensions nat map into the point. Extension is always extensions over base for functor F : C to D , base is C', proj functor C to C', so LanF or RanF is from C' to D. Limits and colimits, take C' as point, p as ! functor. Left adjoint and right adjoint, left adjoint pick target as base, for left Kan extension, right adjoint pick source as base for right Kan extension. Derived functor, RIGHT Kan extension gives total LEFT derived functors to C -> D, left Kan extension gives RIGHT derived functor HoC -> HoD, same thing with resolutions resulting in different handedness.

J^k is some orthogonality ideal, get exterior product. One can now do the exterior algebra. Tensor algebra T(g) is take T^k to infinity, think as some sort of formal power series, J be elements x otimes y - y otimes x = [x,y] i.e.. get noncommutativity as Lie bracket, get univerasl enveloping algebra. Let K be number field with ring of integers O_K, for v a place of K, K_v be completion of K at v with valuation O_V, Let A_K be adele ring of K, at finitely many places, consisting (x_v)_v in prod K_v such that x_v in O_v for all but finitely many places. There is diagonal embeddings of K in A_K, K^\times in idele calss group I_K. Local fields K_v with usual topology are locally compact abelian groups which are Pontryagin self dual with the calculation Hom(K_v, U(1)) = K^_v. Restricted product on adele ring, then the ring of adeles is also Pontryagin self dual, this is the restricted product topology. Quotient I_K^1 / K^cross is compact by definition of global field as a field that sits in a locally compact ring discretely and cocompactly. This immediately implies finiteness of class number of K and Dirichlet's unit theorem.

https://gaborszabo.nfshost.com/Slides/2018-11-Banff-IntroCAlg-v3.pdf  slides on C* algebras, 155 slides very complete. Bounded linear operators become Banach algebras with operator norms, adjoint operation is involution. Complex Hilbert space with inner product. Abstract C*-algebra is complex Banach algebra with involution preserving square norm on involution. Concrete C* algebra is a self-adjoint subalgebra A in B(H) for some Hilbert space closed in the operator norm. Note this is basically secretly some sort of 6 functor formalism but with norms. Spectral theory C(X) for continuous functions X to C remembers X by Yoneda, or by considering character theory secretly. Gelfand-Neimark is a special case of dualizing object into two categories C*-algebra and C(X). Gelfand-Naimark-Segal means abstract C*-algebra can be expressed as concete C*-algebra. Neumann series is very invertible magic, in fact it cuts right to chase 1/x = sum(1-x)^n, or geometric series, geometric series is a hint on localisation. Spectrum can be defined as those elements which are not invertible for lambda - x, as in those elements which satisfy the hypothesis of Schur's lemma. Noninvertibility corresponds to killing off a dimension using the spectral theorem. Spectral radius is max eigenvalue, furthermore this is compact subset under norm. Neumann series trick is used to justify continuity of character theory. Spectrum is also known as character space. Characters are eigenvectors for noninvertibility to lose dimension to correspond to evaluated elements in C (algebraically closed is helpful here). Spectral radius formula is given by limit of geometric lim rootn(||x^n||). Feldfand transform is unital homomorphism A to C(A^), the most general kind of transform. Self adjoint, spectral element is real corresponds to observables. Charracters are involution preserving, hint use complex conjugation in C. Gelfand-Naimark theorem can be stated as Gelfand transform is isometric *-isomorphism. Functional calculus, not sure what this is. Representation on Hilbert space is *-homomorphism A to B(H) (bounded linear operators on H), faithful if injective, nondegenerate if spanbar(A)H = H, cyclic representation for some norm = 1 to give partitions of unity. Recall partitions means I can do thermodynamics. I got tired at slide 60.

https://math.berkeley.edu/~sosun/ICBS24.pdf  decent introduction to this topic, Kahler geometry studies triples, g Riemannian metric, J is almost complex structure, omega is a 2-form. Standard example is Fubini-Study metric. Not all Fano manifold can admit a Kahler-Einstein metric. Calabi conjecture. A Kahler metric is Kahler-Einstein, Ric(omega) = lambda omega. X is K-stable. A Riemannian metric g is Kahler if it has its holonomy group contained in U(n), so complex structure J is essentially determined by metric g. Canonical Kahler metric. Calabi conjecture. K-stability. Hermitian-Yang-Mills condition iff stable holomorphic bundles. K-stability algebraization by replacing geodesic rays in H by one parameter subgroups in PGL(N+1,C). Critical points means stable. K-stability is algebraic geometry. Generalises Hilbert-Mumford condition. One studies degenerations. Get algebraic scheeme by taking lambda(t) for one parameter subgroup, t tends to 0. Main strategy is via continuous deformation, choose intiial Kahler metric, deform it to Kahler-Einstein metric, if deformation fails, produce destabilising test configurations. example, cone singularities. Opem: show weak Kahler Einstein potential is Kahler-Einstein metric. Study of moduli space, Birkar's birational geometry. Natural objects from caonical Kahler metrics have algebraic geometry. bubbling theory. Optimal degeneration theroy of Fano manifolds.

Moduli space of smooth algebraic curves of genus bigger than 1 can be compactified by adding singular curves wihth nodes, locally defined by xy = 0, this is the Deligne-Mumford compactification, whenever a node forms, lcoally the corresponding metrics splits into union of two hyperbolic cusps with infinite diameter. Extremal Kahler metric amounts to solving difficult nonlinear elliptic PDE. K-stability, Donaldson-Futaki invariant, Hilbert-Mumford criterion for stability in geometric invariant theory. Strong is pointwise, weak is always under inner prod or in measure. But strong topology has harder convergence, weak topology has easier convergence by making mor sets compact.

https://choum.net/~chris/talks/Beamer_ILCLI.pdf  short tour in model theory, semantic gives meaning, syntax is to formalise the semantic. Godel completeness of first order logic. First order theory of ZF is built on top of Naive Set Theory. Model theories use ZF. Exact definition of model theory is hard since it interacts a lot with mathematics. Model theorists focus on semantics, only the set itself is important. Quantifier elimination if ther exists another formula which does not use for all and there exists that is equivalent. A structure has QE if the theory or its axioms have quantifer elimination. Quantifier elimination is a classical tool. Default of expressibility or collapse of semantic. Example x > 0 eliminated quantifiers for x = y^2 up to the level of sets. Compactness theorem, symbolic definition? Classification theory by Shelah uses infinite combinatorics. A theory is stable if for all model M of T and for all formula, there is no infinite sequence of elements of M such that the following holds i.e. i <= j iff M follows formula phi(a_i,a_j). Informally no formula interprets order that behaves like (N, <=), (R, +, *, <) is not stable but (C, +. *) is stable. Stability is an extremely successful notion since it occurs in C, and allows for lagebraic geometry to use it. Model theory is too general, general tools are weak for particular eaxmples.

A subset is aproper filter if I in filter, empty not in filter, A, B in filter implies intersection in filter, A in filter A in B in I, then B in filter. I is nonempty set, P(I) is power set of I, filters are in the power set of I. Frechet filter gives notion of largeness. Ultrafilter is maximal filter, filter not contained in any other filter, principal filter on I denoted by F_x is filter for one element, x in I, F_x is ultrafilter. Ultrafilter is a deicision about which of X or its complement is large. Los's theorem. Compactness theorem, T is L theory, T is consistent if and only if every finite subset of T is consistent. Can be proved using ultrafitlers. Quantifier elimination, model theroy is to study all definable sets.
