https://www.math.nagoya-u.ac.jp/~richard/teaching/f2025/SML_Haruki_1.pdf  best notes I have found on matrix exponentials, matrix exponential is power series of A^k / k!, or alternatively as product formula (1 + A/n)^n as n tends to infinity whose expansion gives the symmetric function k!, or there is the Campbell-Baker-Hausdorff formula. It is related to Kahler differentials since it is infinitely differentiable for case A = 1, exp(t)' = exp(t), integral of exp(i pi x) when converted to a power series gives rise to a Hodge decomposition to sine and cosine, integration also means shifting of power series terms so this looks like rotations by i when integrating. It turns determinant intro trace det(exp(A)) = exp(det(A)). Differentiating the conjugate gives the Lie brakcet i.e. (exp(tA) B exp(-tA)) = AB - BA which gives rise to the canonical time evolution, one parameter semigroup. Hodge decomposition. Combinatorics, Lie theory, algebraic geometry, Hodge theory, operator algebra and operator algebra theory, stochastic calculus all comes from this very basic example, control theory. Logs are localsections of exponential maps.

https://www.wias-berlin.de/people/vanzuijlen/docs/LN_theory_of_function_spaces.pdf 127 pages on the theory of function spaces, define support of f to be set of all x for neighbourhood of x, there exists an element y in that neighbourhood (pullback to y) such that f(y) is nonzero.if F is a set of functions, there exists a subset of compactly supported functions i.e. F_c = {f in F : supp f is compact}. Support means set of all y values via neighbourhood that gives existence via pullback. Recall that homology and Poincare duality is needed for one of this definition. Take D(omega) to be the vector space of C^\infty(Omega)_c i.e. smooth infinitely differential functions on outcome space with compact support i.e. f(y) is nonzero. An eleemnt of D(Omega) is a test function. A linear function u from D(Omega) -> F is called a distribution (think about probability distribution) for all compact sets in outcome space in D(Omega) or C_c^\infty(Omega), there exists a constant C > 0 and k in N_0 such that |u([hi]| <= C|phi|_C^k), C^k is k-time differentiable, supp phi in K, K compact in Omega, phi in D(Omega) i.e. distributions are defined so that it satisfies a duality statement which I am supposed to remember but I cannot remember. I think it is similar to some of the Tannakian or representation theory dualities. So a distribution is a linear function u such that there exists an inequality for the norm in C^k space to be bounded when u acts on a test function, and the support of the test function must be contained in a compact set. Test function is D(omega), distribution is vector space D'(omega).

Divisor group of a curve comes from Galois theory, let C/k be a smooth curve, for all P in C(bar k) define formal symbol [P] i.e. all equiv classes in C(bar k), divisor group is free abelian group on the set of formal symbols, its elements are divisors i.e. think of divisors as alphabets of formal symbols formed as linear combinations under valuation of D, v_P(D)[P], where v_P[D] is the vlaaution of D at P which is zero for all but finitely many P (cofinitely many zeroes, mimics adeles and class field theory). It has support supp(D) which is the set of formal symbols with nonzero valuation, and degree which is the sum of all valuations of the divisors. For closed points you define it directly in terms of the degree of the divisor deg P instead of [P]. Associated to each function there is a divisor div f := sum of v_P(f)[P], these are principal diviosrs, there is group homomorphism div fg = div f + div g, div: k(C)^\times -> Div C, image is Princ C is subgroup of Div C, from homology theory, makes sense to set image Prin C to be the kernel of the map, define Picard group as Div C / Princ C. Degree map whose kernel is the diviosr class group. This gives the Abel Jacobi map as well from C(k) -> Pic^0 C. Abel Jacobi map is map from C(k) function field, O rational point in C(k) to Picard group C or P -> [P] - [O], k = bar k is special case, smooth projective curves of genus 1 with rational points i.e. elliptic curves are precisely curves where the Abel Jacobi map is an isomorphism, can be used as a definition? Weil reciprocity, let C be a smooth projective curve over k = k bar, let f and g in k(C)^\times be functions whose divisors have disjoint support, then f(div g) = g(div f). https://people.math.ethz.ch/~kowalski/number-theory.pdf cardinal, partitions, difference set, other notations.  Fermat, prime congruent to 1 mod 4 is sum of two squares. The couple a and b is unique up to exchange. Quadratic residue, congruence x^2 = -1 mod p always have solution via Wilson's theorem, pick k^2 + 1 congruent to 0 mod p by Wilson's using fact that p is prime. Pigeonhole, look for small integers a - kb = 0 mod p. By averaging, possible values for a is floor root p + 1, possible values for p is also floor root p + 1. So totally number of pairs is (floor root p + 1)^2 > root p by product rule of combinatorics, number of pairs is striclty greater than p. Pigeonhole suggests there must be two distinct pairs a_1 - kb_1 = a_2 - kb_2 mod p. rearrange and get (a_1 - a_2) cong k(b_1 - b_2), do dummy sub a = a_1 - a_2, b = b_1 - b_2. then a cong kb mod p. Square the congruence, use fact that k^2 = -1 mod p by Wilson's theorem, get a^2 + b^2 = 0 mod p. It is multiple of p, Both a^2 and b^2 are bounded by prime p, nonzero WLOG, only multiple between 0 and 2p is p itself by definition of prime, so a^2 + b^2 = p. This is a brilliant proof. Wilson's theorem seems key to this. Prime number theorey, Cheybychev arguments.

https://web.williams.edu/Mathematics/lg5/GaussSum.pdf Gauss sums in 3 pages, Fourier transform of finite field. Fourier transform of primitive Dirichlet character mod q is proportional to complex conjugate of character. Called Gauss sum, appears in Dirichlet L-functions and quadratic forms. Precise value of Gauss sum is more challenging, use Fourier analysis. Prove Gauss's theorem. Theorem 3, chi mod p is nontrivial char, then tau(chi) is root p if p is 1 mod 4, i root p is p is -1 mod 4. Restate this in Fourier transforms bar chi is chi if p is 1 mod 4, -i chi if p is -1 mod 4. Quadratic characters are eigenfunctions of the Fourier transform, eigenvalues is 1 or -i. Mix multiplicative cahracter with additive one, key idea is to disentangle the two. Quadratic gauss sum is discrete analogue of Gaussian integral. Use Poisson summation somehow.

Essentially the same as Borcherd's proof. Simplify using an appropriate definition to tau(chi)^2 = chi(-1)p, need to show this.

https://web.williams.edu/Mathematics/lg5/Feynman.pdf differentiating under integral sign in 5 pages, derivation of gamma function by differentiating under integral sign, Inna Zakharevich gave this as a proof. Consider F(t) = inegral exp(-tx) dx from 0 to infty, gets 1/t for all positive t, but differentiate under integral sign, gets -1/t^2, differentiatee it n times gets integral x^n exp(-tx) dx. = n!/t^n+1. Immediately implies gamma function formula, n! = integral x^n exp(-x) dx. So you get gamma function from integrating exp(-tx) to 1/t.

https://web.williams.edu/Mathematics/lg5/TopEuclid.pdf Furstenberg's topological prove on infinitely many primes, consider ollection of biinfinite arithemtic progressions on Z, this is a basis of topology on Z, in particualrluy for any prime pZ is both open and closed, complement of pZ is union of p-1 arithmetic progressions. Finitely many primes suggest union of pZ for all p is closed, but complement of this set is +-1, clearly not open, contradiction.

https://web.williams.edu/Mathematics/lg5/holder.pdf Holder's by triangle inequality, wlog triangle inequality holds for nonzero sums, then check that ratio si greater o requal to 1. Take alpha_n = c_n / sum c_k, enough to show if sum alpha_n = 1, sum of norms alpha_n >= 1, use representation by cosines to prove this using exponential form for alpha. One can prove Holder's inequality with this. Triangle inequality gives Young's inequality as minimum requirement. Reduce to case of ratios of A_n / sum A_k. Use concavity later.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=j6lVP_U_WQE, computation of the simplicial torus, use a triangulation, the torus has one vertices, three edges and two faces. Need to know the boundary maps, delta_0 is 0, delta_1 is going to be 0 conveniently. Bilinear pairing, show unique homomorphism, f: Z x H -> K, want to show f': H -> K such that f = f' theta, forces f'h = f' theta(1,h) = f(1,h), define f' by f'h = f(1,h), check that this is a homomorphism. Z/n otimes H isomorphic to H/nH, you can get this by reading off the tensor product in proposition 5. For Tor, this is by examining the kernel of the map, it looks like n_H, nh = 0 of elements H of order, this is the kernel; Corollary: Z/n otimes Q = 0 because you cannot make H/nH, and Tor(Z/n, Q) is also 0 since we cannot make it. We have nZ + mZ = dZ, this is almost the definition of gcd(n,m).

Bilinear form on rep, rho rep, V_rho can be given structure of C[G] module i.e. map from G to V_rho? Define symmetric bilinear form (this is like Hodge theory), (rho, rho') = dim Hom_C[G] (Vrho, Vho'), Schur's if irreducible, this is indicator function for irreducible reps. Induced representations, let s and g in G, define action of G on V = {f: GG -> V_tau | f(hg) = tauh(h) f(g) for all h in H, g in G, H < G, tau representing H}. This definition does not make much sense. (sf)(g) = f(gs), it looks like some sort of associative law on the representation? It has transitivity. Basically one enforces the transitivity law? Frobenius rciprocity is the statement that over inner hom of group algebras Hom(Ind^G_H V_tau, V_rho) is isomorphic to Hom(V_tau, Res^G_H V_rho). Embed V_tau into V = Ind^G_H, using this. I do not understand this dimension. OK now comes the concrete computation, GL_2(k) = G, k = F_q has following subgroups. U, A, B, P, D, Z. These looks quite random.
